00576224: The input is a 2×2 tile that is expanded into a 6×6 pattern. Each input row is repeated three times across to make a 2×6 strip; then three such strips are stacked vertically: the top and bottom strips use the original 2×2 tile, while the middle strip uses a left-right mirror of that tile. So the output is an original / horizontally flipped / original arrangement of the 2×2 pattern, tiled across the width.
007bbfb7: The 3×3 input is used as a self-stamping template. Build a 9×9 output as a 3×3 array of 3×3 blocks, and for every nonzero cell in the input, place a full copy of the entire input pattern into the corresponding block position; blocks corresponding to zero cells stay all zero. Thus the input pattern is replicated at the locations marked by its own colored cells.
009d5c81: The grid contains a large light-blue shape (8) and a small dark-blue marker shape (1). The marker is deleted, and the large 8-shape is recolored according to the marker’s silhouette: an upward-pointing triangle/arrow means color 7, a downward-pointing triangle/arrow means color 3, and a plus/cross means color 2. The geometry and position of the large shape stay unchanged; only its color changes.
00d62c1b: Treat the green cells as walls and the black cells as empty space. Any orthogonally connected black region that does **not** touch the outer border of the grid—i.e. a hole fully enclosed by green—is recolored yellow. All green cells and all border-connected black background remain unchanged.
00dbd492: Find each hollow red square/frame (color 2) and fill its empty interior while leaving the red border and any red cells already inside unchanged. The fill color depends on the square’s size: 5×5 frames get color 8, 7×7 frames get color 4, and 9×9 frames get color 3. All background outside the frames stays the same.
017c7c7b: The input contains a single nonzero pattern that repeats vertically with some fixed row period. Create a new grid with the same width but 1.5× the original height, continue that same vertical repetition downward until the taller grid is filled, and recolor the pattern from 1 to 2. Background 0 cells remain 0.
025d127b: Each non-background colored object is “pushed” one cell to the right, but only for the parts that are not on its bottom edge or its rightmost edge. In other words, keep the object’s lowest row and its far-right column fixed, erase the original object, and redraw all remaining cells shifted right by one. This is applied independently to every colored shape in the grid.
03560426: Find all non-background connected colored shapes and sort them from left to right as they appear in the input. Rebuild them at the top-left of an otherwise empty grid: place the first shape with its top-left corner at the grid’s top-left, then place each next shape so its own top-left corner sits exactly on the previous shape’s bottom-right corner. This creates a diagonal chain of overlapping shapes, where each earlier shape’s bottom-right cell is overwritten by the next shape, while the last shape remains complete.
045e512c: Find the largest non-background object and treat it as the central template. Every other colored object is copied repeatedly in the direction from the template toward that object (up, down, left, right, or diagonal), using a step equal to the template’s height and width, so the object tiles outward away from the center until the grid edge. Each repeated copy is recolored to the background color that would be covered there, so these outward rays inherit the color of the region they pass through, while the original objects remain in place.
0520fde7: The input contains two equal-sized binary patterns made of blue cells, separated by a gray divider line. Compare the two side-by-side patterns cell by cell and keep only the positions where both patterns have a blue cell. The output is a single pattern of that same size, with those shared positions colored red and all other cells black.
05269061: The input contains partial clues for a 3-color diagonal stripe pattern: each nonzero color appears only on one family of parallel diagonals running from upper-right to lower-left. The task is to identify which color belongs to each of the three repeating diagonal classes, then fill the entire grid with that repeating 3-step diagonal pattern. In other words, extend the sparse diagonal hints so every cell is colored according to its diagonal’s class, replacing the zeros with a full periodic tiling.
05a7bcf2:
The grid contains a full blue divider line of 8s (vertical or horizontal), with 4-shapes on one side and 2-shapes on the other. First, every 4 is recolored to 3; then, for each row/column that contains such a shape, a straight band is completed from that shape to the blue divider using 4s, continued past the divider using 8s, and the 2-cells on the far side in that same row/column are packed against the outer border.  

If the divider is horizontal, the whole operation is the vertical analogue of the same rule. In short: turn the 4-object into a 3-marked source, extend it to the 8-line, extend the 8-line through to the opposite side, and push the aligned 2-pattern outward to the edge.
05f2a901: The grid contains two objects: an irregular shape and a solid 2×2 square. The 2×2 square stays fixed, while the other shape is translated straight toward it along a single cardinal direction, preserving its form. The moving shape stops at the nearest position where it becomes edge-adjacent to the square, without overlapping it.
0607ce86: The input contains several same-sized rectangular motif regions arranged in a regular grid, with stray noise both inside them and elsewhere. The task identifies the repeated motif shape, infers the intended clean pattern by taking the most common color at each cell position across all motif copies, and then redraws every motif region with that single consensus pattern. Everything outside those repeated motif rectangles is cleared to black.
0692e18c:
The input is a small square grid with background 0 and one foreground color. The output is a larger square whose size is the input size squared, viewed as an \(n \times n\) array of \(n \times n\) blocks.

For each foreground cell in the input, place one copy of the input’s color-inverted pattern (swap foreground and background) into the corresponding block of the output; blocks corresponding to 0-cells stay all 0. So the original shape acts as a mask telling where to stamp its own inverse.
06df4c85: The picture is a regular lattice of small square cells separated by solid border lines, which stay unchanged. Treat each colored 2×2 block inside a cell as a marker: whenever two markers of the same color lie in the same cell-row or the same cell-column, fill every intervening cell along that straight horizontal or vertical path with that same color. Thus the output “connects” equal-colored blocks into continuous row/column runs, while isolated blocks remain as they are.
070dd51e: Each nonzero color appears as two seed cells. If the two cells of a color are in the same row, fill the whole horizontal segment between them; if they are in the same column, fill the whole vertical segment between them. When a horizontal and a vertical segment cross, the vertical segment’s color is kept at the intersection (vertical lines are drawn on top of horizontal ones).
08ed6ac7: The input contains several bottom-aligned vertical gray bars of different heights. Recolor each bar according to its height rank while keeping its position and shape unchanged: tallest becomes color 1 (blue), second tallest color 2 (red), third tallest color 3 (green), and shortest color 4 (yellow). All background cells remain 0.
09629e4f:
The gray 5-lines form a 3×3 array of 3×3 subgrids. Find the unique subgrid that has five black cells (0) instead of four; this subgrid is the template.  
Then “zoom” that template up: for each cell position within the template, fill the corresponding whole 3×3 subgrid of the output with that cell’s color, while keeping the gray separator lines unchanged.
0962bcdd: The input contains one or more small 3×3 plus-signs: a center cell of one color with its four orthogonal neighbors in a second color. In the output, each plus is expanded into a larger symmetric star centered at the same place: the arm color is extended one more cell outward in the four cardinal directions, while the center color is added along all four diagonals for two cells. The original cross remains, so each object becomes a 5×5 combined “plus + X” pattern.
09c534e7: Treat each non-background connected shape as a solid blob whose main color is 1, possibly containing a few seed cells of other colors. Recolor only the blob’s interior cells—those completely surrounded on all 8 sides by non-background cells—using the blob’s seed color, while leaving the outer 1-cell-thick boundary unchanged. Blobs with no such fully enclosed interior cells stay the same, and each blob uses its own contained non-1 color.
0a1d4ef5: Find the large connected components that are perfect solid rectangles (their bounding box is completely filled by one color) and ignore all scattered noise pixels. These rectangles are arranged in horizontal bands; compress each rectangle to a single cell of the same color, preserving their top-to-bottom band order and left-to-right order within each band. The output is the small grid of those rectangle colors.
0a2355a6: The input contains several separate sky-blue (`8`) shapes on a black background. Each shape is recolored according to how many enclosed black holes/cavities it contains: 1 hole → `1`, 2 holes → `3`, 3 holes → `2`, and 4 holes → `4`. The geometry and position of every shape stay the same; only the color changes.
0a938d79:
The input contains exactly two nonzero seed pixels on the border. Each seed is expanded into a full stripe perpendicular to that border: left/right-edge seeds become full horizontal rows, while top/bottom-edge seeds become full vertical columns.  

Then those two colored stripes are repeated in the same order across the grid, with a period equal to twice the distance between the original seed positions along the repetition axis, producing alternating colored bars separated by the same-sized gaps as in the original pair.
0b148d64: The input contains several large monochrome regions: one for each color value, including the black background/separator color. The task is to find the color-region whose bounding rectangle is smallest, then crop out that entire rectangle from the original grid. In these examples, this corresponds to extracting the small quadrant/block containing the “odd” non-black color, preserving its internal 0s exactly.
0b17323b: The input contains several color-1 cells lying on a straight, evenly spaced diagonal. Keep those cells unchanged, then continue the same diagonal beyond the last 1 using the same step size, placing color-2 cells at each next position. Stop when the next step would go outside the grid.
0bb8deee: The input contains a full horizontal line and a full vertical line of the same color, forming a cross that divides the grid into four quadrants. Ignore this cross, and in each quadrant take the colored shape there, cropped to its tight bounding box. Assemble these four cropped quadrant-shapes into a new 2×2 arrangement: top-left, top-right, bottom-left, and bottom-right, producing a compact 6×6 output.
0becf7df: The 2×2 block in the upper-left corner is a color key that stays unchanged. In each row of this key, the two colors form a swap pair: top-row colors swap with each other everywhere else in the grid, and bottom-row colors swap with each other everywhere else in the grid. The shapes and positions of all nonzero regions remain the same; only their colors are exchanged according to those two horizontal pairs.
0c786b71: The output is a 2×2 enlargement of the input grid, with each quadrant containing a mirrored version of the original. The bottom-right quadrant is the input unchanged, the bottom-left is flipped left-right, the top-right is flipped top-bottom, and the top-left is flipped both ways (equivalently rotated 180°). This creates a larger pattern symmetric across both the horizontal and vertical center lines.
0c9aba6e: The grid is split into two equal parts by the horizontal row of 7s: a top 6×4 pattern made of 2s and 0s, and a bottom 6×4 pattern made of 6s and 0s. The output is a 6×4 grid marking only the cells where both halves are empty at the same position: if the top cell and the corresponding bottom cell are both 0, the output cell is 8; otherwise it is 0. In other words, it shows the overlap of the background spaces of the two patterns.
0ca9ddb6: Keep the input grid unchanged, but decorate two specific colors with different 1-cell halos. Around every blue `1`, place orange `7`s in the four orthogonal neighbors (up, down, left, right); around every red `2`, place yellow `4`s in the four diagonal neighbors (the four corners). All other colors are left alone, and any halo cells that would fall outside the grid are simply omitted.
0d3d703e: The output keeps the grid exactly the same shape and pattern, but recolors every cell using fixed opposite-color pairs. The swaps are: 1↔5, 2↔6, 3↔4, and 8↔9; any occurrence of one color is replaced by its partner. In other words, it is a simple color-inversion-by-pairs with no spatial changes.
0d87d2a6: Blue cells act as endpoints: whenever two 1s lie on the same row or the same column, draw a solid blue line connecting them. Then examine each red (2) connected shape; if the line passes through it or even touches it edge-adjacently, recolor that entire red shape to blue. Everything else stays unchanged.
0e206a2e: Find the larger connected multicolor shape(s) that include a filler color plus a few distinctive colored “anchor” cells, and erase them from their original location. Elsewhere in the grid are small target constellations made only of those anchor colors; for each such target, place a copy of the large shape transformed by some rotation and/or reflection so that its anchor-colored cells land exactly on the matching target cells. The rest of the shape’s filler cells are then drawn around those matched anchors.
0e671a1a: The input contains exactly three single-cell markers: 3, 4, and 2. The output keeps those markers unchanged and adds gray (5) cells to connect them with one orthogonal polyline in the order **3 → 4 → 2**: from the 3, go vertically to the row of the 4, then horizontally to the 4; from the 4, go vertically to the row of the 2, then horizontally to the 2. Only the intermediate path cells are colored 5, so the path reaches 3 vertically and reaches 2 horizontally.
0f63c0b9: The input contains a few isolated colored cells; only their **row positions** matter. Sort these dots from top to bottom and partition the grid into horizontal bands at the midpoints between neighboring dot rows, with any odd extra row assigned to the upper band. In each band, use that dot’s color to paint the **left and right border cells** of every row in the band, and paint the dot’s own row as a **full-width horizontal line**; additionally, the top row is fully filled with the topmost color and the bottom row is fully filled with the bottommost color.
103eff5b: The grid contains a small multicolored pattern and a larger shape made entirely of 8s. The large 8-shape is the same pattern as the small one, but rotated 90° clockwise and enlarged so each small cell becomes a solid block. The output leaves the small pattern unchanged and recolors the large 8-shape by copying the small pattern’s colors after that 90° clockwise rotation, replacing the 8s with the corresponding colors.
10fcaaa3:
The output is a 2×2 tiling of the input grid, so the original picture is repeated twice horizontally and twice vertically. Then every background cell that lies diagonally adjacent to any non-background cell in this tiled image is colored 8, while the original colored cells remain unchanged.  

Visually, this adds an 8-colored diagonal “halo” or checker-like surround around the repeated nonzero pixels/shapes.
11852cab: Each input contains an almost-complete symmetric motif inside a 5×5 neighborhood: one color forms the central part, another forms the side midpoints, and a third color is supposed to occupy the four outer corner positions of that motif. The transformation finds the color that currently appears only once or twice and completes its missing symmetric copies so that those cells form a full four-way symmetric set around the motif’s center. All existing cells stay unchanged; only the missing instances of that sparse color are added.
1190bc91: The input contains one main nonzero line of colors (either horizontal or vertical) plus one or more 2-cell same-color bars elsewhere. The output fills the whole grid with diagonal bands: the colors of the main line are copied repeatedly along both diagonal directions, so each anti-diagonal/diagonal carries that ordered sequence outward from the original line. The 2-cell bars act as corner/background fillers for the wedge-shaped regions beyond the ends of the main line, extending their color diagonally into those outer areas, while cells not reached remain 0.
1190e5a7: The input is a large field of one dominant color, crossed by full-width horizontal lines and full-height vertical lines of the other color. These separator lines partition the background into rectangular sections; the output is a smaller solid rectangle whose height equals the number of row-sections and whose width equals the number of column-sections. Every cell in the output is filled with the dominant background color.
11dc524f: The grid contains two non-background objects: a shape made of 2s and a shape made of 5s. Keep the 2-shape fixed, slide it straight toward the 5-shape until the two objects are just adjacent, then place a mirrored copy of the 2-shape on the far side of that contact and recolor this mirrored copy to 5. The result is the original 2-object plus a 5-object that is the reflected continuation of it across the touching boundary, on a 7 background.
11e1fe23: The input contains three isolated colored cells that occupy three corners of an axis-aligned rectangle/square. The output keeps those original cells and adds a small 3×3 “summary” of their arrangement at the rectangle’s center: put a gray cell (5) at the midpoint of the diagonal corner pair, then place one copy of each original color in the diagonally adjacent cell around that gray center, in the same directional quadrant (NW/NE/SW/SE) as its source cell relative to the center.
12422b43: The gray 5s in the leftmost column act as markers: the number of such cells tells you how many top rows form a repeatable template. Copy those marked rows, but erase the gray marker cell itself, and then paste this row-pattern repeatedly downward starting just below the original nonzero content. The original drawing stays unchanged; only the empty lower part is filled by cycling through that marker-defined template.
12997ef3: The blue cells (color 1) define a template shape, taken within its tight bounding box. Every other non-background colored cell is treated as a color marker: make one copy of the blue template for each marker, recoloring the template cells with that marker’s color while keeping the template’s internal zeros. Arrange these colored copies compactly in the same order as the markers—top-to-bottom if the markers are stacked vertically, or left-to-right if they are arranged horizontally.
12eac192: Treat each non-background colored region as a same-color connected component using side-touching adjacency. Any such component of size 1 or 2 is recolored entirely to green (3), while larger components stay in their original color. The background (black/0) is unchanged.
13713586: A full border line of gray 5s marks one side of the grid and acts as an anchor. Each other colored object is a straight segment perpendicular to that border; it is expanded into a solid rectangle by filling all cells between the segment and the gray border, stopping just before the gray line. If these rectangles overlap, the one whose original segment was closer to the gray border is the visible one in the overlap.
137eaa0f: Each non-background connected cluster contains exactly one gray 5-cell acting as an anchor, plus one or more nearby colored cells. The output is formed by translating all of these clusters so that their gray anchor cells coincide, then superimposing the colored cells at the same relative offsets around that shared center. Finally, the overlaid result is cropped to its tight bounding box, yielding a 3×3 pattern with the 5 in the middle.
137f0df0: Find the bounding rectangle of all gray (5) cells. Recolor every black cell inside that rectangle to red (2), leaving the gray cells unchanged. Then extend from those new red regions outward along the same rows and columns beyond the rectangle: any remaining black cell that has a clear horizontal or vertical line to the rectangle interior, without a gray cell blocking it, becomes blue (1).
13f06aa5: Treat each connected non-background shape as a “pointer” whose rarest-colored cell is its tip. If that tip lies on the top, bottom, left, or right edge of the shape’s bounding box, extend a straight line of that tip’s color outward from the tip in that direction until it reaches the corresponding outer border of the whole grid, and also paint that entire border line the same color. When two such painted border lines overlap, their intersection cell is set to 0.
140c817e: Each input cell of color 1 becomes the center of a crosshair. In the output, its entire row and entire column are painted color 1, the original 1 itself is changed to color 2, and its four diagonal neighbors are marked with color 3 when they lie inside the grid. All other cells stay as the original background color, with the center cells overriding the row/column lines.
14754a24: The yellow 4s act as markers for special local neighborhoods. For any cell whose 4-neighborhood contains only 4s and 5s, if that neighborhood contains at least two 4s, and those 4s do not themselves have any diagonally adjacent extra 4s outside that same local cross-shaped neighborhood, then all 5s in that neighborhood are recolored to 2. In effect, the task highlights certain tight plus-shaped clusters centered around groups of adjacent 4s by turning the neighboring 5s in those clusters red.
1478ab18: The grid contains several marker cells of color 5. Find the pair of 5s that lie on the same row, column, or diagonal and are farthest apart, treat them as two corners, then use their row/column-swapped corner as the third corner of a right triangle. Draw the three sides of that triangle in color 8, leaving the original 5 cells unchanged.
14b8e18c: Find each connected non-background object whose bounding box is a square larger than 1×1. Around every such object, place color-2 marks just outside the object at the four diagonal positions from its bounding-box corners (equivalently, the cells adjacent to each corner along the outside border), while leaving all original colored cells unchanged. Objects that are not square-bounded are ignored.
150deff5: The gray shape is a tiling made from two kinds of pieces: solid 2×2 squares and straight 3-cell bars (horizontal or vertical). Recolor each gray cell according to which piece it belongs to: 2×2 square cells become light blue (8), while 3-cell bar cells become red (2). The background stays unchanged.
15113be4:
The grid is partitioned by color-4 separator lines into many 3×3 tiles. One tile contains a distinctive “template” color (the only color besides 0, 1, and 4), and the positions of that color within the tile define a small shape.

Copy that same shape into every other 3×3 tile where all corresponding cells are currently color 1: recolor those matching 1-cells to the template color, while leaving all other cells unchanged. The original template occurrence stays as it is.
15660dd6:
The input consists of three horizontally stacked panels, each split into the same set of vertical compartments by 8-colored separators. In each compartment, two of the three panels show a shape made of color 2, while the remaining panel instead contains a different solid-colored figure; the output selects that “odd one out” figure for each compartment.

Each selected compartment is redrawn in a single row of panels: its border/background color 1 is replaced by the left-edge label color of the panel it came from (5, 9, or 0), and its special figure color replaces the 2s in the copied template. The chosen compartments are then concatenated horizontally, separated by one column of 8s.
15663ba9: The input contains one or more orthogonally connected shapes drawn in a single nonzero color on a black background. Recolor only those shape cells that are 90° corner cells: if the corner is an outer/convex corner touching the exterior background, change it to **4**; if it is an inner/concave corner whose two open sides face a fully enclosed hole, change it to **2**. All other cells remain unchanged.
15696249: Find the row or column in the 3×3 input whose three cells are all the same color. Use that line as a cue: if it is a row, copy the whole 3×3 pattern three times side-by-side to make a 3×9 strip; if it is a column, copy the whole pattern three times top-to-bottom to make a 9×3 strip. Place this strip into a blank 9×9 canvas at the matching position of that uniform line (top/middle/bottom for rows, left/center/right for columns), leaving all other cells black.
17829a00: The grid has a solid top row in one color and a solid bottom row in another, with scattered cells of those two colors inside a field of 7s. The interior cells of the top-row color are moved upward until each connected shape touches the top border, while the interior cells of the bottom-row color are moved downward until each connected shape touches the bottom border. For the bottom-color shapes, any vertical gaps within the same columns are filled so they become solid downward-hanging forms; all other interior cells become 7.
178fcbfb: Each isolated colored cell is expanded into a full line across the grid, with the direction determined by its color. Red cells (2) become full vertical columns, while blue (1) and green (3) cells become full horizontal rows of their own color. If a horizontal row crosses a red column, the horizontal color remains, so blue/green override red at intersections.
17b80ad2: Look only at columns whose bottom cell is color 5. In each such column, extend colors upward to fill every empty cell: start from the bottom with color 5, and as you scan upward, whenever you hit an existing nonzero cell, that cell’s color becomes the new color to keep propagating upward. All other columns stay unchanged, so the marked columns become solid vertical bands made of stacked color segments inherited from the original colored cells in that column.
17b866bd: The grid is a tiling of identical 4×4 motifs spaced every 5 cells; each motif has a fixed hollow shape made of 8s and 0s. Any nonzero cell that appears at a lattice intersection (row/column multiples of 5) acts as a marker for the motif immediately down-right of that intersection: copy that motif’s hollow zero pattern and fill those zero cells with the marker’s color. After placing all such colored fillings, the marker cells themselves are erased to 0, while the rest of the background pattern stays unchanged.
17cae0c1: The 3×9 input is split into three adjacent 3×3 blocks, each containing a simple gray-on-black symbol. Each symbol is recognized and replaced by a solid-colored 3×3 block: top horizontal line → 6, bottom horizontal line → 1, diagonal of three cells → 9, single center cell → 4, and full border/ring of eight gray cells → 3. The output keeps the three blocks in the same left-to-right order, but each becomes uniformly filled with its corresponding color.
18286ef8: The grid is partitioned by full zero rows and columns into rectangular regions; one region contains a 3×3 block of 5s with a single 9 inside it, and elsewhere there is a single 6. The 6 is turned into a 9, and the original 9 inside the 5-block is erased back to 5. If the 6 and the 9 lie in different regions separated by one of the zero divider lines, the 9 is also shifted one cell from its old position toward the 6 across that divider; if they are in the same region, no shift occurs.
182e5d0f: The grid contains several connected shapes made of color 3 on a 7-background, plus some 0s and 5s. Any 3-shape that touches a 5 and also has a “special endpoint” next to at least two 0 cells is reduced to just that endpoint: the endpoint stays 3, and the adjacent 3 cell along the shape is turned into 5, while the rest of that shape disappears into 7. All other 3-shapes are left unchanged, and any original 5 that is not attached to one of these reducible 3-shapes is kept.
18419cfa: Each connected blue (8) frame acts like a mirror box for the red (2) pattern inside it. For every such frame, the existing red cells are reflected across both the vertical and horizontal center lines of that frame, and all reflected copies are added, so the red pattern becomes fully symmetric in all four quadrants. The blue frame and the rest of the grid stay unchanged.
18447a8d:
The grid is split into horizontal panels by full-width separator rows of 7s. In each panel, the left side contains a row-by-row shape made of 8s, and the right side contains a row-by-row complementary shape in some other single color; the output moves that colored shape into the left half so that, on each row, it fills the space up to the middle immediately after the existing 8s, while the entire right half becomes 7s.

The color assigned to each panel is not necessarily kept: each panel receives the color from the panel whose right-side shape matches the complement of its 8-pattern (i.e. the panel with the same row lengths as the spaces needed to complete its left half to the midpoint).
184a9768: Treat the large colored hollow rectangles as containers and ignore the scattered gray 5s, which simply disappear. Every other colored shape that lies outside those containers is picked up and translated, without rotation, into a matching empty hole inside one of the containers, preserving its color and shape. The output keeps only the original container shapes with their holes filled by those moved pieces; everything else outside becomes black.
195ba7dc: The middle column of 2s is just a divider: split the input into the 6-column block on the left and the 6-column block on the right. Compare the two blocks cell by cell and produce a single 6-column output. A cell is black (0) only if both corresponding input cells are black; otherwise it is blue (1), so the output is the overlap/intersection of the two halves’ black cells.
1990f7a8: The input contains four separate red shapes, each occupying a small 3×3 bounding box and lying in one of the four quadrants of the large grid. The output compresses these four shapes into a canonical 7×7 layout: a 3×3 slot in each corner, with the middle row and middle column left all black to form a separating cross. Each shape is copied unchanged into the corner corresponding to its original position (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right).
19bb5feb: Find the rectangular light-blue (8) area and treat it as a board split into four quadrants: top-left, top-right, bottom-left, and bottom-right. For each quadrant, output the color of the small non-8 block located there; if a quadrant contains only blue background, output 0 instead. The result is a 2×2 grid preserving those four quadrant positions.
1a07d186: Full-width/full-height monochrome rows or columns act as fixed “bars.” Any other isolated colored cell whose color matches one of these bars is moved straight toward the matching bar until it sits in the cell immediately adjacent to it, staying in the same column for horizontal bars or the same row for vertical bars, and remaining on its original side (above/below or left/right). Isolated cells whose color has no corresponding bar are erased, while the bars themselves stay unchanged.
1a244afd: The grid contains isolated `1`s and `6`s on an `8` background. Each `6` is paired with the nearest `1` that lies in the same row or column; the `6`s are then removed, and for each such pair a `7` is placed at the point obtained by turning the `1→6` offset 90° counterclockwise around the `1` (same distance preserved). The `1`s stay unchanged, and if the rotated position would fall outside the grid, no `7` is drawn.
1a2e2828: Find the one straight line in the picture that is completely uniform in color across its full extent: either a whole row of one color or a whole column of one color. All other candidate lines are interrupted at crossings, but this one remains unchanged all the way through. Output a 1×1 grid containing that line’s color.
1a6449f1: Find the largest nonzero single-color rectangular frame (a 1-cell-thick border) in the input. The output is the subgrid strictly inside that frame: remove the border and copy the enclosed interior pattern unchanged, preserving its size and colors.
1acc24af: The blue shape at the top defines one or more downward “slots”: for each row spanned by the blue figure, take the empty cells between that row’s leftmost and rightmost blue cells, and group these interior gaps into connected cavities. Then examine each gray connected component below; if the component can be translated and rotated so that it completely covers one of those blue-defined cavities, without overlapping any blue cells and with the rest of the piece staying at or below the blue figure, that gray component is recolored red (2). Gray pieces that cannot fit any such cavity remain gray.
1b2d62fb: The middle column of 1s is a divider; remove it and compare the left and right 3-column halves cell-by-cell in the same positions. Build a 5×3 output grid where a cell becomes 8 only if both corresponding cells on the two sides are black (0); otherwise it is 0. So the output highlights the overlap of the black cells shared by the two halves.
1b59e163: The grid contains several multi-cell colored motifs made mostly of color 1, each with one distinctive non-1 “anchor” color inside or attached to it, plus separate single-cell markers of those same anchor colors. The output removes all isolated markers (including the color-5 cells) and redraws each motif at every location indicated by a matching single-cell anchor color, aligning the motif so its anchor-colored cell lands exactly on that marker. The result is a clean background with copies of each pattern stamped onto all matching anchor positions.
1b60fb0c: The blue shape has a central vertical “spine” (sometimes one column wide, sometimes two adjacent columns wide). The task reflects the blue cells that lie to the right of this spine across that vertical axis, and draws the reflected copy on the left in red. Existing blue cells are kept unchanged, so red is only added where the mirrored position was empty.
1b8318e3: The color-5 2×2 squares are fixed anchors and remain unchanged. Every other nonzero cell is moved from its original location to the empty cell immediately next to the nearest such 2×2 square, so that it becomes attached to that square on the side facing its original position. If a piece is already in a suitable adjacent spot, it stays there; the rest of the grid is cleared to background.
1be83260: The input is a grid of identical motif tiles arranged in a larger rectangular array, with one special tile containing a few non-base-colored “marker” cells. The output rebuilds the whole tile array without the black background, using the unmarked tile as a template and recoloring it separately for each marker position found in the special tile: each output tile copy is filled entirely with the color of the marker at the corresponding location, while positions with no marker stay in the original base color. The recolored copies are arranged in the same tile layout as the input, with separator rows/columns between tiles kept in the base color.
1bfc4729: The input contains two isolated colored cells: one in the top half and one in the bottom half. In the output, each cell’s color is expanded into a rectangular frame for its half of the grid: the upper color paints the full top row, the full row containing the upper cell, and the left/right border columns down to the middle; the lower color paints the full bottom row, the full row containing the lower cell, and the left/right border columns up from the middle. All other cells remain background.
1c02dbbe: The input contains one large rectangle of color 5 and, for each other color, a few isolated “marker” pixels placed on or just outside that rectangle. Each such color defines an axis-aligned rectangular subregion inside the 5-rectangle: take the bounding box of that color’s markers and intersect it with the big 5-rectangle, then fill that overlap completely with the marker’s color. Everything else stays the same, so any marker pixels that were outside the big rectangle disappear to background while markers on its border become part of the filled subrectangle.
1c0d0a4b: The all-black rows and columns act as separators, partitioning the grid into isolated rectangular “character” blocks (in these examples, 3×3 blocks). Inside each such block, the cyan pattern is erased: every 8 becomes 0, and every black cell of that same block is turned into red (2). The separator rows/columns and any cells outside the blocks remain unchanged.
1c56ad9f: The input contains a single nonzero-colored figure made of repeated horizontal bands and/or vertical strokes. Keep the figure the same color and shape overall, but apply a horizontal “wobble” to every other occupied row of the figure: rows selected in an alternating pattern are shifted by one cell left or right, with the shift direction itself alternating left/right as you move downward. All other rows stay fixed, producing a zigzag/wave distortion inside the original figure.
1c786137: Find the single-color object that forms a hollow rectangular frame (a one-cell-thick box) somewhere in the noisy grid. The output is the exact contents inside that frame: crop the rectangle enclosed by the border and remove the border itself, preserving all interior colors and layout.
1caeab9d: The grid contains several separate colored objects, with the blue object (color 1) acting as the reference. Every non-background object is shifted only vertically so that its bottom edge lands on the same row as the blue object’s bottom edge; equivalently, all objects become horizontally aligned at the blue object’s vertical level. Their shapes, colors, and column positions stay unchanged, and their original locations are cleared to background.
1cf80156: The input contains one colored figure on a background, plus full-width/full-height uniform background rows and columns around it. The output is the tight rectangular crop containing the non-background figure: remove all-empty border rows and columns and keep the figure exactly as it appears inside that bounding box. The figure’s color and internal 0 gaps are preserved.
1d0a4b61: The input is a regularly repeating wallpaper/quilt pattern that has been partially erased by black 0-cells. Recover the underlying 2D periodic tile from the intact nonzero cells, then fill every 0 with the color that belongs at that same position in the repeating pattern. The output is the fully restored seamless pattern, including any missing border/grid lines made of color 1.
1d398264: Find the lone 3×3 nonzero block and keep it in place. Treat its center cell as the origin, and for each of the eight surrounding cells, extend that cell’s color outward in the same compass direction from the center (up, down, left, right, or one of the four diagonals), continuing to the grid edge. The result is the original 3×3 pattern with eight straight colored rays emanating from it.
1d61978c: The gray cells form one or two 45° diagonal chains on a dark background. Split the gray pattern by diagonal orientation: cells belonging to the “\” diagonal chain are recolored one way and cells belonging to the “/” diagonal chain the other way. Specifically, one diagonal family becomes red (2) and the other becomes azure (8), and all remaining gray cells are removed by turning them into the background color 7.
1da012fc: A solid color-5 rectangle acts as a legend box: inside it are a few single colored marker cells. Outside that box are several disconnected shapes, all drawn in one common placeholder color. Recolor those outside shapes, preserving their geometry and position, by matching them in top-to-bottom/left-to-right order with the marker cells in the legend box in the same order; each shape takes the color of its corresponding marker, while the legend box itself stays unchanged.
1e0a9b12: The grid applies vertical gravity independently in each column. All non-background cells (anything not 0) fall straight downward as far as possible, stacking at the bottom of their own column with no gaps, while keeping their original top-to-bottom order within that column. The grid size and colors stay the same; only vertical positions change.
1e32b0e9: The grid is split into a 3×3 array of subgrids by two full horizontal and two full vertical separator lines of one color. Take the non-background pattern inside the upper-left subgrid as a template, and stamp that same pattern at the same relative position in every subgrid. Any template cell that is missing in a target subgrid is filled with the separator-line color, while any already present nonzero cell there is preserved.
1e5d6875: The grid contains L-shaped triominoes of color 5 and/or color 2 on a background of 7. For each 5-shaped L, add a matching L made of color 4 immediately up-left of it; for each 2-shaped L, add a matching L made of color 3 immediately down-right of it. Only paint these shifted copies where they fall on background cells, so existing 2s and 5s stay unchanged and overlaps favor the 3-copy over the 4-copy.
1e81d6f9: The gray 5s form a corner-shaped “key” in the upper-left, enclosing one special colored cell near its inside corner. That enclosed cell’s color is the target color. Copy the grid unchanged except erase every other occurrence of that target color elsewhere in the grid, leaving only the keyed one.
1efba499: Identify the dominant non-background color as the central shape, which is either mainly horizontal or mainly vertical, and keep only that shape in place. All other colored cells are removed from their original positions and reassigned relative to this shape along each row/column it occupies: cells found on the left/right (or above/below, for a vertical shape) are moved to the cells immediately adjacent to the shape on the opposite side, preserving their row/column and color. Rows/columns that do not intersect the main shape keep any non-shape colored cells unchanged.
1f0c79e5: Each input contains one small non-background object made of color 2 plus one other color. Ignore the 2-cells, keep the other color, and use the shape formed by the non-2 cells as a diagonal “stamp”: repeat that shape along the down-right diagonal direction across the whole grid until it runs off the edges. The output is just this repeated diagonal trail in the surviving color, with the original 2s removed.
1f642eb9: Find the solid rectangular block of color 8 in the grid. Every other nonzero single cell outside that block is “projected” straight toward it along its shared row or column, and its color is copied onto the first cell of the 8-rectangle that it meets (overwriting that border 8). The original outside markers remain where they are; only the touched edge cells of the 8-block are recolored.
1f85a75f: The input contains many isolated noise pixels, plus one non-background color that forms the only multi-cell connected shape. Identify that color, choosing the one whose total number of cells is greatest among colors that appear in exactly one connected component. Then crop out the smallest rectangle containing that shape and output that rectangle, preserving the shape and any 0-background holes inside it.
1f876c06: Each non-background color appears in exactly two cells, which act as endpoints. For every such color, fill in the straight 45° diagonal segment between its two matching cells using that same color, so the two endpoints become connected by a one-cell-wide diagonal line. All original cells remain, and everything else stays unchanged.
1fad071e: Find all blue (color 1) objects that are exactly 2×2 squares, ignoring all other shapes and colors. Count these blue 2×2 blocks, then output a 1×5 horizontal bar whose leftmost cells are blue for that count and whose remaining cells are background/black (0).
2013d3e2: Find the smallest rectangle that contains all nonzero cells, then take its upper-left quarter. In these examples that bounding box is 6×6, so the output is the 3×3 block from the top-left corner of that box, copied exactly including any zeros.
2037f2c7:
The input contains two separate multicolored objects on a black background. Ignore their colors, align the two objects by their own top-left corners, and compare only which cells are occupied.  

The output is the tightly cropped mask of the cells that belong to exactly one of the two aligned shapes (their symmetric difference), drawn in color 8 on a 0 background.
2072aba6:
The input grid is enlarged by a factor of 2 in both height and width, so each original cell becomes a 2×2 block. Black cells (0) stay black as solid 2×2 blocks, while each gray cell (5) is replaced by the same 2×2 checkerboard tile:
top-left and bottom-right are 1, and top-right and bottom-left are 2.
20818e16:
Find the background color (the most common color) and ignore it. For each remaining color, treat all its cells as a solid rectangle given by that color’s bounding box, then sort these colored rectangles by size from smallest to largest.

Create a new grid whose size matches the largest such rectangle, fill it with that largest rectangle’s color, and place every smaller rectangle anchored at the top-left corner on top of it. The result is a nested top-left stack: the smallest block occupies the upper-left area, the next larger surrounds it, and so on, with the largest color filling the rest.
20981f0e: The color-2 cells stay fixed and act as a lattice of marker rows and columns that partition the grid into rectangular compartments. In each compartment, the color-1 connected shape is translated without rotation or reflection so that it becomes centered between the surrounding marker rows and marker columns. Equivalently, each small 1-shape is moved so its bounding box sits in the middle of its red-defined rectangle, while everything else is cleared to background.
20fb2937:
The top band supplies three “paint” colors: the colors at columns 0, 4, and 8. Below the solid separator row, the scattered marker colors in columns 1, 5, and 9 indicate where 3×3 stamps of those three top colors should be placed in the lower part of the grid, centered on each marker (so each marker expands to a 3×3 block).  

The output is just this lower section (rows below the horizontal divider), with a 7-background and all such expanded 3×3 color blocks clipped to the grid boundaries.
212895b5: Find the central 3×3 block of color 8 and treat the scattered 5s as obstacles. From the midpoint of each side of the 8-block, draw a color-4 arm that goes outward from the block and then turns clockwise, forming a pinwheel-like set of L-shaped rays; each arm continues only through empty cells and stops at a 5 or the grid edge. From each corner of the 8-block, also draw a color-2 diagonal ray outward, again stopping at obstacles or the border; these red diagonals are added after the yellow arms, so they overwrite them where they cross.
21f83797: The input contains exactly two red cells, which act as opposite corners of an axis-aligned rectangle. In the output, each of their rows is extended into a full horizontal red line and each of their columns into a full vertical red line, spanning the whole grid. The rectangle strictly between those two rows and two columns is then filled with blue, while the red lines remain as the border/cross structure.
2204b7a8: The input has two solid border bars of different colors on opposite sides of the grid, either top/bottom rows or left/right columns, plus some scattered green cells inside. Recolor each green cell to match the nearer border bar: for horizontal bars, greens in the upper half take the top bar’s color and greens in the lower half take the bottom bar’s color; for vertical bars, greens on the left take the left bar’s color and greens on the right take the right bar’s color. Everything else stays unchanged.
22168020: For each non-background color independently, look at all of its cells and connect every pair of same-colored cells that lie on a straight ARC line (horizontal, vertical, or diagonal). In these examples, the colored pixels form the two slanted sides and base of wedge/triangle-like outlines, so the transformation fills in the missing interior by drawing the horizontal segments between the matching boundary pixels. The original colored cells stay the same, and different colors are completed separately.
22208ba4: The grid contains a background plus several non-background corner objects. Any color that appears in more than one corner is selected; all objects of those repeated colors are moved diagonally inward from their corner by exactly their own height and width, while colors that appear only once stay where they are. The output is otherwise just the background, with the unmoved unique-color corner objects and the shifted repeated-color corner objects painted in their new positions.
22233c11: Find each green connected object and treat its bounding box as a rectangle. For every such rectangle, place a same-sized copy of the rectangle in color 8 at the two diagonally opposite positions just outside it: one shifted up-left by its own height and width, and one shifted down-right by its own height and width. Any part of these new 8-shapes that would fall outside the grid is simply clipped, while the original green cells stay unchanged.
22425bda: The input contains several full-length colored lines on a uniform background: horizontal lines, vertical lines, and sometimes one diagonal. Each non-background color is treated as one such line, and the output is a single row listing those colors ordered by line length from longest to shortest. Since all lines span the grid, this effectively means: diagonal first (if present), then horizontal/vertical lines ordered by the grid dimension they span, with ties broken by larger color number first.
22806e14:
The grid contains one “template” object that is not a solid filled rectangle, plus several solid rectangular blocks of another color. The output uses the template object’s color to mark the centers of all solid rectangles whose height and width are both odd; if there are not enough such odd-by-odd rectangles to account for every cell of the template object, the template object is first erased from its original location.

In other words, the cells of the irregular/sparse object are converted into center dots placed inside qualifying filled rectangles, one dot per rectangle, using the template’s color.
2281f1f4: The gray cells act as row and column markers: take every row that contains a gray cell and every column that contains a gray cell, and mark all their intersections. Any such intersection that was empty becomes red (2), while original gray (5) cells stay gray. In these examples, this appears as copying the top row’s gray-column pattern into each marked row from the right edge.
228f6490: Find the gray (5) frame-like regions and the black holes fully enclosed inside them. Then find the external colored objects whose shapes exactly match those hole shapes, and “move” each matching object into the corresponding hole: the hole is filled with that object’s color, and the object’s original cells are turned to background black. Unmatched small/noise objects stay unchanged.
22a4bbc2: Treat each nonzero monochrome connected shape (color 1 or 8) as a separate object, and order these objects from top to bottom by their highest cell. Recolor every third object in that vertical sequence—starting with the topmost one—to color 2, while leaving the other objects unchanged. The background 0 stays the same.
22eb0ac0: The grid contains colored cells only on the border, typically as pairs on opposite ends of a row or column. Whenever two border cells of the same nonzero color lie directly opposite each other on the same straight line, they are connected by filling the entire segment between them with that color. In these examples, this means rows whose leftmost and rightmost cells match are turned into solid horizontal lines of that color; all other cells stay unchanged.
230f2e48: Each non-background connected figure contains one gray 5, one black 0, and some red 2s forming a straight “arm” from the 5 toward the 0. The black 0 acts like a turning point: remove all red cells that lie past the 0 in the same direction from the 5, then redraw the same number of red cells starting from the 0 in a perpendicular direction. The perpendicular branch is chosen to point outward toward the nearest grid edge, while the 5 and 0 stay fixed.
234bbc79: Each non-background colored object has one or more gray cells attached to it; those gray cells are converted into that object’s own color, effectively extending the object at its marked “connector” side. Then the separate objects, taken in left-to-right order, are slid only vertically so that each object’s connector touches the previous object, forming one continuous horizontal chain without changing any object’s shape. Finally, all-empty columns are removed, so the result is a compact 3-row image with no gray cells and no fully black columns.
23581191: The input contains two isolated non-background pixels, one of each color. Each pixel is expanded into a full cross: fill its entire row and its entire column with that pixel’s color, extending to the grid edges. The two cells where the row of one color meets the column of the other are marked with color 2, while all untouched cells remain background.
239be575: Each input contains two separate 2×2 blocks of color 2 and some cells of color 8. Check whether there is a single orthogonally connected cluster of 8s that touches both 2×2 blocks (i.e. an 8-path linking the two red squares). If such a connecting 8-cluster exists, the output is a 1×1 grid containing 8; otherwise the output is a 1×1 grid containing 0.
23b5c85d: The input contains several solid color regions, including the black background. Select the color region with the smallest area (fewest cells), which in these examples is a small filled rectangle embedded among larger rectangles. The output is just that region cropped to its own bounding box, so it becomes a standalone rectangle of the same size and color.
25094a63: The grid contains a noisy multicolor background with some hidden solid monochrome rectangles. Any axis-aligned rectangle that is completely filled with one color and is at least 4 cells tall and 4 cells wide is recolored to color 4 (yellow). Everything else stays unchanged.
252143c9: The output discards the original picture and replaces it with a grid of all 7s except for one straight ray. The color used for that ray is the color of the exact center cell of the input, and the ray starts at that center cell and extends in the compass/diagonal direction pointing toward the center of mass of all cells of that same color. In other words, keep only the center cell’s color and draw a full in-bounds line from the grid center toward where that color cluster lies.
253bf280: Find the sky-blue cells (8) and connect any two that lie on the same row or the same column. The cells strictly between such aligned endpoints are filled with green (3), forming straight horizontal or vertical segments, while the 8 endpoints themselves stay unchanged. Any 8 that has no partner in its row or column is left alone.
2546ccf6: The grid is partitioned into equal rectangular cells by full-width/full-height separator lines of one color. Inside these cells, each non-separator colored shape is meant to form a 2×2 symmetric block: if the same shape/color appears in three of the four corresponding cells, the missing fourth cell is filled with the 180°-rotated copy of that shape from the diagonally opposite occupied cell. All separator lines and existing shapes remain unchanged.
256b0a75: Find the axis-aligned rectangle implied by the color-8 cells, and use its bounding box as a frame: complete the whole border in 8 and fill the rectangle’s interior with the color found at the one corner of that box that is not already 8. Then, for every other nonzero cell outside the rectangle, extend its color in a straight line directly away from the rectangle (up if above it, down if below it, left if left of it, right if right of it), continuing through empty cells until the grid edge or another colored cell blocks it. All original nonzero cells are preserved unless they are part of the completed 8-frame or the interior fill.
25c199f5: The 17-column input is split into three 5×5 panels separated by vertical divider columns of color 6. From each panel, keep only the rows that are not entirely background color 7, preserving their top-to-bottom order within that panel, and concatenate these kept rows starting from the right panel, then the middle, then the left. The result is a 5×5 grid; if fewer than 5 rows are collected, pad the top with all-7 rows.
25d487eb: Each input contains one connected non-background shape made mostly of one color, with a single cell of a different color embedded on its boundary. Treat that odd-colored cell as marking the “back” of the shape, and extend a straight 1-cell-wide ray of that odd color from the opposite tip of the shape, continuing outward in the same axis-aligned direction until it reaches the grid edge. The original shape is left unchanged, and the new line is only added in empty background cells.
25d8a9c8: Process the grid row by row. Any row whose cells are all the same color is replaced by a solid gray row (all 5s), while any row containing more than one color is replaced by a solid black row (all 0s). The output keeps the same size as the input and ignores the original colors except for whether each row is uniform or mixed.
25e02866:
The input contains several separate same-sized rectangular patches sitting on a uniform background. Each patch is mostly filled with one common color, with a few “informative” cells in other colors.  

The output is a single rectangle of that shared size, formed by superimposing all of those patches in alignment: start with the common fill color, then copy in every cell from the patches whose color is different from that fill color. In effect, the shared dominant color acts like transparency, and the scattered colored marks from all rectangles are merged into one composite.
25ff71a9: The input contains a single connected non-background shape on a 0 background. The entire colored shape is translated downward by exactly one cell, preserving its color and geometry. The cells it leaves behind become 0, and any part that would move outside the grid is simply not shown.
2601afb7: The input contains several vertical colored bars on a uniform background. In the output, these bars stay vertical and keep their original column positions, but their colors are cyclically reassigned: each bar takes the color of the bar immediately to its left (with wraparound from the leftmost bar to the rightmost). Because the bars have different heights, this effectively redraws each column segment from its own top down to the bottom using the neighboring bar’s color.
264363fd: The grid contains one small isolated “template” motif and several larger solid rectangular regions, each with one or more special center pixels of the same color as the template’s center. Remove the template from its original location, then copy its cross/plus structure onto every matching center pixel inside the large rectangles. The copied motif is expanded to the bounds of that rectangle: the template’s horizontal-arm color fills the whole row through the center, the vertical-arm color fills the whole column through the center, and the original center color is preserved.
2685904e: Count the number of light-blue cells (8) in the top row; call this number \(N\). Then inspect the colored row near the bottom (the row below the gray 5-bar): any color that appears exactly \(N\) times in that row is selected. For every occurrence of each selected color, draw a vertical bar of that same color upward from just above the gray horizontal line, with height \(N\), while leaving everything else unchanged.
2697da3f: Take the minimal bounding box containing all yellow (4) cells and use that cropped pattern as a template. Build a larger square image with an empty central square, then place four oriented copies of the template around it: the original on the left, its left-right mirror on the right, a 90° clockwise rotation on the top, and a 90° counterclockwise rotation on the bottom. The result is a four-way symmetric arrangement of the original shape around a blank center.
272f95fa: The light-blue 8s form two full horizontal bars and two full vertical bars, partitioning the canvas into a 3×3 arrangement of rectangular regions. Keep these 8-bars unchanged, and fill the five regions forming a plus sign with fixed colors: top-center = 2, middle-left = 4, center = 6, middle-right = 3, and bottom-center = 1. The four corner regions remain background 0.
2753e76c: Count the number of separate same-colored objects for each nonzero color in the input. Then build a small summary grid with one row per color, ordered from the color with the most objects to the fewest; each row contains that many cells of the color, right-aligned, with 0s padding on the left. The output’s height is the number of nonzero colors, and its width is the largest object-count among them.
278e5215: The input contains two separate non-background blocks: a larger pattern made of 5s and 0s, and a smaller colored strip-like template. The output has the same size as the 5/0 pattern and is formed by using that pattern as a mask: wherever the mask has a 5, copy the color from the upper part of the template, repeating its rows cyclically down the output; wherever the mask has a 0, fill from the template’s single-color row. In effect, the 5-shape is “painted” with the repeating colored rows, while its holes/background are filled from the template’s dominant/background color row.
27a28665: Treat the 3×3 input as a binary shape: ignore the actual nonzero color and look only at which cells are filled. The filled pattern is always one of four 5-cell motifs, and each motif is classified into a fixed 1×1 output color: plus sign → 6, X-with-center → 2, the asymmetric corner/L-like pattern → 3, and the remaining bent/zigzag 5-cell pattern → 1. The output is therefore a single cell encoding the detected shape type.
27a77e38:
Copy the grid unchanged except for one cell: the center cell of the bottom row.  
Choose the non-background color that appears most often in the grid, ignoring black (0) and the gray separator color (5), and place that color in that bottom-center cell.  
So the output visually “reports” the dominant top-region color by stamping it once at the very bottom middle.
27f8ce4f: Create a larger grid whose size is the input size squared (for these examples, a 3×3 input becomes a 9×9 output), viewed as a 3×3 array of 3×3 blocks. Find the color that appears most often in the input, and use its cell positions as block locations: in each corresponding block, place a full copy of the original input. All other blocks remain filled with 0.
281123b4: The 4×19 input is split into four 4×4 panels by the vertical green separator columns. Each panel is a transparent layer containing one meaningful color only: blue (8), gray (5), red/maroon (9), or yellow (4), with 0 as empty space. The output is the single 4×4 image formed by stacking these layers in the fixed order gray → blue → yellow → red, so later colors overwrite earlier ones wherever they appear.
28bf18c6: Find the single non-background shape in the large grid and crop out its tight bounding rectangle, keeping any 0s inside that rectangle. Then make the output by placing two identical copies of this cropped pattern side by side, so the output has the same height as the crop and twice its width.
28e73c20: The input is just an empty black square, and the output draws a single-cell-wide green spiral on it. The spiral starts at the upper-left corner, runs right across the top edge, then turns clockwise and keeps winding inward with a one-cell gap between successive arms until it can no longer continue. All non-spiral cells remain black.
292dd178: Find each connected color-1 shape that is an almost-complete rectangular frame: its bounding box perimeter is all 1s except for one missing border cell. Color the frame’s interior (the cells inside its bounding box that are not part of the 1s) with color 2. Also color the missing border cell and continue a straight color-2 ray outward from that gap, perpendicular to that side of the rectangle, until the ray reaches the edge of the grid.
29623171: The gray separator lines partition the grid into equal rectangular cells (here, a 3×3 array of 3×3 regions). In each region, count how many cells contain the non-background, non-gray color. Any region(s) with the highest such count are turned into a solid block of that color, while every other non-gray region is cleared to black; the gray divider lines stay unchanged.
29700607: Each nonzero color defines a path starting from its occurrence on the top row. If that color appears again elsewhere, draw a same-colored vertical line straight down from the top-row cell to the other copy’s row, then a horizontal line along that row to the other copy, forming an L-shaped connection. If a color appears only once, just extend it straight downward to the bottom edge.
29c11459: Find each pair of non-background cells that lie on the same straight row or column with only background between them. Extend both endpoint colors inward toward the midpoint, and place color 5 at the exact middle cell(s) where they meet. In the shown examples this happens horizontally: the left color fills to the center from the left, the right color fills to the center from the right, and untouched cells remain unchanged.
2a28add5: The grid is treated row by row, with 7 as background. In any row that contains a 6, look at all non-7 cells in that row: replace that whole horizontal span—from the leftmost non-7 cell to the rightmost non-7 cell—with 8s in the output. All other rows remain entirely 7, and the original non-7 colors are discarded.
2a5f8217: The grid contains several connected shapes, and every shape that is colored **1** has an identical copy elsewhere in the grid in some other color. The output keeps all non-1 shapes unchanged, but recolors each **1**-shape to match the color of its geometrically identical twin. Thus color **1** disappears, while all shapes stay in the same positions and orientations.
2b01abd0: Find the solid line of color 1, which acts as a mirror axis; it may be vertical or horizontal. The only other nonzero shape, made from exactly two colors, is reflected across this axis onto the empty side while keeping its original colors. Meanwhile, on the side where the shape originally was, those two shape colors are swapped with each other, and the axis/background stay unchanged.
2b9ef948: The input contains a 3×3 square of 4s with a different-colored center, plus a separate marker made from that same center color, some 5s, and one 4. The output discards the 5s and turns the whole grid into the center color, then places 4s along the two full diagonals crossing a derived point: this point is the 3×3 square’s center shifted by the offset from the colored marker cell to the lone 4 in the other object. The 3×3 ring of 4s around that crossing point is also drawn, leaving its center in the background color.
2bcee788: The input contains three color regions: the large background, a larger nonzero shape, and a smaller nonzero “marker” shape. Reflect the larger nonzero shape across the side where the smaller shape sits (left/right gives a vertical mirror, above/below gives a horizontal mirror), so the reflected copy replaces the marker cells and extends the shape symmetrically. Then recolor everything else in the grid to green (3), leaving only the original-plus-mirrored main shape in its original color.
2bee17df: The grid has a two-color frame-like boundary (colors 2 and 8) enclosing an interior of mostly 0s. Inside this interior, find every row whose non-border cells are all the same background color and every column with the same property; recolor those full empty horizontal and vertical stretches to 3. In other words, the output highlights the completely unobstructed interior rows and columns by drawing green bars through them, while leaving the border colors and any blocking cells unchanged.
2c0b0aff: The input contains several separate rectangular patches made of colors 8 and 3 on a 0 background. Among these patches, select the one containing the most complete green “plus” shapes: a 3-cell whose four orthogonal neighbors are also 3. The output is that entire rectangular patch, cropped out unchanged.
2c608aff: Find the one large solid rectangle and treat it as the central target. Then, for each stray cell of the other non-background color, if it is horizontally aligned with the rectangle (its row falls within the rectangle’s height) or vertically aligned with it (its column falls within the rectangle’s width), extend that cell into a straight line of its own color until it reaches the rectangle’s edge, without overwriting the rectangle itself. Stray cells that are not aligned with the rectangle are left unchanged.
2c737e39: Find the non-background connected shape that contains a gray cell (5), and treat that gray cell as the shape’s anchor. Copy the whole shape to the location of the other lone gray cell so that the source gray and target gray coincide. In the copied version, omit the gray anchor cell itself (leave that target cell as background 0), while keeping the original shape unchanged.
2ccd9fef: The input consists of several identical-sized copies of the same panel, tiled vertically or horizontally, sharing the same fixed background/frame but showing different stages of a small colored pattern inside. The output is a single panel of that base size, formed by combining the copies so that the moving/growing colored marks are superimposed into one completed figure. In effect, repeated panels are collapsed into one by keeping the common template and merging the non-background differences across the copies.
2dc579da: The input contains a full horizontal line and a full vertical line of the same color, forming a cross that splits the grid into four corner rectangles. Three of those regions are uniform, while one contains a single differently colored cell. The output is simply that one corner region, cropped out unchanged, with the cross and the other three regions discarded.
2dd70a9a: The grid contains one short segment of 3s and one short segment of 2s, plus scattered 8s that act as obstacles. Extend the 3-segment with more 3s to make an orthogonal path to the 2-segment: continue straight in the segment’s own orientation until blocked by an 8, then turn 90° and continue toward the 2s, repeating as needed. Only empty cells are painted, and the path stops when it reaches the side of the 2-segment.
2de01db2: Process each row independently. Ignore any “noise” colors other than 0, 4, 6, and 8 by treating them as background, then identify the row’s single meaningful color among {4,6,8}. The output is the rowwise inverse of that color/background pattern: every cell that was that color becomes 0, and every other cell becomes that row’s color.
2dee498d: The input is made of three identical copies of a smaller pattern placed side by side horizontally. The transformation removes the redundancy by keeping only one copy: output the leftmost third of the grid, with the same height as the input.
2e65ae53: The grid contains several large rectangular “window” frames drawn in the dominant nonzero color, each split by one full horizontal and one full vertical divider into four quadrants. Across all such frames, the four quadrant roles are shared: whichever color appears in a given quadrant position in any one frame defines the color for that quadrant in every frame. The output fills each frame’s quadrants uniformly with these canonical quadrant colors, propagating the discovered top-left, top-right, bottom-left, and bottom-right colors into frames where those quadrants were empty or only partially filled.
2f0c5170:
The input contains two non-background rectangular windows of 0s. One window has just a single colored “anchor” cell, while the other contains a small pattern made of that same anchor color plus some 4s.  

The output is a blank 0-filled grid the same size as the single-anchor window, and the full nonzero pattern from the other window is copied into it so that the anchor cell lands in the same relative position as the lone anchor in the target window. Any copied cells that would fall outside the window are omitted.
2f767503: The grid contains a small marker made of a line of 5s and a single 9 attached to one end; this marker defines a direction away from the 9 along the axis of the 5-line. Starting from the 5-cell at the same row/column as the 9, look outward in that direction and erase any connected region of 4s found there by turning those 4s into 7s. All other cells, including the 5s and 9, stay unchanged.
2faf500b: Treat each connected nonzero cluster independently. In each cluster, the 6-cells form a straight “divider” line: if the cluster is wider than tall, each 6 marks a vertical split in its row, and if it is taller than wide, each 6 marks a horizontal split in its column. The output keeps only 9s, placing them immediately on the side of each original 9 away from the corresponding 6, so each row/column is split at the 6 positions and the 9-runs are pushed outward, leaving gaps where the 6s were.
305b1341:
The small 2-column legend in the upper-left lists 2 or 3 color pairs. Elsewhere in the input, each first color of those pairs appears as a sparse rectangular lattice/outline marker; for each such marker, the output expands it to the full bounding rectangle plus a 1-cell border, filling that whole rectangle with a checkerboard/parity pattern where the marker color stays on its original parity cells and the paired color fills the alternating cells.

When several pairs are present, each marked region is expanded this way using its corresponding pair, and overlapping rectangles are drawn in the legend order so later ones can overwrite earlier ones. The legend itself disappears, leaving only the reconstructed patterned rectangles on a black background.
30f42897: Treat the rare non-background color cells as marks placed on the grid’s outer border, and measure their positions along the perimeter as a single cyclic loop. Then repeat that same perimeter pattern at a fixed interval equal to the number of marked cells, wrapping around the border, so the color reappears at regularly spaced border locations. The interior stays unchanged; only perimeter cells are added in the marked color.
310f3251: The output is a 3×3 tiling of the input grid, so the whole input pattern is repeated three times across and three times down. Before tiling, add a color-2 marker one cell diagonally up-left of every nonzero cell, with wraparound inside the original grid (so a cell on the top or left edge places its marker on the bottom or right edge respectively). These new 2s are only written into background cells, so original colored cells stay unchanged.
3194b014: Find the largest connected colored blob in the input, ignoring the background/noise cells. The output is always a 3×3 square filled entirely with the color of that biggest region.
319f2597: Find the unique 2×2 block of black cells (0) in the grid. Extend that block across the whole grid by turning every cell in its two rows and its two columns black, creating a 2-cells-thick plus sign. However, any red cells (color 2) lying on those rows or columns are protected and stay red instead of being overwritten.
31aa019c: Find the cell whose color is the rarest in the whole input grid (in these examples, it is a color that appears exactly once). Produce a blank background grid of the same size, keep that rare cell in its original position and original color, and color all 8 neighboring cells around it with color 2, forming a 3×3 block centered on that cell. Everything else becomes background.
31adaf00: The grid contains only 0s and 5s, and the task is to highlight every solid axis-aligned **square** made entirely of 0s, with side length at least 2. Those square regions are recolored from 0 to 1, while all 5s and all other 0-regions that are not such squares remain unchanged. If squares overlap or nest, only the largest non-overlapping ones are kept and filled.
31d5ba1a: The 6-row input is treated as two stacked 3-row patterns: a top pattern made of 9s and a bottom pattern made of 4s. Compare the two halves cell by cell and produce a 3x5 output where a cell becomes 6 exactly when the mark appears in only one of the two halves at that position. If both halves have a mark there, or both are empty, the output cell is 0.
320afe60: The input contains several disconnected white shapes on a uniform background. Each shape is copied horizontally so that its bounding box is flush with one side of the grid while staying in the same rows and preserving its exact pattern of holes/gaps. Shapes that are “open” on exactly one side of their bounding box are moved to the right edge and recolored green (3), while all other shapes are moved to the left edge and recolored red (2); the original shapes are removed.
321b1fc6: Find the one connected shape that is multicolored; it serves as a color template. Copy that exact internal color arrangement onto every other connected shape of the same geometry that is currently a single solid color, aligning by each shape’s top-left corner. Then erase the original multicolored template shape, leaving only its recolored copies.
32597951: Find the rectangular area spanned by all light-blue cells (color 8). Within that bounding rectangle, recolor every dark-blue cell (color 1) to green (color 3), while leaving the 8s and any 0s unchanged. Everything outside this rectangle stays exactly the same.
32e9702f: The input contains a single non-background color on a black background. In the output, every occurrence of that colored shape is shifted one cell to the left, keeping its color and row positions the same; any part that would move off the grid disappears. All remaining cells, including the original black background and vacated cells, are filled with gray (5).
33067df9: The input is read only at the odd row/odd column positions, treating those cells as a small sparse picture.
