Each sampled row becomes one horizontal band in a fixed 26×26 output, and each maximal run of equal nonzero colors within that row is expanded into a solid rectangle whose width reflects the run length and whose horizontal position reflects the run’s column position; the bands are separated by black gaps and surrounded by a black border. If the same colored run appears in the same horizontal place in two consecutive sampled rows, the corresponding rectangles are connected vertically across the gap.
332202d5:
The input has one distinguished vertical column: most rows show an 8 in that column, while a few special full-width colored rows show a 1 there. In the output, each special row turns into a separator row of all 1s, with the distinguished column changed back to 8.

Every other row is recolored according to the nearest special row above/below: it becomes a copy of that row’s color across the whole width, with a 1 in the distinguished column. If a row is exactly midway between two special rows of different colors, it becomes an all-1 separator row instead.
332efdb3: The input content is ignored except for its size. The output is a same-sized grid filled with color 1, except that cells at every odd row and odd column position (using 0-based indexing) are set back to 0. Visually, this creates a solid 1 border and a regular pattern of isolated 0 single cells spaced one cell apart inside.
3345333e: The grid contains a solid rectangular block of one non-background color that is hiding part of a shape drawn in the other non-background color. Remove that rectangle, then reconstruct the hidden part of the shape by reflecting the visible shape across its vertical symmetry axis, so the remaining colored figure becomes left-right symmetric. Cells in the former rectangle become either the shape color or background depending on whether the mirrored counterpart exists.
337b420f:
The 17-column input is three separate 5×5 panels, split by two vertical columns of 0s. Each panel contains one non-background shape on an 8-background; the output is a single 5×5 grid formed by taking the three shapes and superimposing them.

The largest shape is kept fixed, while the other two shapes are translated one step in one of the four cardinal directions so that they fit into the same 5×5 frame without overlapping existing non-8 cells. The result is the merged composition of the three motifs on an 8 background.
3391f8c0: The grid contains exactly two foreground shape families, each in its own color. The output swaps these families: every occurrence of color A is replaced by a copy of color B’s shape, placed at the same object position, and every occurrence of color B is replaced by a copy of color A’s shape at its position. In other words, the two object types exchange both shape and color while keeping their original placements/anchors in the grid.
33b52de3: The grid contains a small rectangular “palette” of colored cells and, elsewhere, an array of identical gray (5) shapes arranged in the same row-by-column layout. Each gray shape is recolored uniformly using the color from the palette cell at the corresponding position in that array. Everything else, including the palette itself and all background zeros, stays unchanged.
3428a4f5: The grid is split into two equal 6×5 patterns by the horizontal row of 4s. Compare the upper and lower patterns cell by cell: if the two cells are the same, the output cell is 0; if they differ, the output cell is 3. The result is therefore a 6×5 difference map showing where the two halves disagree.
342ae2ed: The grid contains exactly two separate objects of each non-background color. For each color, the output draws a straight 8-direction diagonal/orthogonal line of that same color between the two matching objects, choosing the pair of opposite corners that are closest to each other. The original colored blocks stay unchanged, and only the missing cells along the connecting segment are added.
342dd610: The grid is rebuilt on an all-8 background, and any special colored cells are copied to shifted positions based on their color. Color 7 moves up by 2 rows, color 9 moves down by 2 rows, color 2 moves left by 2 columns, and color 1 moves right by 1 column. The original colored cells disappear, so the output shows only these translated markers.
3490cc26: The grid contains one red 2×2 block and several blue 2×2 blocks. Starting from the red block, repeatedly move to the nearest unvisited blue block that is exactly aligned with the current block in the same row or column and has no other 2×2 block in between; fill the gap between them with orange 7s as a 2-cell-thick straight bar. After each connection, that blue block becomes the new current block, and the process stops when no further straight aligned connection is possible.
34b99a2b: The input is split into two equal 5×4 panels by the vertical column of 4s in the middle, and that divider is removed. The output compares the left and right panels cell-by-cell, ignoring the actual nonzero color values: place a 2 wherever exactly one of the two corresponding cells is filled (nonzero), and leave 0 wherever both are empty or both are filled. In other words, the output is the occupancy XOR of the two side panels.
34cfa167: The input contains two separate blocks of color 1; these act as opposite corner anchors of a larger rectangular motif. The output recreates the local pattern attached to the first 1-block at both anchor positions, then extends it across the rectangle between them: the colors immediately to the right of the first block are repeated horizontally along the top and bottom bands, and the colors immediately below it are repeated vertically along the left and right bands. The result is a symmetric rectangular frame/panel connecting the two 1-blocks, with the original background elsewhere unchanged.
351d6448:
The input is split into four 3-row “frames” separated by full-width gray divider rows. Each frame shows the same motif one step later than the previous one, typically by shifting a changed part horizontally or extending a colored run.

The output is the next 3-row frame in that progression, with the gray separators omitted. In other words, infer the step-by-step horizontal evolution shown across the four stacked snapshots and extrapolate one more snapshot.
358ba94e: The input contains several separate monochrome shapes of the same outer size, each being a filled square-like block with some black holes inside. Count the number of black cells inside each shape’s bounding box and find the one whose hole count is the common/majority value among the set. The output is that selected shape, cropped to its 5×5 bounding box and preserved exactly.
3618c87e: The non-background color-1 cells “drop” straight down within their own columns to the opposite edge of the grid, landing on the border line of color-5 cells. Their original positions are erased to background, and the destination border cells in those same columns are changed from 5 to 1. All other cells, including the intermediate 5s, stay unchanged.
363442ee:
The grid contains a colored 3×3 “template” block in the upper-left, separated from the rest by a full vertical line of color 5. Every isolated cell of color 1 elsewhere in the grid marks where another copy of that 3×3 template should be placed.  

For each marker, paste the template without rotation or reflection so that the template’s center cell lands exactly on the color-1 cell, overwriting that marker. The original template and the separator line remain unchanged.
36d67576: The grid contains several disconnected copies of the same yellow shape (color 4), but only one copy is “complete”: it also has nearby decoration cells in other colors. That complete copy serves as a template, and every other yellow copy is filled in by adding the same non-yellow cells in the corresponding relative positions after applying the rotation/reflection that matches that copy’s orientation. Existing cells are preserved, so the result is that all copies become fully decorated like the template.
36fdfd69: Find each connected cluster of color 2 and look at its smallest axis-aligned bounding rectangle. Inside that rectangle, any cells of the other non-background color are turned into color 4, while the 2-cells themselves stay unchanged. In effect, each irregular 2-based shape is “completed” into a solid rectangle by recoloring the missing parts with 4.
37ce87bb: The grid contains vertical stacks of colored cells (8s and sometimes 2s) on a 7-background. Add a new vertical line of 5s exactly two columns to the right of the rightmost existing non-background column. The 5-line occupies only the bottom part of the grid: its height equals the difference between the number of 8-cells and the number of 2-cells, so it starts that many rows up from the bottom and extends to the last row.
37d3e8b2: Find each connected light-blue (8) shape and count how many enclosed black regions (“holes”) it contains inside its bounding area. Recolor the entire shape according to that hole count while leaving the holes and background unchanged: 1 hole → color 1, 2 holes → color 2, 3 holes → color 3, and 4 or more holes → color 7.
3906de3d: The grid contains a fixed shape of 1s near the top and some 2s lower down. Treat each column independently: all 2s in that column slide straight upward as one vertical stack until the topmost 2 is immediately below the highest blocking 1 in that column, or at the top edge if there is no 1. The 1s stay unchanged, the 2s keep their count per column, and their old lower positions are cleared to 0.
396d80d7: The input contains a small non-background motif made of two non-background colors: one color forms the main shape, and the other appears only in interior cells of that shape. The output adds the interior color to the background cells that touch the shape only diagonally, but not orthogonally, creating an outer ring of that color at the shape’s diagonal corners. In effect, the inner accent color is propagated to the diagonal-only neighbors around the figure, while everything else stays unchanged.
3979b1a8:
The 5×5 input always has three colors: the corner color, the center-cross color, and the remaining background color. The output is a 10×10 grid made by copying the input into the upper-left quadrant, then extending it outward using these three colors in a repeating cycle: corner → center → background.

The added top-right columns are vertical stripes of that cycle, the added bottom-left rows are horizontal stripes of the same cycle, and the bottom-right quadrant is filled as nested square/diagonal layers based on distance from the original 5×5 block, with the main diagonal of that quadrant advanced to the next color in the cycle.
39a8645d: The input contains several disconnected non-background objects, each a small single-color shape. Ignore their positions, compare their shapes up to translation, and find the shape that appears most often among all objects. Output that repeated shape alone, cropped to its own bounding box and drawn on a background-colored canvas.
39e1d7f9:
The picture is a regular lattice of cells separated by solid grid lines. One cell contains a multicolored “template” object: a central colored block together with some neighboring colored blocks around it; the same central block color also appears elsewhere without that full neighborhood.

The transformation copies the neighborhood pattern from that template to every other occurrence of the same central block color, placing the same surrounding colored cells at the same relative offsets (up/down/left/right, and diagonals if present). Existing grid lines and already-colored cells stay unchanged; only empty surrounding cells are filled.
3a301edc: Each input contains one nonzero rectangular figure made of a large outer block and a smaller inner block of a different color. The output adds a larger surrounding rectangle in the inner block’s color, centered on the original figure: it extends outward by the inner block’s height above/below and by the inner block’s width left/right, clipped by the grid edges. The original outer and inner colored parts are then kept unchanged on top of this new surround.
3aa6fb7a: The grid contains separate light-blue (8) shapes, each being a 3-cell L that occupies three corners of a rectangle. For each such shape, place a dark-blue (1) cell at the missing corner of its bounding rectangle, but only if that corner is currently background. In these examples, this means completing each incomplete 2×2 square of 8s by adding a 1 in the fourth corner.
3ac3eb23: Each nonzero cell in the top row acts as a seed for a vertical 3-column zigzag/checker pattern of the same color. The seed’s own column is filled on rows 0, 2, 4, …, while the columns immediately to its left and right are filled on rows 1, 3, 5, …. This alternating pattern continues down to the bottom of the grid, with all other cells remaining black.
3ad05f52: The blue 8-cells form one or more outlined “pipe/grid” structures, and a single non-8 color appears as a seed inside some enclosed compartments. That seed color spreads through the interior empty spaces of the 8-structures, propagating across connected compartments and corridors while never crossing the 8 walls. In effect, every region inside the 8 framework that is reachable from the initial colored patch gets filled with that same color, while the 8 outlines and the exterior background stay unchanged.
3af2c5a8: The input pattern is expanded into a grid with double the width and double the height. Put the original input in the upper-left quadrant, place its left-right mirror in the upper-right, then mirror that whole top half top-to-bottom to form the bottom half. The result is a 2×2 symmetric arrangement: original, horizontal reflection, vertical reflection, and both reflections.
3b4c2228:
The input contains scattered cells of several colors, but only the green cells (color 3) matter: count how many complete 2×2 green squares appear anywhere in the grid.  
The output is always a 3×3 black grid, with that count encoded by placing blue 1s on the main diagonal from top-left downward: one block gives only the top-left 1, two blocks give the first two diagonal 1s, and three blocks fill the whole diagonal.
3bd292e8: The red/2 cells stay fixed and act as barriers that split the background 7-cells into connected regions. Starting from the region that touches the bottom-left corner, recolor that whole region 5, then move across each red barrier to adjacent 7-regions and color them alternately 3, 5, 3, … so neighboring regions on opposite sides of a 2-path get opposite colors. Thus the 7-regions become an alternating two-color map, with the bottom-left region seeded as 5.
3bd67248: The input is a square grid whose leftmost column is a solid nonzero color and everything else is background. Keep that colored left column unchanged, then add a descending anti-diagonal of red `2`s across the empty area, starting at the top-right corner and moving down-left until it reaches column 1. Finally, fill the bottom row from column 1 to the right edge with yellow `4`s.
3bdb4ada: The input contains one or more solid monochrome rectangles whose short side is 3 cells. Each such rectangle is turned into a “windowed” block by changing only its middle line: along that center row, every other cell is replaced by black, starting from the second cell of the rectangle, while the outer border cells stay the original color. All other cells remain unchanged.
3befdf3e: Each non-background object is a rectangular frame of one color surrounding a solid inner rectangle of a second color. In the output, the two colors are swapped within that original rectangle: the old inner color becomes the rectangle’s border, and the old border color fills the inside. Then the old border color is extended outward from all four sides as four centered rectangular arms, with arm thickness equal to the original inner rectangle’s height/width in that direction, creating a plus-shaped expansion around the swapped rectangle.
3c9b0459: The task rotates the entire input picture by 180 degrees. Equivalently, the grid is flipped top-to-bottom and left-to-right, so each cell moves to the position directly opposite through the center. Colors stay the same; only their positions are reversed.
3cd86f4f: The output is the input grid turned into a right-leaning diagonal strip by shifting each row horizontally. If the grid has height \(H\), the top row is shifted right by \(H-1\) cells, the next by \(H-2\), and so on until the bottom row is not shifted; the empty spaces are filled with black (0). Equivalently, each row keeps its contents unchanged but gets decreasing left padding and increasing right padding, so the total width grows by \(H-1\).
3d31c5b3: The 12×6 input is four aligned 3×6 layers stacked vertically: a gray/black layer (5), then a yellow layer (4), a red layer (2), and a cyan layer (8). The output is a single 3×6 composite made by taking the top gray layer as the base, then filling its black cells with colors from the lower layers at the same positions in this priority order: yellow first, then cyan, then red. Existing nonblack cells are preserved, so each lower layer only fills gaps left by the earlier ones.
3d588dc9: The grid contains several black 0-shapes and several other colored objects; among the black shapes, exactly one is a 14-cell “wedge/stair-step” shape. Find the non-singleton colored object whose vertical span overlaps this black wedge the most (breaking ties by choosing the closer one horizontally), then treat the side of the black wedge facing that object as its contact side. Recolor that contact edge of the black wedge to 6, and erase the remaining protruding “tail” cells of that wedge back to the background color; everything else stays unchanged.
3d6c6e23: Nonzero cells are treated column by column. In each occupied column, the scattered cells are erased and replaced by a centered triangular stack at the bottom of the grid, using rows of widths 1, 3, 5, … so that the total number of cells in each color is preserved. If a column contains multiple colors, they form successive layers of the triangle in top-to-bottom order according to their vertical order in the input.
3de23699: Find the four isolated cells of one color that sit at the corners of a rectangle; they act as a frame around a differently colored shape. Take only the area strictly inside that rectangle, discard the border/corner cells, and recolor every non-background cell in that interior to the corner-marker color. The output is this cropped interior pattern, with zeros left unchanged.
3e980e27: Each non-isolated marker color defines a template: find the connected object containing a **3** and take the rest of that object as its decoration pattern, and likewise find the connected object containing a **2** and take the rest of that object as its decoration pattern. Then copy the **3**-template unchanged onto every other lone **3** cell, aligning the template’s original 3-position with that cell; copy the **2**-template onto every other lone **2** cell the same way, but first mirror that template left-right. The original objects and marker cells remain in place.
3eda0437: Find the largest axis-aligned rectangular block made entirely of black cells (color 0), with both height and width at least 2. Recolor that whole zero rectangle to magenta (color 6), leaving every other cell unchanged. If multiple such rectangles exist, the chosen one is the one with greatest area.
3ee1011a: The input contains several isolated non-black monochrome objects, each just a short line or block of some size. These objects are reordered by size, and their colors are used to build a square output whose side length equals the largest object size. The largest object’s color fills the outer border, the next largest fills the next inner square ring, and so on, producing concentric one-cell-thick square layers, with the smallest object occupying the center region.
3f23242b: Each green cell (3) is treated as the center of a fixed 5-row motif. Two rows above it, draw a 5-cell horizontal bar of 5s; one row above, on the same row, place a 5 directly above the green and 2s at the left and right ends; on the green’s row and the row below, keep/add those side 2s; and two rows below, draw a 5-cell horizontal bar of 8s centered on the green. On that bottom row, all remaining cells across the grid are filled with 2, so the 8-bar sits inside a full-width horizontal red line.
3f7978a0: Find the pair of gray (5) vertical bars that are each capped by a blue (8) cell at both their top and bottom ends. These two bars and their four blue caps define a rectangle: the bars are the left/right sides, and the blue caps mark the top and bottom corners. Output the cropped subgrid spanning exactly that rectangle, preserving all cells inside unchanged.
4093f84a: The grid contains a solid gray bar/rectangle (color 5) spanning the full height or full width, plus scattered single cells of one other color on either side of it. Each colored cell is “pulled” straight toward the gray bar along the perpendicular direction and turns gray; cells from the same row/column and same side pack tightly against the bar with no gaps, forming short gray extensions outward from it. The original colored cells disappear, and the central gray bar remains in place.
40f6cd08: The grid contains several separate solid rectangles sharing the same base color; exactly one of them is decorated internally with a nested “frame/stripe” pattern made of additional colors. The task is to use that decorated rectangle as a template and redraw the same layered border structure inside every other plain rectangle of that base color. The copied pattern is adapted to each target rectangle’s size, preserving the order of colored layers from the outside inward.
412b6263:
The input picture is rotated 90° counterclockwise. This rotated image is then framed with a 1-cell border of color 1 on the left and right, and a special horizontal separator row of `7 ... 7` with 1s in between is placed above, between, and below two identical copies of the framed rotated image.  

So the output is: separator row, bordered rotated image, separator row, the same bordered rotated image again, separator row.
414297c0: Find the large solid-colored rectangular block and crop the picture to that block; this cropped rectangle becomes the output canvas, filled with its original background color. Inside that block, keep the special non-background cells in their same positions. Then, for each such colored cell, look elsewhere in the input for an occurrence of the same color that is surrounded by red (2) cells, and copy that red neighbor pattern onto the matching cell inside the rectangle using the same relative offsets.
41ace6b5:
The grid is organized around the odd-numbered columns, which contain fixed colors (top rows of 7s, then a row of 2s, then a row of 5s). The even-numbered columns are “completed” into a clean vertical staircase pattern: above the 2-row they become 8, on the 5-row they become 1, and below that they become 1 for a certain depth and then 9 farther down.  

In other words, the scattered 8s and 1s in the even columns indicate how far each column’s vertical fill has progressed; the output regularizes this by extending each even column into a full contiguous stack, while leaving the odd columns’ original 7/2/5 structure unchanged.
41e4d17e: Find each isolated blue frame made of 1s that is a 5×5 hollow square/ring. For every such square, locate its center cell and draw a magenta cross through it: extend a vertical line through the whole grid in the center column and a horizontal line through the whole grid in the center row. These new 6-colored lines are only added on background cells, so the original blue square borders remain unchanged and appear on top.
423a55dc: The input contains a single non-background shape. The output redraws that same shape with a leftward shear anchored on its bottom row: cells on the bottommost occupied row stay fixed, and each row above is shifted left by exactly its vertical distance from that bottom row. This makes vertical parts become up-left diagonals while preserving the original color and grid size; any shifted cells that would leave the grid are simply clipped.
4258a5f9: Each non-background seed cell (the gray 5s) is kept in place and gets a one-cell border added around it in all eight directions. In other words, every gray cell becomes the center of a 3×3 block, with its eight neighboring cells colored blue (1) while the center stays gray. If these blue neighborhoods overlap, they simply merge.
4290ef0e: The input contains one disconnected object for each non-background color; each such object is a small motif that belongs in one sector of a larger symmetric picture. These motifs are ordered from outermost/largest to innermost/smallest, placed along the main diagonal of a new square canvas, and then copied by horizontal, vertical, and diagonal reflection so the final image has 4-way symmetry. The output is this reconstructed symmetric square, on the original background color, with any singleton color becoming the exact center cell.
42918530: The grid is a lattice of separated 5×5 colored square frames. For each color, look across all frames of that color: if one of them contains a non-empty 3×3 interior pattern, that pattern is treated as the template for that color. Then every same-colored frame whose interior is completely empty is filled with that same interior shape, using its own frame color, while all other cells remain unchanged.
42a15761: The grid is split into equal 3-column vertical blocks, separated by single full-height black columns. These 3-column blocks are reordered from left to right by increasing number of black cells they contain, while each block’s internal pattern stays unchanged. The black separator columns remain in place between the reordered blocks.
42a50994: The grid is filtered to remove all singleton colored cells. Any non-background cell that does not touch another cell of the same color in any of the 8 neighboring directions (including diagonals) is erased and replaced by the background color. All same-colored clusters of size 2 or more are left unchanged.
42f14c03:
Find the non-background object that has “holes” or missing cells inside its own bounding rectangle. Those missing-cell shapes are used as slots: if elsewhere in the grid there are separate connected components of exactly the same shapes (possibly in different colors), place those colors into the holes.  

The output is just that object’s bounding box, with its original color preserved and each internal gap recolored by the matching external component(s).
42f83767:
The input contains three parts: a small palette row at the upper left listing the active nonzero colors, a rectangular “glyph library” at the upper right made of several equal-width patterns drawn in color 5, and a lower-left color matrix. The output is built by replacing each cell of that lower-left matrix with the corresponding 5-pattern block from the library, recolored so that 5 becomes that cell’s color.  

So the lower-left pattern is magnified into a mosaic: each symbol/color in it expands into its own template block, and these blocks are concatenated in the same row/column arrangement as the original matrix.
4347f46a: Each non-background colored block is turned from a solid filled rectangle into a hollow rectangular frame. Keep the outer one-cell-thick border of each colored region in its original color, and replace all cells strictly inside that border with the background color (black/0). The positions, sizes, and colors of the rectangles otherwise stay the same.
4364c1c4: The input contains pairs of touching non-background objects of different colors, stacked vertically so one object is above the other. For each such adjacent pair, the upper object is shifted one cell left and the lower object is shifted one cell right, preserving each object’s exact shape. Everything else stays as background, so the two parts appear to pull apart horizontally.
444801d8: Each non-background shape is a blue open-top “cup” or rectangular outline containing one differently colored seed cell. For each cup, fill all empty cells inside the cup with the seed’s color, and also paint a horizontal strip of that same color on the row immediately above the cup, spanning the cup’s full width. The blue outline itself and all unrelated cells remain unchanged.
445eab21: The input contains multiple separate non-background shapes, typically hollow rectangular frames of different colors. Compare the shapes by the area of their bounding box (height × width), pick the largest one, and ignore the others. The output is a fixed 2×2 block filled entirely with that chosen shape’s color.
447fd412: Find the unique mixed red/blue motif: a red square (or single red cell) with a blue shape attached to it. Use that blue part as a template, and for every other red square/block in the grid, add the same blue shape in the same relative position/orientation to that red block. If the target red block is \(k \times k\), scale the blue template by factor \(k\) (replace each template cell by a \(k \times k\) block), while leaving all original cells unchanged.
44d8ac46: Copy the grid unchanged except for certain black holes inside the gray (5) shapes. For each connected gray object, look at the empty cells inside its bounding box; if those missing cells form a solid axis-aligned square (including a 1×1 square), fill that square with red (2). Non-square or irregular holes are left unchanged.
44f52bb0: The input is a 3×3 pattern of red cells on black, and the output is always a 1×1 indicator cell. If the pattern has mirror symmetry in either direction—left/right or top/bottom—the output is color 1; if it has neither vertical nor horizontal reflection symmetry, the output is color 7.
4522001f: The input contains a 2×2 corner pattern made of three green cells and one red cell; the red cell marks which corner of that little square is special. In the output, this 2×2 pattern is enlarged into a solid 4×4 green square, and a second identical 4×4 green square is placed diagonally opposite it. The whole result is drawn on a 9×9 black canvas, with the red cell used only as an orientation marker and not appearing in the output.
456873bc: The grid is partitioned into equal square blocks by a solid bar of 3s, which serves only as a separator and is erased in the output. One non-bar block containing a small pattern of 2s is taken as a template, and every 2-cell in that template “stamps” a copy of the whole template into the corresponding block position of the full grid. In each stamped copy, the cell corresponding to the triggering 2 is recolored from 2 to 8, while all other copied 2s stay 2 and all 3s disappear to 0.
45737921: The grid contains one or more separate non-background shapes, and each such shape is made from exactly two colors. For each shape independently, swap those two colors everywhere inside the shape: every cell of the first color becomes the second, and every cell of the second becomes the first. The shape’s position and geometry stay unchanged, and the background remains 0.
458e3a53: The grid is partitioned by full-width monochrome separator rows and full-height monochrome separator columns, creating a lattice of rectangular sub-blocks. Among these sub-blocks, some are solid single-color rectangles; the task extracts the colors of those solid rectangles and arranges them into a smaller grid in the same relative row/column layout. In other words, the output is a compressed map of the uniformly filled interior blocks, ignoring the patterned background and separator lines.
45bbe264: Each non-background colored cell acts as the center of a full cross: extend its color across the entire row and the entire column. When a row from one colored cell crosses a column from a different colored cell, mark that intersection with color 2 instead of either original color. Cells not on any such row or column remain 0.
4612dd53: The blue cells form a sparse orthogonal outline inside their overall bounding rectangle. The output keeps all original blue cells and adds red cells to complete the missing parts of that outline: within the bounded area, fill every background cell that lies on a row or column between existing blue cells, but only along the dominant direction of the figure (horizontal if there are more horizontal line positions, otherwise vertical). In effect, the broken blue line segments are connected by red to make the implied shape continuous.
46442a0e: The output is a 2×2 mosaic made from the input grid and its rotations, so the overall size doubles in both height and width. The top-left quadrant is the original pattern, the top-right is the input rotated 90° clockwise, the bottom-left is the input rotated 90° counterclockwise, and the bottom-right is the input rotated 180°.
465b7d93: The grid contains a hollow rectangle made of color 6 and one separate non-background colored shape elsewhere. Remove that separate shape from its original location, and redraw it inside the rectangle’s interior using the same color, scaled to fit the interior area. If the given shape is just a single cell, the whole interior is filled with that color; otherwise its line/outline structure is enlarged to the rectangle’s inside.
469497ad: The output is the input grid enlarged uniformly by a factor equal to “number of distinct colors in the input minus 1,” so each original cell becomes a solid square block. Among the non-background colors, identify the smallest connected color region; in the enlarged image, draw a border one cell outside that region and place color 2 only on the two diagonals of that border’s surrounding square/rectangle (the four corner-to-corner diagonal rays outside the region). Everything else remains just the enlarged version of the original grid.
46c35fc7: The grid contains one or more isolated non-background 3×3 blocks embedded in a field of 7s. Each such 3×3 block is transformed in place by reflecting its contents across the anti-diagonal (the diagonal from top-right to bottom-left), while the center cell stays unchanged. The blocks do not move; only the arrangement of values inside each 3×3 patch is mirrored this way.
46f33fce:
The 10×10 input is interpreted on a coarser 5×5 lattice by grouping each 2×2 area into one position. Any colored cell in the input marks its corresponding coarse position with that same color, while empty cells stay empty.  
The result is then enlarged to 20×20 by turning each coarse cell into a solid 4×4 block, so each original colored pixel becomes a 4×4 square placed according to its 2×2-group location. Adjacent marked coarse cells of the same color appear as touching larger rectangles.
470c91de: The grid contains several solid-colored rectangular blocks, each touched orthogonally by a single marker cell of color 8. For each marker, find the adjacent rectangle whose cells plus that 8 would make a perfect filled rectangle, then absorb the 8 into that rectangle and shift the whole completed rectangle one cell outward from the marker, i.e. away from the corner/side where the 8 was attached. The output is just these shifted, completed rectangles drawn on a blank 7-background, with all 8s removed.
47c1f68c: The input is a square grid split by a full central horizontal/vertical cross of one color, with a smaller shape appearing only in the upper-left quadrant in another color. Remove that central row and column, then copy the upper-left shape into all four quadrants of the reduced grid by mirroring it left-right and up-down to make 4-way symmetry. Recolor the copied shape with the cross’s color, leaving all other cells as background.
48131b3c: The input uses exactly two colors. Swap those two colors everywhere, then repeat the resulting color-inverted grid in a 2×2 tiling. The output is therefore four identical copies of the inverted input, making the grid twice as wide and twice as tall.
484b58aa: The grid is a periodic wallpaper pattern that has been damaged by replacing some cells with 0s. The transformation recovers the underlying repeat and fills every 0-region with the values that should appear there, extending the pattern consistently in both row and column directions. The output is therefore the same full repeating pattern with all holes removed.
4852f2fa: Find the shape made by the 8-cells and crop it to its smallest enclosing square, padding with zeros above or to the left if its bounding box is not already square. Then count the number of 4-cells in the input, and place that cropped/padded 8-pattern side by side horizontally that many times. The output contains only this repeated 8-pattern on a 0 background.
48634b99: The grid has a background of 7s, several disconnected foreground clusters made of 8s, and exactly one cluster that contains some 9s. The 9-marked segment is removed by turning those 9s back into 8s, and the same vertical segment length is transferred onto a different all-8 cluster of the matching size. The new 9 segment is painted as a vertical run in that target cluster, aligned with the cluster’s left edge and placed at the top if the original 9s were already at the top of their source cluster, otherwise at the bottom.
48d8fb45: Find the unique object that includes the single gray marker cell (5): it is the only multi-color connected component, made of the gray cell plus one colored shape touching it diagonally or orthogonally. Remove the gray marker, then crop the remaining colored shape to its tight bounding box and output that cropped pattern on a black background.
48f8583b: The 3×3 input is enlarged to a 9×9 canvas, viewed as a 3×3 array of 3×3 blocks. Find the color that appears least often in the input, and for every cell containing that color, copy the entire original 3×3 pattern into the corresponding block position of the 9×9 grid. All other cells in the output remain 0.
4938f0c2: The grid contains a special 2×2 square (color 3) that acts as the center. Take the other colored shape (color 2) and complete its four-way symmetry around that square: keep the original copy, then add its horizontal mirror, vertical mirror, and double mirror so the same motif appears in all four corner regions around the 2×2 center. If those mirrored copies are already present, the output is unchanged.
494ef9d7: Look at each row independently. If a row contains exactly two nonzero cells and those two values are either {4,7} or {1,8}, then the right-hand value is moved left so that the pair becomes horizontally adjacent, immediately to the right of the left-hand value. All other rows stay unchanged, and the moved cell’s original position is cleared to 0.
496994bd: Take the non-background pattern occupying the top part of the grid and copy it to the bottom of the grid as a vertical mirror image: the top row becomes the bottom row, the second row becomes the second-from-bottom row, etc. The original top rows stay unchanged, and the empty/background rows in the middle remain unchanged.
49d1d64f: The output is the input grid padded with a one-cell border on all four sides. The original grid is placed unchanged in the center; each non-corner border cell copies the adjacent edge value of the input (top row extended upward, bottom row downward, left column leftward, right column rightward). The four new corner cells are all set to black/0.
4a1cacc2: The grid contains a single cell of a rare non-background color on an otherwise uniform background. Find which of the four grid corners is closest to that colored cell (by Manhattan distance), then fill the entire axis-aligned rectangle between the cell and that corner with the cell’s color. Everything else stays unchanged.
4acc7107:
The input contains exactly two colors, each split into two disconnected shapes. For each color, take its two shapes and stack them vertically near the bottom of the grid: the shape that was farther left in the input goes on the bottom, and the other goes above it, with one empty row between them; both are left-aligned within that color’s stack.

Then place the two color-stacks side by side, separated by one empty column. The color whose shapes appear farther left in the input becomes the left stack, and the other color becomes the right stack.
4b6b68e5: Keep only the nonzero connected components that act as closed outlines; everything else in the grid is discarded. For each such closed outline, find the region of cells it encloses and look at the colors originally present inside that region, ignoring background and the outline’s own color. Fill the entire enclosed interior with whichever interior color appears most often, while leaving the outline itself unchanged.
4be741c5: The input is a set of broad color bands, either stacked top-to-bottom or arranged left-to-right, possibly with small irregularities at the boundaries. Compress the picture to a 1-cell-thick “palette strip” by listing the distinct band colors in their order of appearance: output a vertical column for top-to-bottom bands, or a horizontal row for left-to-right bands. Each color appears exactly once in the result.
4c177718: The gray row splits the input into a top part and a 9-row bottom part; the output is just that bottom part. Keep the single shape already in the lower part fixed, then take the rightmost non-gray shape from the upper part (ignoring the upper 1-shape and the 2-shape) and place it in the lower part with the same orientation and left alignment as the lower shape. Put this copied upper-right shape directly below the lower shape if the upper middle 2-shape is “top-heavy” (more cells in its top row than bottom row), otherwise place it directly above.
4c4377d9: The output doubles the grid’s height while keeping the width unchanged. It places a vertically flipped copy of the input above the original input, so the top half is the input’s rows in reverse order and the bottom half is the unchanged input. This creates a picture mirrored across the horizontal seam between the two halves.
4c5c2cf0: Find the unique 5-cell object shaped like the “5” on a die; it is symmetric horizontally and vertically and serves as the center/reference. Leave that object unchanged, and take the other colored shape and reflect it across the die-5 object’s vertical axis, horizontal axis, and both axes. The result is four copies of the non-central shape, one in each quadrant around the central symmetric marker.
4cd1b7b2: The input is a partially filled 4×4 grid using colors/numbers 1–4, with 0 marking blank cells. Fill every 0 so that each row and each column contains all four values 1, 2, 3, and 4 exactly once, while keeping the given nonzero cells unchanged. In other words, the task completes a 4×4 Latin square.
4df5b0ae: Treat color 7 as the background and ignore any object that already touches all four edges of the grid (such as a full border/frame). Take every other connected non-background shape, keep its exact internal pattern and orientation, and move it to the bottom of a new all-7 grid. The shapes are packed side-by-side along the bottom row from left to right, ordered from smallest to largest, with each shape bottom-aligned.
4e45f183:
The grid is split by the black lines into a 3×3 array of 5×5 tiles. One non-black color is the tile background and the other is a small motif; in each tile, that motif is slid within the 5×5 area so that its center of mass is moved to one of the 3×3 positions (top-left, top, top-right, …, bottom-right) according to whether the motif lies above/below and left/right of the tile center.  

So each tile is “snapped” to the nearest corner/edge/center position inside itself, preserving the motif’s shape and colors, while the black separators and overall tile layout stay unchanged.
4e469f39: Each gray object is a hollow rectangular/U-shaped frame whose top edge has exactly one missing cell. In the output, that missing top cell and the whole interior of the frame are filled with red (2). Then, from the gap’s column, a horizontal red line is drawn on the row immediately above the frame, extending all the way to the grid edge on the side where the top border has the longer gray segment.
4e7e0eb9: The grid may be partitioned by full-width/full-height colored separator lines into rectangular sub-panels, and the same rule is applied recursively inside each panel. If a panel has no further separators, treat color 1 as a placeholder: replace every 1 in that panel with the panel’s other nonzero color (or leave it as 1 if no other color exists). If a panel is split by exactly one horizontal separator line, flip the panel top-to-bottom; if split by exactly one vertical separator line, flip it left-to-right.
4f537728: The grid is a lattice of 2×2 foreground blocks (color 1) separated by black rows and columns, with one special 2×2 block in a different color. That special block’s color is propagated to form a thick cross: every foreground cell in the same two rows or the same two columns as the special block is recolored to the special color. Black separator lines stay unchanged, and all other foreground blocks remain as they were.
4ff4c9da:
The grid is partitioned into rectangular tiles by the full-width/full-height separator lines (uniform rows/columns). Within these tiles, color 8 marks a small pattern placed on top of a repeated background motif.

The transformation completes that marking pattern across all equivalent tiles: if several tiles have the same underlying arrangement (ignoring 8s), then every such tile receives the union of all 8-positions seen in any member of that group. Also, if a tile with 8s is the only one of its exact background type, its 8-pattern is transferred to other tiles whose underlying non-separator color layout has the same row/column count profile, so the same shape is reproduced there too.
5034a0b5: The outer border acts as a set of directional labels and stays unchanged. Inside the border, every non-background cell is shifted by one step in the direction indicated by the matching border color: cells colored like the top edge move up, like the bottom edge move down, like the left edge move left, and like the right edge move right; if such a move would leave the interior, it instead wraps to the opposite side of the interior strip. All other interior cells are reset to the dominant background color, while cells of unrelated colors remain where they are.
505fff84: Find each row that contains a dark-blue `1`; in that same row there is a light-blue `8` to its right. For each such row, copy the horizontal strip of cells strictly between the `1` and the `8`, excluding the marker cells themselves. Stack these extracted strips in top-to-bottom order to form the output.
506d28a5: The horizontal row of 4s is a divider: take the 2/0 pattern above it and the 1/0 pattern below it, align them cell-for-cell, and discard the divider. Produce an output the size of one half, where any position occupied in either half becomes 3, and only positions that are 0 in both halves stay 0. In other words, it is the union of the top red shape and bottom blue shape, recolored uniformly to green.
50846271: The red cells (color 2) act as seeds for hidden straight line segments running through adjacent background-colored cells (the rarer color, here 0). These 0-corridors are traced outward from each red cluster in the four cardinal directions, and the reached corridor cells are highlighted as color 8. Then, among the resulting 0-colored objects, any largest 1-cell-thick horizontal or vertical bar is treated as a centerline, and a full cross of that bar’s length is drawn in 8 through its center.
508bd3b6: The input contains a short diagonal chain of blue 8s and one solid rectangular block of red 2s that acts like a wall. Extend the blue diagonal in the same 45° direction using green 3s until it reaches the side of the red block; then continue from that contact point in the reflected diagonal direction, as if the line bounced off the rectangle. Leave the original blue and red cells unchanged.
50a16a69: The input contains a periodic colored pattern in the upper-left, while the bottom-right remainder is filled with one uniform “mask” color. The transformation ignores that mask color, infers the repeating tile from the visible patterned cells, shifts the pattern one step left (equivalently, starts from the second column of the visible repeat), and then extends this periodic pattern to cover the entire grid. The solid filler region disappears and is replaced by the continued checker/stripe pattern.
50aad11f: Find each connected shape made of color 6 and pair it with the nearest non-6 colored object, which is just a single marker cell giving that shape’s new color. Recolor each 6-shape with its marker’s color, then crop each recolored shape to its own tight bounding box. Finally, preserve the shapes’ original overall arrangement order (left-to-right if the set is wider than tall, otherwise top-to-bottom) and pack the cropped shapes together with no gaps by concatenating them horizontally or vertically.
50c07299: The input contains a short anti-diagonal chain of color-2 cells (slanting down-left) on a uniform background. The output discards the original placement and redraws a new anti-diagonal of the same color, one cell longer than the input chain. This new diagonal is positioned so that its upper-right endpoint is shifted up by 1 and left by 1 from the input chain’s upper-right endpoint, and then extended further up-left by its new length.
50cb2852: Each non-background colored object is a solid rectangle. In the output, the rectangle keeps its original 1-cell-thick border, while its interior cells are recolored to 8, creating a framed/hollowed version of the same rectangle. Background cells remain unchanged.
50f325b5: The given 8-cells form a small shape that acts as a template. Find every other occurrence of that same shape elsewhere in the grid, allowing rotations and reflections, where the shape is drawn in color 3 instead of 8. Recolor those matching 3-shapes to 8, leaving everything else unchanged.
5117e062: Find the connected non-background object that contains the unique cyan marker cell (color 8). Extract that whole object’s bounding box as a small output grid, and recolor the marker cell to match the object’s main color, so the output is the marked shape with the 8 replaced by its surrounding color.
5168d44c: A straight dotted line of green cells (color 3) acts as a track, and one green cell is currently enclosed by a red 3×3 ring/frame (color 2 with the green center left intact). The transformation moves that red frame one step forward along the track to the next green dot: downward if the track is vertical, or rightward if the track is horizontal. The old frame disappears, while the green track itself remains unchanged.
516b51b7: Find each connected solid block of color 1 and treat it as a filled rectangle. Keep its outer border as color 1, then recolor the interior as nested concentric rectangular layers: the first inner layer becomes color 2, the next becomes color 3, then color 2 again, alternating inward. Background cells and the positions/shapes of the rectangles stay unchanged.
5207a7b5: The input contains a single vertical gray bar (color 5) starting at the top. Keep this bar unchanged, then use its height as a reference to add top-aligned colored columns on both sides: to the left, fill each successive column with color 8 and make it 2 cells taller than the previous one; to the right, fill each successive column with color 6 and make it 2 cells shorter than the previous one. Stop when you hit the grid edge on the left, or when the right-side height would become nonpositive.
522fdd07: The grid is rebuilt on a uniform 7-background by replacing each non-7 connected monochrome object with a centered square of the same color. For any object larger than one cell, that square is the object’s bounding-box height minus 2 in size (so a 3×3 block becomes a single cell, a 5×6 block becomes a 3×3 square, a 7×7 block becomes 5×5, etc.); for a 1-cell object, it instead expands into a 9×9 square centered on that cell. If the square would extend past the grid edge, it is simply clipped.
52364a65: The grid contains several monochrome foreground shapes on a common background. For each shape, erase the cells in its two leftmost occupied columns, leaving the rest of the shape unchanged and replacing the removed cells with the background color. Thus every colored object is “trimmed” by two columns from its left edge, and very narrow objects may disappear entirely.
5289ad53: Ignore the background and all colors except 3 and 2, and count how many separate connected objects of each of those two colors appear in the input. Then make a fixed 2×3 summary grid, filling cells left-to-right in row-major order: first place one 3 for each color-3 object, then continue with one 2 for each color-2 object. Any unused cells remain 0.
52df9849: The grid contains one or more non-background colored shapes. For any color whose occupied cells form the corners/ends of a simple axis-aligned or diagonal line, fill in the missing cells along that line so the shape becomes continuous; and for any color whose cells lie on the border of an otherwise empty rectangular bounding box, fill the whole rectangle with that color. In short, each non-background color is “completed” inside its own bounding box/connecting span whenever the interior currently contains only background or another crossing shape.
52fd389e: Each yellow/4 rectangular block contains a few embedded cells of one other color. For each such block, draw a larger solid rectangle around it in that embedded color, expanding outward by a number of cells equal to how many embedded non-yellow cells the block contains (clipped by the grid edges). Then restore the original yellow rectangle and its interior colored cells on top, so the result is a colored frame of that thickness surrounding each yellow block, with everything else left black.
538b439f: The grid contains a single full-length separator line (either vertical or horizontal) of one color, plus some solid rectangular blocks of another salient color. Each such block is mirrored across the separator, creating a matching block on the opposite side at the symmetric position. The strip between the original block and its mirrored copy is filled with the separator’s color, while all unrelated cells remain unchanged.
539a4f51: The input is a square pattern made of nested, top-left-anchored square “rings”: each cell’s color depends only on its distance from the top-left corner, so the same color fills a staircase/triangular region where `max(row,col)` is constant. The output doubles the grid size and continues this same ring pattern outward, preserving the original band thicknesses and colors in the same order. Equivalently, the sequence of colors seen along the top row/left column is extended cyclically to larger Chebyshev-distance layers, producing a 10×10 version of the same nested-square design.
53b68214: The input shows a single non-background motif that is already progressing downward by a fixed row/column offset. The task is to place this same motif on a 10×10 canvas and continue the progression in the same direction, so the original rows stay unchanged and extra copies are appended below until row 10 is reached. If the motif reaches the right edge, the continued copy is simply clipped by the grid boundary.
543a7ed5: Find each connected non-background shape (the color-6 objects). Around the bounding rectangle of each such shape, draw a one-cell-thick outer frame in color 3. Also, any background cells trapped inside that rectangle but not part of the original shape—i.e. the shape’s internal holes/gaps—are filled with color 4, while the original 6-cells stay unchanged.
54d82841: Find each small monochrome U-shaped object: three same-colored cells in a row with two same-colored cells directly below the ends, leaving the bottom-middle cell empty. For each such object, place a single yellow cell (color 4) on the bottom row of the grid in the same column as that empty middle slot. Leave everything else unchanged.
54d9e175: The grid is partitioned by gray (5) separator lines into 3×3 compartments. In each compartment, the single colored center cell determines the output: replace the entire 3×3 compartment with one solid color equal to that center color plus 5 (so 1→6, 2→7, 3→8, 4→9). The gray separator lines remain unchanged.
54db823b: The grid contains several separate orthogonally connected blocks made of colors 3 and 9, where 9s are just “holes/details” inside larger 3-shaped regions. Compare these blocks by how many 9-cells they contain, and erase the block with the fewest 9s by turning all of its cells to 0. Everything else stays unchanged.
54dc2872: The grid contains several larger connected multicolor shapes plus some isolated single-cell “markers.” If a marker’s color appears as the unique accent color inside one larger shape, that whole shape is moved so that the shape’s accent-colored cell lands on the marker’s position. Shapes with no matching marker stay where they are, matched markers disappear because they are covered by the moved shape, and unmatched single cells remain.
55059096: Find the green plus-shaped objects (color 3) and consider their center cells. Whenever two centers lie on the same 45° diagonal, connect them by drawing that diagonal path in red (color 2). Only the empty cells between the pluses are filled, so the original green shapes remain unchanged.
551d5bf1: The input contains several hollow rectangular frames made of color 1, sometimes with one or more missing cells on a side. In the output, each frame is “filled” with color 8 in all cells inside its bounding rectangle that are not already part of the frame. Any missing border cell acts like an opening: from each such gap, color 8 continues straight outward in the perpendicular direction until it reaches the edge of the grid.
5521c0d9: The input contains several separate nonzero shapes resting near the bottom of the grid. Each shape is moved straight upward by exactly its own height, keeping its color, width, and horizontal position unchanged. The original locations are cleared back to background, so every object appears one “self-height” above where it started.
5582e5ca: Find the color that appears most often in the input grid. Then produce an output grid of the same size in which every cell is filled with that dominant color.
5587a8d0: Ignore the background color 7 and look only at the distinct remaining colors. Count how many cells each non-background color occupies, then build a new odd-sized square whose side length is \(2n-1\), where \(n\) is the number of such colors. Fill this square with concentric square layers ordered by decreasing frequency: the most common color becomes the outer border, the next most common the next inner border, and so on, with the rarest color at the center.
5614dbcf: The 9×9 input is treated as a 3×3 array of 3×3 blocks, and the output is the corresponding 3×3 compressed image. In each 3×3 block, gray cells (color 5) are ignored as noise; if the block contains a colored “brick,” that brick’s color is placed in the matching output cell, otherwise the output cell is 0. Thus the task removes the scattered gray marks and shrinks each solid colored 3×3 region to a single cell in the same relative position.
5623160b: The grid contains a connected framework of color 9 that acts like a hub, with several other colored objects touching it at one side. In the output, the 9-shaped hub stays fixed, while every non-background, non-9 object is detached from the hub and slid straight through it to the opposite side, preserving its shape and orientation. Each object moves along the row or column where it touched the 9s, continuing until it reaches the corresponding edge-aligned position on the far side.
56dc2b01:
The input contains a full-length line of color 2 (horizontal or vertical) and a separate color-3 shape. The color-3 shape is translated straight toward the 2-line until it sits immediately adjacent to it, without changing its form or orientation.

Then a new full-length line of color 8, parallel to the 2-line, is added on the opposite side of the moved shape so that the shape is sandwiched between the 2-line and the new 8-line. The shape’s original location is cleared to background.
56ff96f3: Each non-background color appears as a small set of seed cells, typically two cells marking opposite corners of an axis-aligned rectangle. For each color, fill in the entire bounding box spanning that color’s cells, turning the sparse corner markers into a solid rectangle of the same color. All other cells remain unchanged.
5751f35e: The input is a sparse hint of one or two nested rectangles made from nonzero colors. The output reconstructs these as solid, centered rectangular regions: the color whose marks span the largest rectangle becomes a filled outer rectangle, and the color inside it becomes a smaller filled inner rectangle. If there are three colors including 0, the remaining color is used as the background outside the outer rectangle; if there are only two nonzero colors, the original 0 background remains outside.
575b1a71: The grid is unchanged except for the black cells (0). Group those black cells by the column they lie in, sort the occupied columns from left to right, and recolor all black cells in the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th such columns as 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively. All non-black background cells remain 5.
5783df64: The input grid is partitioned into a 3×3 array of equal-sized rectangular regions. Each region contains exactly one non-black cell, and the output is a 3×3 grid formed by taking that lone colored value from each region and placing it in the corresponding position. In other words, the task compresses the large sparse grid into a 3×3 summary while preserving the regions’ layout.
5792cb4d: The non-background cells form a single 4-connected path of colored pixels on an otherwise uniform background. The output keeps that path in the same locations but reverses the order of colors along it: one endpoint’s color moves to the opposite endpoint, the next color moves to the next position, and so on. In effect, the colored “snake” stays fixed geometrically while its color sequence is flipped end-to-end.
57aa92db:
Each non-background object uses exactly two colors, and one color is shared across all objects. The smallest such object is the template: its non-shared color forms a pattern around the shared-color cell/block, and this pattern defines the layout to copy.

For every other object, keep its shared-color rectangle fixed, then reproduce the template’s surrounding pattern around it, scaling each template cell to the size of that rectangle and recoloring the copied parts with the object’s other color. In short, all two-color objects are completed into the same shape as the template, aligned on the common color.
57edb29d:
The grid is partitioned by color 4 into several rectangular panels. Most panels have a dominant fill color plus a few cells of one shared “symbol” color; one panel is plain, containing only its fill color.

The output is that plain panel, cropped out, but with the shared symbol color painted into it. A symbol cell is placed wherever any decorated panel has that symbol at the same distance from the nearest interior edge/corner (so symmetric positions within differently sized panels are merged), while the border and all other cells stay the plain panel’s base color.
5833af48:
The input contains a large solid rectangle of some color \(C\), and above it a smaller “instruction” area split into left and right parts by a blank column. The left part gives a small stamp shape via its 8-cells, while the right part gives a coarse placement map whose 8-cells mark where copies of that stamp should go inside the large rectangle.  

The output is just the large rectangle cropped out: fill it with color \(C\), then replace cells with 8 by tiling the extracted left-hand 8-pattern at every 8-position indicated by the right-hand map (scaled to the rectangle’s size).
58743b76: The grid contains a large rectangular work area of 0s with scattered marker cells, plus a separate 2×2 color key placed in the non-work-area corner. Split the 0-rectangle into four equal quadrants, and recolor each marker cell according to the matching position in the 2×2 key: top-left markers take the key’s top-left color, top-right take top-right, bottom-left take bottom-left, and bottom-right take bottom-right. Everything else, including the 8-colored border/background and the 2×2 key itself, stays unchanged.
58c02a16:
The input contains a small non-7 template in the upper-left corner, ending at the last row/column that contains any non-7 cell; everything else is background 7. The output fills the whole grid by periodically repeating this template, but whenever a position of the repeated template would land on a 7 inside the template, that copy is replaced by a variant where the template’s secondary non-background color is changed to the template’s main accent color.  

Equivalently: the top-left motif is used as a self-referential tiling rule, producing a wallpaper pattern across the full grid, with 7-valued cells in the motif acting as switches that choose between the original motif and a recolored version.
58e15b12: Each nonzero color forms a pair of vertical bars of equal height. The output replicates each bar-pair repeatedly upward and downward in steps equal to that height, while at each step moving the left bar one column further left and the right bar one column further right, creating an expanding symmetric “V”/hourglass trail of that color. If the trails of the two colors land on the same cells, those overlap cells are recolored 6.
59341089: The input 3×3 pattern is expanded horizontally into a 3×12 strip made of four 3×3 tiles. These tiles alternate between the left-right mirror image of the input and the original input itself: mirrored, original, mirrored, original. Equivalently, each row becomes its reversed version followed by the original row, repeated twice.
5a5a2103:
The picture is partitioned into equal rectangular cells by full-width/full-height separator lines of one color. In each row of cells, the leftmost cell contains a small solid colored block that indicates that row’s color, and somewhere else there is one irregular “template” shape.

The output keeps the separator grid unchanged, erases the original cell contents, and stamps that irregular template into every cell. Each stamped copy keeps the same position/orientation inside its cell, but is recolored using the color of the leftmost marker block from that cell’s row.
5a719d11: The 0-valued rows and column split the picture into independent horizontal bands, and each band contains a left panel and a right panel. In each panel, identify the dominant background color and the sparse foreground shape color. Then erase the original shape colors and redraw each shape using the other panel’s background color; if both panels already have the same background color, both shapes simply disappear and the whole band becomes uniform.
5ad4f10b: Each input has two non-background colors: one color forms a large composite shape made of equal-sized solid blocks, and the other appears as scattered single cells. Take the bounding rectangle of the large block shape and compress it to a 3×3 occupancy map: each of the nine positions is filled if that corresponding large block-region is present, otherwise left empty. Then recolor the filled cells with the scattered-dot color, producing a 3×3 output.
5ad8a7c0: Find the leftmost and rightmost columns that contain any red cells (color 2) anywhere in the grid. Then, for every row whose own red cells already sit exactly at those two extreme columns, fill the entire horizontal segment between them with red. Rows whose red endpoints are not at the global outermost pair are left unchanged.
5adee1b2: The left edge contains 2×2 legend blocks: each block repeats a pair “object color → frame color” in two rows. For every color named in the left column of such a legend, find each separate connected object of that color in the main grid and draw a 1-cell-thick rectangular frame around its bounding box, using the corresponding frame color. The original objects and the legend blocks stay unchanged; only the surrounding frames are added.
5af49b42: The nonzero horizontal bars already present on the top and/or bottom row are the templates. For every interior nonzero cell that is just a single isolated square, find the template bar containing that color and copy the whole bar onto that cell’s row, aligned so the matching color stays exactly at the original cell’s position. If part of the bar would fall outside the grid, only the in-bounds portion is drawn; the original edge bars remain unchanged.
5b37cb25: The grid has a colored frame: the top, bottom, left, and right edges each provide a distinct “direction color.” Inside, there is one non-background interior color forming blobs that contain small background-colored notches or holes touching that blob on exactly three sides. Each such 1-cell notch is replaced by a 3-cell cross centered on it, colored with the frame color corresponding to the open side of the notch: top-edge color if it opens upward, bottom-edge color if downward, left-edge color if leftward, and right-edge color if rightward.
5b526a93: The input contains several identical 3×3 hollow squares/rings of color 1. Take the set of all row positions where such a ring appears and the set of all column positions where such a ring appears; these define a rectangular lattice of possible ring locations. Keep the original color-1 rings unchanged, and fill every missing row/column combination with the same 3×3 hollow ring shape in color 8, leaving each ring’s center cell as background.
5b692c0f: Each non-background connected figure contains some cells of color 4 that mark its symmetry axis. If the 4s lie mostly in one row, that row is the horizontal mirror axis; if they lie mostly in one column, that column is the vertical mirror axis. The figure is completed into a mirror-symmetric shape by copying the fuller side across the 4-axis and deleting unmatched cells on the sparser side, while keeping the 4 cells in place.
5b6cbef5: The 4×4 input acts both as a pattern and as a placement mask. The output is a 16×16 grid split into sixteen 4×4 blocks; for each nonzero cell in the input, the entire original 4×4 pattern is copied into the corresponding block, while blocks corresponding to 0-cells stay all black. Thus the figure is enlarged by self-tiling: the pattern appears exactly at the positions where the input itself is colored.
5bd6f4ac: The output is simply the 3×3 block from the input’s upper-right corner. In other words, take the first three rows and the last three columns of the 9×9 grid and copy that corner subgrid unchanged as the entire output.
5c0a986e: The input contains colored objects on a black background, typically a 1-colored block and a 2-colored block. Keep the original objects, then add a diagonal tail for each: from every color-1 object, extend a line of 1s starting at its upper-left corner and going up-left to the grid edge; from every color-2 object, extend a line of 2s starting at its lower-right corner and going down-right to the grid edge. The result is the original shapes with these color-matched diagonal extensions attached.
5c2c9af4: The input contains three isolated cells of one non-background color. Treat these three cells as the centers of three successively larger, same-centered rectangles: starting from each given cell, expand by a fixed step in both row and column directions to form a square/rectangular frame, and repeat this many times so the frames nest around that seed. The output is the union of all these regularly spaced rectangular outlines, drawn in the original nonzero color on the blank background.
5d2a5c43: The input is split into two 6×4 panels by a vertical divider column of 1s. Overlay the left and right panels cell-by-cell: a cell stays black only if both panels have black there; if either side has a colored cell (4), the output cell becomes 8. The divider is discarded, so the result is a single 6×4 merged panel.
5d588b4d: The input contains a single horizontal run of identical nonzero cells at the far left of the top row; all other cells are background. The output rewrites that run as a 1D pattern made of alternating blocks of the same color, whose block lengths rise from 1 up to the run length and then fall back to 1, with a single 0 separating consecutive blocks. This long sequence is then wrapped row-by-row into rows of the original grid width, padding the final row with 0s if needed.
5daaa586:
Find the rectangle formed by four full-length colored lines: two vertical borders and two horizontal borders. Crop the grid to that rectangle, keeping those border lines as the output’s edges.

Inside the cropped rectangle, one border color also appears as scattered interior pixels. For each such pixel, extend that color in a straight line to its matching border (left/right if it is a vertical border color, up/down if it is a horizontal border color), so isolated marks become connected bars; everything else stays unchanged.
5e6bbc0b: The input is a 0/1 checkerboard with a single 8 replacing one cell. If the 8 lies on the left or right border, each row is compressed into a solid horizontal run of 1s whose length equals the number of 1s in that row; these runs are aligned toward the side containing the 8, and in the 8’s own row an equally long adjacent run on the opposite side is colored 9. If the 8 lies away from the side borders (so effectively top or bottom placement matters), the same idea is applied columnwise: each column becomes a solid vertical run of 1s of height equal to that column’s number of 1s, aligned toward the side containing the 8, and the 8’s column gets an opposite-side run of 9s.
5ecac7f7: The input consists of three same-sized panels placed side by side, separated by single full-height divider columns. Each panel is mostly background color 7 with one non-background connected shape. The output is a single panel of the same size, initially all 7s, formed by copying the shape from the leftmost panel with the highest vertical position, the middle panel with the middle vertical position, and the rightmost panel with the lowest vertical position, preserving each chosen shape’s color and cells.
5ffb2104: Treat each orthogonally connected non-black shape as a rigid piece. All such pieces slide horizontally to the right as far as they can, stopping only when the grid edge or another piece blocks further movement. The shapes’ colors, forms, and vertical positions stay unchanged; only their horizontal positions are compressed toward the right side.
60a26a3e: The grid contains several red “plus” shapes made of four 2s around an empty center. Whenever two such plus-centers lie on the same row or the same column, and there is no other plus-center between them, the empty gap between the two shapes is filled with a straight line of 1s. The line runs only through the space between the shapes, stopping short of the red arms.
60b61512: Find each separate connected yellow shape (color 4). For each one, take its axis-aligned bounding rectangle and recolor every empty cell inside that rectangle to orange (color 7), while leaving the original yellow cells unchanged. In these examples, each yellow shape fits inside a 3×3 box, so the effect is to complete each partial 3×3 block by filling its missing cells with 7.
60c09cac: The task uniformly enlarges the entire picture by a factor of 2 in both height and width. Every input cell, including background 0s, is replaced by a 2×2 block of the same color, so the output is the same image at double resolution.
60d73be6: The grid contains a full horizontal line and a full vertical line of the same color, forming a cross that acts as mirror axes. All non-background, non-axis colored cells are copied by reflection across the vertical axis, the horizontal axis, and both axes, so each marked cell appears in all symmetric positions around the cross. The cross itself and the background stay unchanged, and reflections that would fall outside the grid are simply omitted.
6150a2bd: The output is the input grid rotated 180 degrees. Equivalently, the whole picture is flipped both left-to-right and top-to-bottom, so each cell moves to the position opposite it across the grid’s center while colors stay unchanged.
6165ea8f:
The nonzero cells in the input’s rightmost column give an ordered list of colors. For each of those colors, find the same-colored shape elsewhere in the main part of the input, then build a square comparison table whose first row and first column are labeled by the colors in that order.

Each table entry is a 2×2 block: it is left blank on the diagonal, colored 2 if the two corresponding shapes are the same up to rotation/reflection, and colored 5 if they are different. The whole output is this matrix laid out with 1-cell gaps between the 2×2 blocks.
623ea044: The input contains a single non-background colored cell. In the output, that cell is expanded into an “X”: fill every cell on both diagonals passing through it (top-left to bottom-right and top-right to bottom-left) with the same color, extending to the grid borders. All other cells remain unchanged/background.
626c0bcc: The sky-blue cells form a tiling of small 2×2 motifs: either a full 2×2 square or a 3-cell L-shape (a 2×2 block with one corner missing). Recolor each motif while keeping its cells in place: full 2×2 squares become color 1, and L-shapes are colored by which corner of the 2×2 box is missing — missing top-left → 2, missing bottom-left → 3, missing top-right → 4 (and missing bottom-right would use its own color if present). All background cells remain 0.
62ab2642: Find all orthogonally connected regions of black cells (color 0). Recolor the largest such black region to light blue (8) and the smallest such black region to orange (7), leaving all other cells unchanged.
62b74c02: Find the rectangular nonzero block that sits flush against the left edge of the grid. Reproduce that same block again flush against the right edge, and fill the gap between the two copies row-by-row with the color from the leftmost cell of that row’s block. The result is a left pattern, a solid horizontal extension of its border color, and the same pattern repeated on the far right.
62c24649: The input grid is used as the top-left quadrant of a new grid that is twice as wide and twice as tall. The top-right quadrant is a left-right mirror of the input, the bottom-left quadrant is a top-bottom mirror, and the bottom-right quadrant is mirrored in both directions (equivalently, a 180° rotation). The result is a larger pattern symmetric across both the vertical and horizontal center lines.
6350f1f4:
The grid is partitioned into equal rectangular tiles by separator rows/columns made only of 0s and 5s. Among these tiles, one “clean mixed” tile contains exactly two real colors (ignoring 5), and this tile serves as the reference pattern: its majority color is the default fill, and its minority color marks the special pattern.
