In the output, every non-separator tile is normalized using that reference: tiles containing two colors are replaced entirely by the majority color, tiles containing only the majority color also become solid majority color, and tiles containing only the minority color are replaced by a copy of the reference pattern. Separator rows/columns become all 0, so all 5s disappear.
63613498: Find the gray/5 L-shaped border touching the top and left edges; inside its enclosed corner sits a small colored shape that serves as the template. Search the rest of the grid for another object with the same normalized shape (same geometry, regardless of position or color). Recolor that matching duplicate object to gray/5, leaving everything else unchanged.
639f5a19: Each non-background object is a solid rectangle of color 8, and each such rectangle is recolored independently. Its interior becomes color 4, while a 2-cell-thick frame is added inside the rectangle: the top-left part of the frame is 6, top-right is 1, bottom-left is 2, and bottom-right is 3, with the split between left/right and top/bottom taken at the rectangle’s midpoint. All background cells remain unchanged.
642248e4: The grid contains two opposite solid border lines of different colors, either the top and bottom rows or the left and right columns, plus scattered interior cells of color 1. For each 1-cell, add exactly one new cell in the direction of the nearer border, colored with that border’s color. Thus upper/left-side 1s get a colored neighbor above/left from the top/left border, lower/right-side 1s get one below/right from the bottom/right border; ties go to the top or left border.
642d658d: Find every plus/“flower” pattern centered on a yellow cell (color 4): the cells directly above, below, left, and right of that 4 must all exist and all be the same color. Ignore any such pattern if the surrounding color is also 4. Count the surrounding (“petal”) colors across all these flowers, and output a 1×1 grid containing the color that appears most often.
6430c8c4: The input is split by the solid horizontal divider line of 4s into two equal 4×4 patterns: an upper one with 7s and a lower one with 2s. The output is a 4×4 mask marking the cells that are empty in both halves: put a 3 wherever the corresponding cell is 0 in both the top and bottom patterns, and leave all other cells 0.
6455b5f5: Treat the red (2) cells as walls that partition the black background into separate orthogonally connected empty regions. Find all black regions, recolor every largest region to blue (1) and every smallest region to light blue (8), leaving any medium-sized regions unchanged. If multiple regions tie for largest or smallest, all tied regions get the corresponding color.
64a7c07e: Identify each separate non-background shape in the grid. Move every shape horizontally to the right by exactly its own width, where width means the number of columns its bounding box spans. The shapes keep the same form and rows, and their original positions are cleared to background.
652646ff: The input contains several incomplete 6×6 “diamond/ring” shapes, each associated with one main non-background color; extra stray colors act as irrelevant noise or occlusions. For each such color, recover the full standard 6×6 diamond pattern using the background as the other color, ignoring any foreign cells inside its area. Then output these completed diamonds stacked vertically, ordered so that a diamond that overlaps/depends on another in the input appears above it.
662c240a: The 9×3 input is split into three stacked 3×3 blocks, and the output is exactly one of those blocks. Choose the block whose pattern is **not symmetric across the main diagonal** (equivalently, the other two are diagonal mirrors / transpose-related versions of each other, while this one is the odd one out). Copy that entire 3×3 block unchanged.
668eec9a: The input is mostly a single background color, with several other colors appearing as separate diagonal traces or bands at different heights. Collect all distinct colors other than the background, sort them by the row where each color first appears (topmost first), and if there are fewer than four such colors, pad the front with the background color so there are five colors total. The output is a 5×3 grid whose rows are solid-color stripes, each row repeating one color from that ordered list across all three columns.
66ac4c3b: The grid contains two special evenly spaced “marker” lines of non-background cells, either two rows or two columns. The colored shape on the side of the first marker nearest the edge is copied to the opposite side of the second marker as a mirror image across the gap between the markers. In the copied version, the shape’s color is changed to the color of the first marker line, while the original pattern and everything else stay unchanged.
66e6c45b: The input is a 4×4 grid with a 2×2 block of colored cells in the center. The output clears the grid and places those four center colors into the four corners, preserving their relative positions: center top-left goes to the top-left corner, center top-right to top-right, center bottom-left to bottom-left, and center bottom-right to bottom-right. All non-corner cells become 0.
66f2d22f: Split the input vertically into its left and right halves, which are two same-sized 4×7 patterns. Compare the halves cell-by-cell and produce a 4×7 output where a cell is colored 5 exactly when the corresponding cell is black (0) in both halves; all other output cells are black. In effect, the output marks the overlap of the two halves’ empty/background spaces.
67385a82: Find the non-background colored cells (the green 3s) and group them by side-touching connectivity. Any green connected component with more than one cell is recolored to light blue (8), while single isolated green cells stay green. The background remains unchanged.
673ef223: The grid contains two separate vertical red bars of the same height; one of them has some blue marker cells on rows aligned with that bar. In the bar that has markers, each blue cell is turned into a yellow endpoint, and a horizontal blue segment is drawn from the red bar to that endpoint. Then the same set of row offsets is copied to the other red bar: on the corresponding rows, the whole span on the opposite side of that bar is filled with blue up to the bar, while the red bar itself stays unchanged.
67636eac: Identify each separate non-background colored object and crop it to its own tight bounding box, preserving its exact internal pattern. Then remove all surrounding empty space by concatenating these cropped objects in their original order: stack them top-to-bottom if the objects were arranged mainly vertically in the input, or place them left-to-right if they were arranged mainly horizontally.
6773b310: The full rows and columns of color 8 act as dividers, splitting the 11×11 input into a 3×3 array of smaller regions. For each region, count the color-6 cells; if a region contains exactly two 6s, mark the corresponding cell in the output as 1, otherwise leave it 0. The result is a compressed 3×3 summary of which sectors contain exactly two magenta cells.
67a3c6ac: The output is the input grid reflected left-to-right, as if seen in a mirror placed vertically through its center. Equivalently, each row is reversed while the top-to-bottom order of rows stays the same.
67a423a3: The input contains two non-background line colors: one vertical line and one horizontal line crossing at a single cell. The output highlights that crossing by drawing a color-4 ring around it: fill the eight cells of the surrounding 3×3 neighborhood with 4, while leaving the center intersection cell and the rest of the grid unchanged.
67c52801: The bottom row is a solid “floor” color, and the row above it contains gaps (runs of 0s) inside that floor. All other colored shapes from higher up are removed from their original positions and packed into those gaps so they sit on the floor, extending upward as needed. Each shape is oriented so that its horizontal width matches one gap, so the output keeps only the unchanged floor/base pattern plus the relocated pieces, with everything above cleared to 0.
67e8384a: The input pattern is expanded into a 2×2 tiling of mirrored copies, doubling both width and height. The top-left quadrant is the original grid, the top-right is its left-right reflection, the bottom-left is its top-bottom reflection, and the bottom-right is reflected in both directions (equivalently a 180° rotation). The result is a larger grid with horizontal and vertical symmetry through its center.
681b3aeb: The input contains exactly two separate colored polyominoes on a black background. Move these two pieces by translation only, keeping their shapes and orientations unchanged, until they interlock perfectly to form one solid rectangle with no gaps or overlaps. Then crop away the background and output just that packed rectangle (a 3×3 square in the examples), preserving the original colors.
6855a6e4:
The grid contains a single red frame/bracket shape (color 2) and two non-red colors: one color is the frame itself, and the two other colors are the movable pattern and background. The non-frame pattern that lies outside the open side(s) of the red shape is removed from its original position, mirrored across the frame’s central axis, and placed inside the empty region enclosed between the red parts, leaving the red structure unchanged.

If the red frame is vertically oriented, the outside pattern from the left/right is flipped horizontally into the middle; if the frame is horizontally oriented, the outside pattern from above/below is flipped vertically into the middle. The original copied pattern disappears from the outside.
689c358e: Inside the 6-colored frame, each non-6/non-7 color forms a small shape on the 7 background. For each such color, find its “main” row (the row containing the most cells of that color) and its “main” column (the column containing the most cells of that color), then mark the outer border at those aligned positions. On the side of the border closer to the shape, place that color; on the opposite side, place 0, while leaving the rest of the grid unchanged.
68b16354: The task flips the entire grid upside down. In other words, the rows are reversed in order: the bottom row becomes the top row, the top row becomes the bottom row, and each row’s left-to-right contents stay unchanged.
68b67ca3: The input is a 6×6 grid that encodes a 3×3 picture on every other row and every other column. To produce the output, keep only the cells at even row and even column positions (rows 0,2,4 and columns 0,2,4), discarding the alternating spacer rows and columns. This yields the smaller 3×3 grid with the same pattern of colored cells.
68bc2e87: The input contains several non-background colored shapes that act like overlapping/nested rectangular outlines on an 8-colored background. Determine the containment order of these colored frames/borders—i.e. which color is outermost, which lies inside it, and so on. The output is a single vertical column listing those colors from outermost to innermost.
692cd3b6: The input contains two small red “frame” objects, each centered on a gray 5 and missing one side-adjacent red cell; that missing neighbor is the object’s opening. The transformation finds the opening of each object, then fills with yellow 4 all background cells in the axis-aligned rectangle spanned by the two openings, including the opening cells themselves. Existing red and gray cells are preserved, so the result is a solid yellow bridge connecting the two open sides.
694f12f3: Find the solid color-4 rectangular blocks and treat each as a framed rectangle. Keep each rectangle’s outer 1-cell border as color 4, but recolor its interior: the larger rectangle gets color 2 and the smaller rectangle gets color 1. The background and everything outside these rectangles stay unchanged.
695367ec: Create a fixed 15×15 black grid, using the input’s single color as a separator color. The input’s height and width determine the spacing: after every block of that many rows, draw a full horizontal line in the input color, and after every block of that many columns, draw a full vertical line in the input color. This produces a lattice of black rectangles the same size as the input, separated by 1-cell colored grid lines.
696d4842: Each non-singleton colored shape is a 1-cell-wide path with two endpoints, and each isolated single pixel lies exactly in line with one of those endpoints. For each such dot/shape pair, extend the shape straight from the aligned endpoint until it reaches the dot, using the shape’s original color. Then recolor an equally long segment from the opposite end of the original shape with the dot’s color, so the dot’s color “eats into” the far end while the shape grows toward the dot.
69889d6e: Starting from the single red cell, extend a red path that climbs toward the upper-right in a staircase pattern: alternate one step up and one step right, coloring each visited cell red. If the next step in the intended direction would hit a blue cell, take the other direction instead, so the path bends around blue obstacles while continuing upward/rightward. Stop when no valid next step remains; blue cells stay unchanged.
6a11f6da: Treat the 15×5 input as three stacked 5×5 layers: the top layer contains only color 1, the middle only color 8, and the bottom only color 6. Collapse these three layers into one 5×5 image by overlaying corresponding cells with priority 6 on top, then 1, then 8; black (0) is transparent. Thus each output cell shows the highest-priority nonzero color present at that position across the three sections.
6a1e5592: The grid has a solid colored “roof” at the top with several black holes in it, and separate gray pieces below. Each gray connected piece is moved upward, without rotation or reflection, until it plugs the matching hole shape in the roof; if a piece is taller than the hole, the extra cells hang just below the roof. After moving, those pieces are recolored blue, and their original gray locations are cleared to black while the roof’s original color stays unchanged.
6a980be1: The input contains a repeating stripe pattern built around fixed color-3 separator lines, with some stripes marked by color 2 and others left empty. The output extends that same marked/unmarked rhythm across the whole grid: every stripe aligned with a stripe that contains 2 is filled everywhere outside the 3-lines with the border color, while every unmarked stripe is filled with 0. The 3 separators stay in place, and the original 2 cells remain as 2.
6aa20dc0:
Find the unique multicolored “template” object: it is the only non-background object with the most colors. Within that template, one non-background color appears most often; this is the color to be added, while the other colors mark anchor positions.

Elsewhere in the grid, whenever one of those other template colors appears as an isolated pixel or solid square block, copy the template’s frequent color around it as a scaled version of the template’s shape: keep only the cells of the template that have the frequent color, scale them to the target block’s size, and allow any rotation or reflection that matches the target color’s position in the template. The original objects remain unchanged; only these added copies of the frequent color are painted.
6ad5bdfd: A solid line of color 2 on one border acts like a wall or magnet. Every other colored connected shape is slid straight toward that border—left, right, up, or down depending on which edge the 2-line is on—while keeping its shape and orientation unchanged. The shapes pack tightly against the 2-line and against previously stopped shapes, so the far side of the grid becomes empty.
6b9890af: Find the hollow rectangular frame made of color 2 and crop to that frame; this frame becomes the whole output. Then take the other non-background pattern elsewhere in the input, read it as a small bitmap inside its bounding box, and enlarge it uniformly so it exactly fills the interior of the frame. Place this scaled pattern one cell inside the border, leaving the color-2 border unchanged.
6bcdb01e: The input contains a short 3-colored segment on one border and several 8s acting like diagonal reflectors. Starting just beyond the border segment, extend a straight path of 3s into the grid; whenever the path hits an 8, it “bounces” by turning 90° according to the diagonally adjacent 8 in that corner, then continues. The output is the original grid with this traced bouncing path added in color 3, while the 8s remain unchanged.
6c434453: Find every isolated 3×3 blue ring: a square whose eight border cells are blue (1) and whose center is empty/background (0). Replace each such ring with a red plus sign centered in the same place, so the center and its four orthogonal neighbors become red (2) and the four corners of that 3×3 area become background. All other shapes remain unchanged.
6ca952ad: The grid contains several disconnected shapes of the rare color on a uniform background. Small shapes (up to 3 cells) stay exactly where they are, while every larger shape is moved straight downward so that its bottom edge lands on the bottom row of the grid. The shapes keep their original form and horizontal position; only their vertical placement changes.
6cbe9eb8: The input is a regular repeating background pattern with a few overlaid colored shapes that break that pattern. The output isolates those overlaid shapes: it takes the smallest rectangle covering all non-background colors, keeps the special shapes/colors, and turns any remaining background cells inside that rectangle into 0. In effect, it extracts the hidden picture from the patterned wallpaper.
6cdd2623: Find the unique non-background color whose cells lie only on the grid’s outer border and act as four anchor points. Any two of these anchors that share a row cause that entire row to be filled with that color, and any two that share a column cause that entire column to be filled with that color. Everything else is erased to background, so the result is just the full-width/full-height bar(s) defined by those border anchors.
6cf79266: The grid is left unchanged except for any exact 3×3 square of black cells (color 0). Every such all-black 3×3 block is highlighted by recoloring all 9 of its cells to blue (color 1), while all other cells keep their original color.
6d0160f0:
The 11×11 grid is partitioned by the gray 5-lines into a 3×3 array of 3×3 cells. Find the unique 3×3 block that contains the yellow cell (4), and note the yellow cell’s position inside that block (top/middle/bottom × left/center/right).  

The output keeps only the gray divider lines and otherwise clears the grid to black, except that the entire 3×3 block containing the yellow is copied into the block whose global 3×3 position matches the yellow’s local position inside its source block. In that copied block, only the non-gray contents are kept.
6d0aefbc: The output doubles the grid’s width while keeping the height the same. It places the original image unchanged on the left, and a left-right mirror reflection of the entire input on the right, so each row is followed by its reversed version.
6d1d5c90: The leftmost column is a special marker column and is removed entirely in the output. The row containing the single `2` in that column determines a vertical cyclic shift: after deleting the first column, the rows are rotated so that the rows starting at the `2` row move to the top, with the rows above it wrapping around to the bottom.
6d58a25d: The input contains a distinctive hollow chevron / inverted‑V made of one color, plus scattered single cells of another color. Look at the columns that lie inside the chevron’s opening: whenever one of those columns contains a scattered cell below the chevron, turn that whole interior column into a solid vertical stripe of the scattered color from the chevron’s lower opening down to the bottom of the grid. The chevron itself and all original cells remain unchanged.
6d75e8bb: Find the smallest axis-aligned rectangle that contains all the light-blue (8) cells. Keep the 8s as they are, and fill every other cell inside that rectangle with red (2), leaving everything outside the rectangle unchanged. This turns the incomplete blue shape into a fully filled rectangular block made of 8s and 2s.
6df30ad6: Find the connected shape made of color 5 and keep only its footprint. Among all other non-background colored objects, choose the one that is closest to the 5-shape by Manhattan distance; if distances tie, prefer the color that appears fewer times overall in the grid. Recolor the entire 5-shape with that chosen object’s color, and erase everything else to 0.
6e02f1e3: The output ignores the arrangement of the input colors and depends only on how many distinct colors appear in the grid. It creates a same-sized black grid with a gray line: if there is 1 color, the gray line is the top row; if there are 2 colors, it is the main diagonal (top-left to bottom-right); if there are 3 colors, it is the anti-diagonal (top-right to bottom-left).
6e19193c: Each non-background object is a 3-cell L-shape occupying three corners of a 2×2 square. The missing corner determines a diagonal direction: from that empty corner, continue outward one more step in the same direction and draw a diagonal line of the same color all the way to the grid edge. The original L-shapes stay unchanged, and only these diagonal extensions are added.
6e82a1ae: The input contains several separate gray shapes (color 5) on a black background. Each shape stays in the same place and keeps the same form, but it is recolored according to how many cells it contains: 2-cell shapes become color 3, 3-cell shapes become color 2, and 4-cell shapes become color 1. All background cells remain unchanged.
6ea4a07e: Each input is a 3×3 grid containing black cells and exactly one other color. The pattern is inverted: every colored cell turns black, and every black cell is filled with a new color, so the foreground/background positions swap. The replacement color depends on the original non-black color: 3 becomes 1, 5 becomes 4, and 8 becomes 2.
6ecd11f4: Each input contains two non-background objects: a large single-color shape and a smaller multicolored square/rectangle. The large shape is interpreted as a coarse binary template by partitioning its bounding box into the same row/column counts as the small colored block; a cell is “filled” if that corresponding region of the large shape contains the shape color. The output is the small colored block masked by this template: keep entries where the large shape is present, and replace the others with 0.
6f473927: The output is made by placing the input next to a horizontally mirrored copy of itself, so the width doubles while the height stays the same. In the mirrored half, all black cells become blue (0→8) and all red cells become black (2→0). The side on which the mirrored copy is placed depends on the shape’s horizontal position: if the red pattern already touches the left edge, the transformed mirror goes on the left and the original on the right; otherwise the original stays on the left and the transformed mirror is appended on the right.
6f8cd79b: The output keeps the same grid size as the input and adds a one-cell-thick frame around the entire outer edge. Every perimeter cell (top row, bottom row, leftmost column, rightmost column) is colored 8, while all interior cells are left unchanged. In the given examples, this turns an all-0 grid into a hollow rectangular border of 8s.
6fa7a44f: The output is formed by taking the entire input grid and placing a vertically flipped copy directly underneath it. In other words, the top half of the output is the original image, and the bottom half repeats the same rows in reverse order, creating symmetry across a horizontal line.
6ffe8f07: Find the rectangular block of 8s and treat it as the center of a thick “plus” shape. Extend its row span horizontally across the whole grid and its column span vertically across the whole grid, coloring those cells 4. This paints over background and 2s, but leaves any 1s and the original 8 block unchanged.
7039b2d7:
The input is a large field of one dominant background color, crossed by full-width and full-height divider lines of another color. These lines partition the canvas into rectangular regions.  

The output is a smaller solid rectangle of the background color whose height equals the number of horizontal regions and whose width equals the number of vertical regions — equivalently, one more than the number of horizontal divider rows and one more than the number of vertical divider columns.
705a3229: Each nonzero cell is treated as the corner of an L-shape. From that cell, extend a line of the same color horizontally to the nearer side edge (left or right) and vertically to the nearer top/bottom edge, keeping the original cell as the shared corner. The rest of the grid stays unchanged.
712bf12e: The red cells (color 2) are starting points for paths that grow upward through empty cells. Each path keeps moving straight up until the cell above is blocked by a gray 5; then it shifts one step right and continues upward from there. The path paints every visited empty cell red, never overwrites gray cells, never moves left, and stops when it can neither go up nor shift right.
72207abc: Only the middle row matters: take its nonzero colors in left-to-right order, ignoring any zeros between them. Rebuild that middle row by placing those colors cyclically across the full width, with the spacing between successive colored cells growing by 1 each time: first adjacent, then with 1 zero between, then 2 zeros, then 3, and so on. All other cells remain 0, so the top and bottom rows stay blank.
72322fa7: The grid contains one or more complete 3-cell motifs of the form **A–B–A** (same color on both ends, different center), either horizontal or vertical. These complete motifs act as templates: anywhere else in the grid where only part of that same motif appears—such as just the center cell, just the two end cells, or another incomplete fragment using the same two colors—the missing cells are added so it becomes a full copy of the motif in the same orientation. All existing cells stay in place; the output only completes the partial motifs.
72a961c9: The input contains a single horizontal bar of color 1, with some positions replaced by special colors 2 or 8. Keep that bar unchanged, and for each special cell, grow a vertical tower straight upward in the same column: above a 2, place three 1s and then a 2 on top; above an 8, place two 1s and then an 8 on top. All other cells remain 0.
72ca375d: Find the one non-background object that is perfectly symmetric under a left-right mirror. Then crop the grid to that object’s tight bounding box and output just that rectangular patch, preserving its color pattern and any internal black cells.
73182012: Find the rectangular block containing all non-black cells, ignoring the surrounding black background. Then output only that block’s upper-left quarter: keep the top half of its rows and the left half of its columns. Visually, the colored figure is a horizontally and vertically symmetric pattern, and the output is its top-left seed tile.
73c3b0d8: The full-width red row stays fixed, while every yellow cell is shifted down by one row. Any yellow cell that, after this drop, ends up immediately above the red row becomes the tip of a V: fill both upward diagonals from that cell (up-left and up-right) with yellow until they hit the grid boundary. All other cells remain unchanged.
73ccf9c2: The input contains several disconnected non-background shapes of one color. Select the shape that is **not vertically symmetric** within its own bounding box (all the other shapes are left-right mirror symmetric). Then output that chosen shape alone, cropped to its tight bounding rectangle, with its internal 0s and colored cells preserved exactly.
7447852a: The red zigzag line (color 2) is kept unchanged and treated as a barrier that splits the black background into separate connected regions. These black regions are ordered from left to right, and every third region starting with the leftmost one (1st, 4th, 7th, …) is recolored yellow (color 4). All other cells remain unchanged.
7468f01a: The input contains one non-background colored rectangle surrounded by uniform background rows and columns. Remove that surrounding border to isolate the colored rectangle, then reflect the cropped pattern left-to-right. The output is just this horizontally mirrored rectangle, with the outer background discarded.
746b3537: The input is made of full-length color stripes, either horizontal bands (uniform rows) or vertical bands (identical rows forming uniform columns). Compress each contiguous stripe to a single cell, preserving the stripe order and merging only consecutive repeats of the same color. The output is therefore a one-cell-thick line showing the stripe colors in order: a column for horizontal bands and a row for vertical bands.
74dd1130: The output is the input grid reflected across its main diagonal, from top-left to bottom-right. Equivalently, the grid is transposed: rows become columns and columns become rows. Cells on that diagonal stay fixed, while each off-diagonal pair swaps positions with its mirror counterpart.
753ea09b: The grid has one dominant background color and two other colors; among the two non-background colors, the more frequent one forms two main scattered chains. These two chains are treated as the two sides of a single closed outline by connecting their endpoints around the outer border of the grid, choosing the shorter border connection. Then every background cell inside that enclosed region is filled with the frequent non-background color, while the rarer color and all existing non-background cells stay unchanged.
758abdf0:
The grid has a background of 7s and one full border line of 0s (either a whole row or a whole column). Looking along the direction perpendicular to that 0-border, each position containing 8s is treated independently: if there is a single 8, it is extended into a 2-cell bar of 8s pointing away from the 0-border; if there is already a 2-cell bar of 8s, that bar is removed and replaced by a 2-cell bar of 0s placed on the opposite border of the grid.  

So, isolated 8s become doubled, while already-doubled 8s “flip” into matching 0-pairs at the far side.
759f3fd3: The input contains a full horizontal and full vertical green line (color 3) crossing at one cell. The output keeps this green cross unchanged and adds yellow (color 4) cells in concentric square rings centered on the intersection. Equivalently, every non-green cell whose Chebyshev distance from the cross center is a positive even number is colored 4, while odd-distance cells remain 0.
75b8110e: Split the 8×8 input into its four 4×4 quadrants, each containing one colored pattern on black: top-left (4), top-right (5), bottom-left (6), and bottom-right (9). The 4×4 output is made by superimposing these four same-position patterns into one grid, using a fixed priority: top-right/5 first, then bottom-right/9, then bottom-left/6, and finally top-left/4; if all four cells are black, the output cell stays black.
760b3cac: The grid contains two 5-cell, 3×3 motifs: a color-8 shape and a color-4 “arrow/cross” shape. The color-4 shape is left unchanged and serves only to indicate a direction: if its lone tip cell is on the top or bottom edge, make a vertically mirrored copy of the color-8 shape above or below it; if the tip is on the left or right edge, make a horizontally mirrored copy to the left or right. The mirrored copy is placed immediately adjacent to the original color-8 shape, creating a doubled symmetric pattern.
762cd429:
The input contains a tiny 2×2 color seed at the left edge. The output grows this seed to the right in successive stages whose sizes double each time: the 2×2 pattern is copied into blocks of width 2, then 4, then 8, etc., preserving the same quadrant/color arrangement.

At each stage, the top-row colors fill a rectangle extending upward from the seed’s top row, and the bottom-row colors fill a matching rectangle extending downward from the seed’s bottom row; all these doubled copies are placed side by side to the right until the grid is filled or clipped by the border. Unused cells remain 0.
770cc55f: The grid has a colored segment on the top row, a same-colored segment on the bottom row, and a full-width horizontal divider of color 2 in the middle. Find the columns shared by the top and bottom segments, then paint those columns with color 4 in the empty space between the divider and whichever of the two outer segments is longer. Everything else stays unchanged.
776ffc46: Find the fully closed gray rectangular frame(s) made of color 5, and look at the non-gray shape inside each one. That enclosed shape acts as a template: any other object elsewhere in the grid with exactly the same shape and orientation is recolored to the template’s color. The gray frame, the enclosed reference shape itself, and objects inside incomplete/open gray structures are left unchanged.
77fdfe62: The grid contains a rectangular frame of color 1 with four special colors sitting at the four outer corners. Remove the frame and keep only the interior pattern of 0s and 8s, then split that interior into four quadrants. In each quadrant, replace every 8 by the color from the corresponding outer corner of the original grid (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right), while leaving 0s unchanged.
780d0b14: The input is partitioned by full-width/full-height uniform separator lines (the all-0 rows and columns), creating a grid of rectangular blocks. Each block contains mostly one non-background color with some noise/zeros inside. The output is a compressed grid with one cell per block, colored by that block’s dominant non-zero color, preserving the original block layout.
782b5218: Color 2 marks a separating boundary, and the output cleans the grid into three parts based on that boundary. Keep every 2 exactly where it is; in each column, everything above the lowest 2 becomes black (0), and everything below that lowest 2 becomes the grid’s third color (the non-0, non-2 color). Thus the 2s form a preserved divider, with a uniform black region above and a uniform colored region below, even when the divider is jagged or spread across multiple rows.
7837ac64:
The input is a large regular lattice: one dominant nonzero color forms the grid lines, and a few line cells are replaced by other colors. These replacements occur in contiguous rectangular blocks of lattice intersections, effectively encoding a coarse 3×3 pattern.

To produce the output, ignore the background and the lattice color, and compress those colored replacement blocks into a 3×3 grid: each output cell shows the unique non-lattice color present in the corresponding block, or 0 if that block is empty/ambiguous.
78e78cff: The grid has three colors: a background, a repeated “shape” color, and one special color that appears exactly once. Using the special cell’s column as a vertical axis, paint that special color into the background cells between the nearest shape-colored cells to the left and right on each row; if a row has no such shape cells, fill the whole row with the special color. Above the top shape row and below the bottom shape row, this reduces to a vertical stripe through the special cell’s column, while rows containing the shape keep their original non-background cells.
79369cc6: Find the one 3×3 block that already contains both yellow (4) and magenta (6); this is the template. Use the arrangement of its magenta cells as a shape, allowing any rotation or reflection, and scan the whole grid for other 3×3 windows where those magenta cells appear in the same relative positions. Whenever such a match is found, paint the template’s non-magenta positions yellow, leaving the magenta 6s and everything else unchanged.
794b24be: The input’s arrangement of blue cells is ignored; only the number of blue 1s matters. The output is a 3×3 black grid with that many red 2s placed in a fixed canonical pattern: fill the top row from left to right, and if there is a fourth cell, place it in the center of the second row. All other cells are black.
7953d61e: The output is an 8×8 mosaic made from four copies of the 4×4 input, one in each quadrant. The top-left quadrant is the original input, the top-right is the input rotated 90° counterclockwise, the bottom-left is rotated 180°, and the bottom-right is rotated 90° clockwise. This uses all four orientations of the same pattern arranged in a 2×2 block.
79cce52d: The input is a 7×7 grid whose top row and left column are just framing markers; the real picture is the 6×6 interior. One cell colored 2 appears in that frame, and its row/column position tells how to cyclically shift the interior picture vertically and horizontally, with wraparound. The output is that 6×6 interior after this toroidal shift, so the frame colors disappear entirely.
7acdf6d3: There are two non-background colors: one forms a connected shape, while the other appears as a separate set of stray cells elsewhere. Remove the stray-colored cells from their original location, and place that same number of cells inside the connected shape by filling the horizontal gaps between pairs of cells in its rows. The inserted cells occupy the interior row-segments of the shape, so the shape becomes horizontally filled in those rows, colored with the formerly stray color.
7b6016b9: The nonzero cells form wall-like line drawings on a black background and are kept unchanged. Every black region that is connected to the outer border is recolored 3, while any black region completely enclosed by the walls is recolored 2. In short: mark outside space green and enclosed holes red, preserving the original line color.
7b7f7511: The input is made of two identical copies of the same rectangular pattern. These copies are arranged either side-by-side (left half equals right half) or stacked vertically (top half equals bottom half). The output removes the duplication and keeps just one copy of the repeated block: the left half in the horizontal case, or the top half in the vertical case.
7bb29440: The input contains several disconnected rectangular patches on a black background, each mostly filled with color 1 and possibly containing a few color-4 and color-6 cells inside. Compare these patches by counting how many 4s and 6s each one contains in total. The output is the exact cropped rectangle of the patch with the smallest combined number of 4 and 6 cells, preserving all its original colors and layout.
7c008303: The blue 8s form separators that isolate two meaningful parts of the input: a 6×6 mask made of 3s on a 0 background, and a 2×2 block of four colors. The output is the 6×6 mask alone, but split into four 3×3 quadrants and recolored using the corresponding cell of the 2×2 color block: every 3 in each quadrant is replaced by that quadrant’s assigned color, while 0s stay 0. The separator lines and the color block themselves do not appear in the output.
7c8af763: Treat each connected black region (0) as an empty compartment separated by the gray walls (5). For each such region, look at the orthogonally adjacent colored cells around its boundary and count how many are blue (1) versus red (2); then fill the entire black region with whichever of those two colors appears more often. All non-black cells stay unchanged.
7c9b52a0:
The input contains several disconnected same-sized “windows” embedded in a uniform outer background. Each window is mostly 0s and contains a partial colored pattern; all windows have the same dimensions.

The output is one grid of that common window size, formed by superimposing all the windows on top of each other while keeping each cell’s position within its window. Zeros act as empty space, so only the nonzero colored cells from the different windows are combined.
7d18a6fb: Find the 7×7 block of color 1, which acts as a key. Inside it are four colored marker cells at the four corner-quadrant positions; for each marker color, take the shape of that same color found elsewhere in the input (ignoring the cells inside the 1-block), crop it to its own bounding box, and place it into the corresponding quadrant of a blank 7×7 output. The result is a 7×7 assembly of those four color-matched shapes, with the center row and center column left as zeros.
7d1f7ee8: Find the outermost colored objects: those whose bounding rectangles are not strictly inside any other colored object’s bounding rectangle. For each such outer object, repaint every non-background cell anywhere inside its bounding rectangle to that outer object’s color, while leaving background 0 cells unchanged. Thus, inner boxes/shapes inside a larger box are absorbed into the larger box’s color, but standalone outermost shapes remain as they are.
7d419a02: Find the unique 2×2 block of color 6 and treat it as the center. The surrounding color-8 cells form four diagonal “wings”; recolor those wing cells to 4, but keep the axis passing through the 6-block unchanged: if the picture is taller than wide, preserve the two rows of the 6-block, and if it is wider than tall, preserve the two columns of the 6-block. This produces a butterfly-like pattern with a 6-colored body, 8-colored central axis, and 4-colored diagonal wings.
7d7772cc:
The grid contains a single full-width or full-height separator line whose color is unique among the uniform “frontier” lines. Treat this line as a divider: the colored symbols on one side of it stay fixed, while the colored symbols on the other side are redistributed to the outer edge of that side.

More specifically, each non-background cell on the movable side is shifted along its row or column to the far border of that half, but only if the opposite half does not already contain the same color in the corresponding row/column; otherwise it is shifted to the cell adjacent to the divider. The vacated cells are filled with that side’s background color, leaving the divider and the opposite half unchanged.
7ddcd7ec: Find the largest connected colored object as the main body, and treat every other same-colored piece as a small “tip” attached around it. Each such tip is extended outward in the direction from the main body toward that tip, drawing a straight line (here, diagonal) of the same color all the way to the grid edge. The original shape remains unchanged; the output just adds these rays.
7df24a62: Find the one non-background template block: a rectangular patch of color 1 containing some color-4 cells inside it. Use the arrangement of those internal 4-cells as a motif, and search the whole grid for every other occurrence of the same 4-pattern under any rotation or reflection. At each match, stamp the corresponding transformed 1-rectangle there, filling its non-4 cells with 1 while leaving the matched 4 cells unchanged.
7e02026e: The input is a grid of 0s and 8s, and the output highlights certain black cells with color 3. Specifically, every 0-cell that is the center of a plus shape of 0s—meaning its up, down, left, and right neighbors are also 0—is found, and then the whole 5-cell plus (center plus four orthogonal neighbors) is recolored to 3. If multiple such pluses overlap, all cells belonging to any of them become 3; everything else stays unchanged.
7e0986d6: The grid contains one meaningful non-background color forming blocky rectangular regions, plus a second nonzero color that is just noise. Clean the picture by removing all cells of the noisy color: if such a cell lies inside one of the rectangular regions, restore it to the rectangle’s main color; otherwise turn it into background. Equivalently, the output keeps only the solid rectangles of the main color and makes them uniform again.
7e2bad24: The grid contains a diagonal chain of blue/1 cells and one or more straight barrier lines in red/2 or green/3. Extend that diagonal in both directions as if it were a bouncing ray: keep drawing blue cells along the same diagonal, and whenever the path hits a barrier line, reflect it by reversing the horizontal or vertical component appropriate to that barrier’s orientation. The barriers stay unchanged, and the output shows the full reflected zigzag path of the blue line across the grid.
7e4d4f7c: The output keeps only the first two rows of the input, reducing the whole grid to a 3-row summary. The third row is a copy of the top row, but one of its two colors is changed to color 6: specifically, the color that is different from the repeated background color seen in the second row (from column 1 onward). Thus the result is: original top pattern, original second row, then the top pattern with its “non-background” color highlighted as 6.
7e576d6e: The grid contains several parallel 3-cell bars of one color and exactly two single-cell markers of another color; the output uses the marker color to draw one continuous orthogonal “staircase/wire” linking them. The path visits the bars in order along the bars’ main direction (top-to-bottom for horizontal bars, left-to-right for vertical bars), and at each bar it bends so that it passes through the middle cell of that 3-cell bar. In effect, the two lone dots are connected by a rectilinear path that threads through the centers of all the short bars.
7ec998c9: The input is a solid background with one unique non-background cell. In the output, that special cell stays unchanged, and blue cells (color 1) are added to draw a full vertical line through its column plus two edge horizontals: one on the top row and one on the bottom row. These top and bottom segments extend from the special cell’s column to opposite sides of the grid, so the added blue pattern connects that column to one pair of opposite corners (top-left with bottom-right, or top-right with bottom-left depending on the marker’s column parity).
7ee1c6ea: Find the rectangular frame made of color 5. Everything outside this frame, and the frame itself, stays unchanged. Inside the framed area, there are exactly two nonzero/non-5 colors along with black; swap those two colors everywhere inside the frame, while leaving black cells unchanged.
7f4411dc: The input contains one main non-background color forming several solid rectangular blocks, plus scattered stray pixels of the same color. The transformation removes the noise and keeps only those occurrences of a filled 2×2 square of that color. Equivalently, any colored cell that is not part of some solid 2×2 block disappears, so only larger rectangular regions remain.
7fe24cdd: The output is a 2×2 tiling of the input pattern, so the grid doubles in both height and width. The top-left quadrant is the original input, the top-right is the input rotated 90° clockwise, the bottom-right is rotated 180°, and the bottom-left is rotated 90° counterclockwise (270° clockwise). This arranges the four rotational views of the same pattern around the center.
80214e03: The nonzero cells form several large connected “panels” separated in space; together, their top/bottom edges define horizontal bands and their left/right edges define vertical bands, creating a coarse grid of rectangular regions. For each such region, take the dominant nonzero color inside it (ignoring any 0 holes), and place that color into the corresponding output cell. The output is this color summary grid, with the columns ordered from right to left relative to the input.
80af3007:
Find the bounding box of the single non-background shape and treat it as a 3×3 array of equal-sized blocks. Convert that large shape into a 3×3 binary pattern by marking which of those nine blocks contain the shape.

Then produce a 9×9 self-similar image: each filled cell of the 3×3 pattern is replaced by a miniature copy of the same 3×3 pattern, while empty cells become all-background 3×3 tiles. The result is a fractal-like expansion of the coarse 3×3 layout encoded by the input.
810b9b61: Copy the grid, but recolor any blue object that is exactly a hollow axis-aligned rectangle/square border from blue (1) to green (3). In other words, if a connected blue shape matches the full perimeter of its own bounding box and is at least 3 cells tall/wide so it encloses an empty interior, it becomes green; all other blue shapes remain unchanged.
817e6c09: Treat each connected cluster of non-background cells as an object, then sort these objects from left to right. Recolor the objects in an alternating pattern across that left-to-right order: one object stays color 2, the next becomes color 8, then 2, then 8, etc. Which color the leftmost object gets depends on whether the total number of objects is odd or even, so the rightmost object always remains color 2.
81c0276b:
The input is a lattice of full-width/full-height separator lines in one special color, dividing the picture into equal cells that may contain solid 2×2 colored blocks. Ignore the separator color and the background, and count how many times each remaining color appears among those cell-blocks.

The output is a compact histogram: one row per counted color, ordered from least frequent to most frequent, with row length equal to the largest count. In each row, fill the leftmost cells with that color repeated as many times as it occurred, leaving the rest as 0.
825aa9e9: The grid contains a background color 7 plus two other colors: one forms a fixed “support/terrain” region touching the bottom, and the other forms one or more separate objects sitting above it. The transformation moves each object of the non-support color straight downward, without changing its shape or columns, until it rests as low as possible just above the support region or above previously dropped objects in the same columns. In effect, all non-7 objects are gravity-dropped vertically onto the other non-7 color, while the support region itself stays unchanged.
82819916: The first non-empty row is a full-width template made from two colors. Every other non-empty row contains only the leftmost seed of that same two-color pattern, using its own two colors; extend that row across the whole width by copying the template’s color arrangement and substituting the row’s two colors for the template’s two colors. Empty rows and the template row stay unchanged.
83302e8f: The nonzero color forms a lattice of “walls” that partitions the grid into rooms; some wall cells are missing, creating doorways between rooms. Keep all original wall cells unchanged, then recolor every background region: if a connected zero-region is a single fully enclosed room, fill it with green (3); if it connects through one or more openings to other rooms (including the doorway cells themselves), fill the whole connected region with yellow (4).
833966f4: The input is a single vertical column of 5 cells. The transformation swaps the top two cells, leaves the middle cell unchanged, and swaps the bottom two cells. In other words, the order changes from positions 1-2-3-4-5 to 2-1-3-5-4.
833dafe3: The output is a 2×2 tiling of the input reflected across the center, so the final grid is twice as wide and twice as tall. The original input appears in the bottom-right quadrant; the bottom-left is its left-right mirror, the top-right is its top-bottom mirror, and the top-left is mirrored in both directions (equivalently a 180° rotation). This creates a pattern with horizontal and vertical symmetry through the middle.
834ec97d: The input contains a single non-background colored cell. In the output, that cell is moved down by one row, keeping the same column. Then all cells above its original position are filled with color 4 in every other column across the whole grid, choosing the alternating columns that include the cell’s original column.
83b6b474:
The input contains several non-background colored components: up to four larger corner-anchored shapes (each missing one corner of its bounding box) plus one or two thin 1-cell-thick line pieces. The output is a new compact rectangle whose size is determined by the total amount of non-background material, where the larger shapes are placed into the corresponding corners (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right) so that together they outline the border of the rectangle.

Any remaining 1-cell-thick pieces are then inserted into the still-empty full row or full column gaps between those corner pieces, completing the rectangular arrangement. The dominant input color becomes the background of this assembled smaller grid.
83eb0a57: The input contains several disconnected non-background rectangular pattern fragments. Take the largest fragment as the canvas, then place each smaller fragment onto it at the unique offset where the color they share lines up consistently with the canvas’s non-background pattern. The output is this merged composite rectangle, with the smaller fragments overlaid into the large one.
8403a5d5: The input contains a single non-background cell; its color and column determine the pattern. Starting at that column and moving right, every other column is filled completely with the seed color, creating full-height vertical stripes. In the columns between those stripes, place a single gray cell (color 5), alternating between the top row and the bottom row as you move right, beginning with the top row in the first gap column.
84551f4c: Each nonzero column in the input is a solid vertical bar, so it can be read as a sequence of colored columns with heights. The transformation finds every left-to-right chain that starts at a column of color 1 and continues through later nonzero columns whenever the next bar begins exactly where the previous bar’s width would end; such a chain is “laid down” onto the bottom row, turning each vertical bar into a horizontal run of the same color and length. Any nonzero columns not belonging to such a chain are kept as vertical bars in their original positions, and all other cells become 0.
845d6e51: The grid contains a set of colored example shapes and a set of green shapes (color 3) elsewhere; the gray 5-cells are just part of the unchanged scene and are not transformed. Each green connected component is a copy of one example shape, possibly rotated or reflected. Recolor every green shape with the color of the matching example shape, leaving all other cells unchanged.
846bdb03: Find the rectangle whose four corners are the yellow 4-cells; this rectangle becomes the output canvas. Its left and right sides are the two vertical colored bars from the input, while the separate two-color shape elsewhere in the grid is extracted, transposed if needed so its height matches the frame, then inserted between the bars. If necessary, that inserted shape is also flipped left-right so that its left/right color ordering matches the colors of the two side bars.
84ba50d3: The grid contains a full horizontal bar of 2s and one or more connected shapes made of 1s above it. Remove the 1-shapes from above and redraw them below the 2-bar, preserving their columns but flipping them vertically so that each shape “hangs” from the bar: its lowest row or widest horizontal part is placed adjacent to the bar, and the rest extends downward. In the 2-bar, any column where a moved 1-shape touches the bar is cut out, changing that 2 to background 8.
84db8fc4: The grid keeps all nonzero cells unchanged. Partition the black cells (0) into 4-connected regions: any black region that touches the outer border is recolored red (2), while any black region fully enclosed inside the grid is recolored gray (5).
84f2aca1: Find each non-background connected shape that is a hollow rectangular frame, and look at the empty cells inside its bounding box. Fill the interior holes of these frames based on how many cells are missing: a single-cell hole is colored 5, while a two-cell hole is colored 7. Everything else stays unchanged.
855e0971: The grid is partitioned into uniform colored stripes, all oriented the same way in a given puzzle: either horizontal bands or vertical bands. Each black cell (0) inside a stripe is expanded across the full extent of that stripe in the perpendicular direction—so a black dot in a horizontal band becomes a vertical black line through that band, and a black dot in a vertical band becomes a horizontal black line through that band. The black line stays confined to its own colored stripe; everything else remains unchanged.
8597cfd7: The gray horizontal line splits the picture into a top part and a bottom part. Count how many red cells (2) and yellow cells (4) appear above the line and below the line, and compare each color’s change from top to bottom. The output is always a 2×2 solid block colored with whichever of red or yellow increases more from the top half to the bottom half.
85b81ff1: The grid is made of repeating 3-column blocks: two colored columns followed by a full-height black separator column. Only the second column of each block varies, plus the final rightmost column; these “pattern columns” are extracted and ranked by how many black cells they contain. The output keeps all separator columns and the constant background columns fixed, but reorders those varying columns from most black cells on the left to fewest on the right.
85c4e7cd: The input is a set of concentric 1-cell-thick rectangular/square layers, each layer having a uniform color. The output keeps exactly the same nested geometry, but reverses the color order from outside to inside: the innermost layer’s color is moved to the outer border, the next innermost to the next layer out, and so on. If there is a middle layer, it stays in the middle but still participates in the reversed ordering.
85fa5666: Each red 2×2 block stays fixed and acts as a center. The four single colored cells sitting diagonally around that block (upper-left, upper-right, lower-left, lower-right) have their colors rotated one corner clockwise: NW→NE, NE→SE, SE→SW, SW→NW. Then from each corner, that new color is extended outward along the corresponding diagonal ray away from the red block, filling empty cells until the grid edge or another nonzero cell blocks it.
8618d23e: Split the input grid into a top half and a bottom half at the horizontal midpoint. Extend the top half by adding a new rightmost column of 9s, extend the bottom half by adding a new leftmost column of 9s, and insert between them a full horizontal row of 9s. Thus the output is one row taller and one column wider than the input.
868de0fa: The input contains one or more hollow shapes made of color 1 that are perfect square frames. Each square’s interior is filled uniformly while leaving the border unchanged: squares with odd side length are filled with color 7, and squares with even side length are filled with color 2. All non-square objects and background cells remain as they are.
8719f442: The 3×3 input is treated as a blueprint on a 5×5 lattice of 3×3 blocks, producing a 15×15 output. For each cell containing 5 in the input, place a solid 3×3 block of 5s in the correspondingly shifted block position around the center of the large grid; then, if that 5 is not on the top row and/or left column of the input, also place one or two full copies of the original 3×3 pattern in the adjacent block positions that connect that block back toward the center. The result is a larger composite figure made of solid 5-blocks and repeated copies of the original motif, arranged according to the positions of the input’s 5s.
8731374e: Find the largest contiguous monochrome object in the noisy input; it is a solid-colored rectangle with a few single-cell intrusions of another color, and this rectangle is cropped out to become the output. Then each non-background cell inside that rectangle is expanded into a full horizontal and full vertical line across the cropped rectangle, using the intruding color, while all remaining cells stay the rectangle’s original color. Thus the output is the extracted rectangle turned into a stripe/grid pattern whose marked rows and columns come from the anomalous cells.
878187ab: Ignore the positions of the scattered non-background pixels; only their counts matter. Count the two non-7 colors, let the smaller count be the pattern height and the larger count be the pattern width, then produce a fresh 16×16 grid of 7s with a bottom-left rectangle of that size filled with 2s. Inside that rectangle, replace cells by 4 to draw two symmetric diagonals starting near the top center and spreading outward one step per row, merging into a single center cell when the width difference requires it.
87ab05b8: The input is a 4×4 grid divided into four 2×2 quadrants, with one cell colored 2 somewhere among other distracting colors. The output ignores all other colors, keeps a background of 6, and expands the quadrant containing the 2-cell into a solid 2×2 block of color 2. Thus, the single red marker indicates which quadrant should be highlighted.
880c1354: The grid contains a fixed central structure made of 4s and 7s, while the non-4/non-7 colors occupy the outer corner regions. The transformation leaves the 4/7 pattern unchanged and cyclically reassigns the corner-region colors according to the four corners: each corner color is replaced by the color from the previous occupied corner in clockwise order around the grid. In effect, the corner/background colors rotate one step around the corners while the central 4-and-7 scaffold stays the same.
88207623: Each red vertical segment (color 2) acts as a mirror axis. On one side of that axis there is a shape made of color 4 cells, and on the other side there is a single isolated marker cell of some other color; the output reflects the 4-shape across the red axis onto the marker’s side, recoloring the reflected copy with the marker’s color. Existing cells are preserved, so the red axis, the original 4-shape, and the marker dot all remain unchanged.
8886d717: The grid contains a border line of 9s on one outer edge, which defines a single “away from the 9s” direction. Each 8 is examined: if it touches any 2-cell orthogonally, it is erased and turned into a 2; otherwise it stays and is copied one step farther away from the 9-border, creating a 2-cell streak of 8s in that direction. Thus, 8s attached to the 2-region are absorbed into it, while isolated 8s in the 7-region grow by one cell away from the 9 edge.
88a10436: Find the single non-background object with more than one cell, and copy that whole colored shape without rotating or reflecting it. Place the copy so that the center of the shape’s bounding box lands on the lone gray cell (color 5), causing that gray cell to be overwritten if covered. The original shape stays where it is.
88a62173: Look at the four 2×2 corner blocks of the 5×5 input: upper-left, upper-right, lower-left, and lower-right. Three of these corner patterns are identical and one is different. The output is exactly the odd one out, copied as a 2×2 grid with the same colors and orientation.
890034e9: Find the one-color rectangular frame already present in the grid: it is a hollow rectangle whose interior is all black (0). Use that framed black pattern as a template, then search the grid for another occurrence of the same black interior shape without the frame. Draw the same colored rectangular border around that matching black region, leaving everything else unchanged.
891232d6:
Each bottom-row 6 acts as a source of an upward-moving path. The path climbs vertically, marking empty cells as 2, until it meets a horizontal run of 7s that includes its column; at that hit point the struck 7 becomes 8, the cell below it becomes 4, the row below the bar is filled rightward with 2s ending in a 3, and a new vertical path continues upward from the bar’s right end.

If the bar is immediately blocked underneath by another 7 before that right end, the bounce does not happen; instead the cell just below the hit is marked 6 and that branch stops. Repeating this for every source traces a staircase-like chain of vertical rises and rightward deflections through the 7-bars.
896d5239: The green 3s mark the vertices of right isosceles triangles whose sides run along the two diagonals of the grid. For each such triangle, the output fills its interior with light blue 8s, including the horizontal or vertical “base strip” between the two diagonal sides, while leaving the green corner/edge cells themselves unchanged. All other cells stay as they were.
8a004b2b: The four cells of color 4 mark the corners of a rectangular frame; the output is exactly that framed rectangle, cropped from the input. Inside the frame are some solid same-sized color blocks, while outside the frame there is a small multicolor “template” using those same colors. Reconstruct the full interior pattern by enlarging that small template so each template cell becomes one block of the interior block size, aligning it with the existing blocks, and keep the 4-corner markers unchanged.
8a371977: The blue/1 cells form a framework that divides the black/0 cells into separate rectangular blocks (sometimes single cells) arranged in a regular grid. Recolor each black block by its position in that block-grid: blocks on the outer border of the arrangement become 2, while blocks strictly inside become 3. All 1s stay unchanged.
8a6d367c: The input contains one large framed rectangle and several small “hint” shapes elsewhere. The output is just that framed rectangle, but its interior is re-filled using the one small hint shape whose upscaled silhouette exactly matches the interior’s non-background pattern. That matching hint’s color is changed to the rectangle’s interior color, scaled up to the rectangle’s cell size, and placed inside the original border.
8abad3cf:
Ignore the background color 7 and look at the other colors by frequency. The rarest non-7 color forms a solid \(n \times n\) square in the output on the right, where \(n\) is determined by how many times that color appears.  

On the left, build another \(n\)-row panel on a 7 background: place the next non-7 color as its own square embedded near the lower-left, and if there is a third non-7 color, mark a single cell of that color at the top-left of the embedded square. Then concatenate this left panel with the solid square on the right.
8b28cd80: The 3×3 input contains a single nonzero cell; its color is copied to the output, and its position determines where a fixed 9×9 spiral is centered. The output is a 9×9 black grid with a one-cell-thick square spiral of that color, starting at the spiral’s center and winding outward counterclockwise (up, left, down, right, …). The spiral is translated so that its center sits at the 9×9 location corresponding to the input cell’s offset from the center of the 3×3 grid.
8ba14f53: Identify each non-black shape and count the number of black cells it encloses as interior holes. Then, in left-to-right order of the shapes, create a 3×3 output and fill it row by row with each shape’s color repeated that many times. Any remaining cells in the 3×3 grid stay black.
8be77c9e: The output keeps the input grid unchanged as its top half, then adds a vertically flipped copy of the same grid directly underneath. In other words, the rows of the input are repeated in reverse order below the original, doubling the height while keeping the width the same. This creates a reflection across a horizontal line at the join between the two halves.
8cb8642d:
Each non-background object is a solid rectangle filled with one main color and containing a single cell of a different color. In the output, the rectangle keeps its outer border in the main color, while its interior is cleared to black.

Then, inside that hollow rectangle, draw a centered X/diamond-like pattern connecting the interior corners toward the middle, using the color of the lone odd cell. Apply this independently to every such rectangle in the grid.
8d5021e8: The input pattern is expanded into a larger symmetric tile: place a left-right mirror image of the input immediately to its left, so the output is twice as wide and the original appears on the right half. Then repeat this doubled-width tile three times vertically, making the final grid three times as tall as the input. Colors and internal structure are preserved exactly.
8d510a79: Find the single full-width gray row, which acts as a divider. Each red/orange cell (2) grows vertically toward that gray row, stopping just before it, while each blue cell (1) grows vertically away from the gray row, continuing to the nearest outer edge of the grid. The original cells and the gray divider remain in place; the result is a set of vertical color trails in the same columns.
8dab14c2: The non-background color forms a thick L/cross-like shape made from one horizontal bar and one vertical bar. The output first infers the full ideal bars by taking the rows with unusually many colored cells as the horizontal bar and the columns with unusually many colored cells as the vertical bar, then filling their union as two solid rectangles. Finally, any mismatches between the original shape and this completed cross are “projected” to the opposite side of the bar they touch: holes inside the bars create matching protrusions just outside the bar, and extra protrusions outside the bars create matching holes inside.
8dae5dfc: Each nonzero connected object is a set of concentric rectangular layers (nested frames or a filled center). For each such object, keep the geometry and position exactly the same but reverse the color sequence from outside to inside: the outermost layer takes the innermost color, the next layer takes the next-inner color, and so on. Background 0 cells remain unchanged.
8e1813be: Find the non-background color objects that are just single-cell-thick lines (ignoring the large block of 5s and the 0 background). If these lines are vertical, list their colors from left to right; if they are horizontal, list their colors from top to bottom. Then make a square whose side length equals the number of such lines, filling it with those colors in order: vertical-line tasks become repeated identical rows, and horizontal-line tasks become repeated identical columns after transposition (equivalently, one solid-colored row per line).
8e2edd66: The 3×3 input is expanded into a 9×9 output viewed as a 3×3 array of 3×3 blocks. First, form the color-inverse of the input by swapping black (0) with the single nonzero color. Then, for each input cell that is black, place this inverted 3×3 pattern into the corresponding block of the output; for each nonblack input cell, leave the corresponding block all black.
8e301a54: The grid contains three special objects on a 7-background: a single 5, a shape made of 2s, and a shape made of 9s. The 5 is moved to one row below its original position, and the 2-shape and 9-shape are shifted vertically so that they become stacked together around the 5, with the taller of the two shapes positioned to overlap the other’s vertical span as much as possible. The shapes’ horizontal positions and internal forms stay unchanged; only their vertical placement is adjusted, and everything else is reset to background.
8e5a5113: The grid is split into three 3×3 panels by two vertical separator columns of color 5. The left panel contains the given pattern, while the middle and right panels are empty in the input. In the output, the left panel stays unchanged, the middle panel is filled with the left pattern rotated 90° clockwise, and the right panel is filled with the same pattern rotated 180° from its original orientation.
8eb1be9a: Take the entire non-background figure as a vertical motif, preserving its exact row order and column pattern. Then repeat that motif upward and downward in steps equal to its own height so the same rows reappear periodically across the whole grid, filling all empty space. If the grid height is not a multiple of the motif height, the repetition is simply truncated at the top or bottom edge.
8ee62060: The grid is split into 2-row bands, and these bands are reordered in reverse from bottom to top while each 2-row band itself stays unchanged. Visually, this makes the repeated small motif that was stepping along one diagonal (top-right to bottom-left) now step along the opposite diagonal (top-left to bottom-right), without changing the motif’s internal colors or shape.
8efcae92: The input contains several separate rectangular patches made only of colors 1 and 2 on a 0 background. Choose the patch with the largest bounding rectangle area; if multiple patches have the same size, pick the one containing the most cells of the rarer non-background color (the 2s). Output that selected patch cropped tightly, preserving its internal 1/2 pattern.
8f2ea7aa: Treat the 9×9 grid as a 3×3 array of equal-sized blocks, where the input contains one colored pattern occupying exactly one block. Copy that block-pattern, unchanged, into every block position obtained by stepping through the block grid in jumps equal to the pattern’s own block size (so a 3×3 pattern in a 9×9 grid is repeated every 3 rows and 3 columns). The result is a tiling of the same motif at those regularly spaced block positions, clipped by the grid boundaries.
8fbca751: Find each separate sky-blue (8) shape and treat all 8-cells that line up through shared rows or shared columns as belonging to one larger shape. For each such shape, take its smallest axis-aligned bounding rectangle and fill every missing cell inside that rectangle with red (2), while leaving the original 8s unchanged. Cells outside these completed rectangles stay the same.
8fff9e47:
The input grid is split into four interleaved subsequences based on cell parity: cells at even/even positions, even/odd positions, odd/even positions, and odd/odd positions. Each subsequence is treated as a 1D list and expanded into a square triangular “corner fill” pattern inside one quadrant of a larger square output, with the four parity groups placed in the top-left, top-right, bottom-left, and bottom-right quadrants respectively.

The output is always a square of size \(2 \times \max(\text{height}, \text{width})\). Within each quadrant, the color at a position depends only on its distance from that quadrant’s nearest outer corner, so the parity-group list appears as nested diagonal/L-shaped bands radiating inward from that corner.
902510d5: The grid contains one large multi-cell pattern and several isolated single cells. Keep the large pattern unchanged, discard the other stray singletons, and use the color that appears most among those discarded singletons to draw a right-triangular wedge in the corner occupied by one of them. The chosen corner determines the triangle’s orientation, and the triangle has as many rows/columns as there were discarded singleton cells, with lengths 1,2,3,… growing inward from that corner.
90347967: The colored pattern is anchored by the single **5**, which stays fixed in place. All other nonzero cells are moved to the opposite side of the 5 by a **180° point reflection** around that 5: each cell keeps its color, but its offset from the 5 is reversed in both row and column directions. The rest of the grid becomes 0.
90c28cc7: The input contains a rectangular mosaic of nonzero-colored uniform blocks on a black background. The task compresses this mosaic into a tiny grid by replacing each block-sized tile position with a single cell of the same color, preserving the row/column arrangement. If a block spans multiple tile positions, its color is repeated across those positions in the output.
90f3ed37: The grid contains one widest/topmost light-blue (8) pattern that serves as a template. Lower down, there are shorter copies of this pattern aligned the same way; each such partial copy is completed to the template’s full width/shape, but only the missing cells are added and they are colored dark blue (1) instead of light blue. Existing 8s stay unchanged, and background/zero positions from the template remain zero.
9110e3c5: The input is reduced to a symbolic 3×3 icon based on which nonzero color is most common in the 7×7 grid. That dominant color is always one of 1, 2, or 3, and each of these three colors maps to its own fixed arrangement of cyan (8) cells on a black background: color 1 gives the pattern `008/880/080`, color 2 gives `000/888/000`, and color 3 gives `088/080/080`. All other input details are ignored.
913fb3ed: Each isolated colored cell is expanded into a 3×3 block centered on itself. The center keeps its original color, while its eight surrounding neighbors are filled with a color determined by that center: 3 gets a ring of 6s, 8 gets a ring of 4s, and 2 gets a ring of 1s. All other cells remain unchanged.
91413438: Treat the 3×3 input as a tile. Count its black cells \(B\) and its colored (nonzero) cells \(C\); make a square output that is \(B\) tiles wide and \(B\) tiles tall, so its size is \((3B)\times(3B)\). Then place exactly \(C\) copies of the original 3×3 tile into this block grid, filling block positions from left to right across the top row, then continuing row by row; all remaining blocks stay all black.
91714a58: The input contains many scattered colored cells plus one large connected non-background object. Within that object, a repeated 1×3 color pattern appears in either horizontal form `[bg, objcolor, bg]` or its vertical transpose, marking a solid rectangular block of the object’s main color. The output keeps only that rectangular block in its original position and turns everything else to the background color.
9172f3a0: The task uniformly magnifies the entire input grid by a factor of 3. Each original cell is replaced by a 3×3 solid block of the same color, including black/background cells, so the layout stays identical but at triple width and height.
917bccba: The input contains two non-background colors: one forms a hollow rectangular frame, and the other forms a full vertical and horizontal line crossing through it. The transformation moves this cross so that its intersection is relocated to the frame’s upper-right corner: the vertical line is redrawn on the frame’s right edge column and the horizontal line on the frame’s top edge row. The frame itself stays unchanged and takes priority wherever the moved cross would overlap it.
928ad970: The grid contains four isolated gray marker cells (color 5) around a smaller colored rectangle/frame. Using the color of that inner shape, draw a larger hollow rectangle whose sides sit exactly one cell inside the outermost rows and columns occupied by the gray markers. Keep the original inner shape and the gray markers unchanged.
92e50de0: The picture is a regular lattice of 3×3 cells separated by 1-pixel grid lines. One cell contains a small motif in the rarest color; that exact motif is copied into every other cell in a checkerboard pattern, specifically all cells with the same row/column parity as the original motif’s cell. The grid lines and all untouched cells remain unchanged.
9344f635: The grid contains small non-background objects on a background of 7. Every 2-cell horizontal segment is expanded to fill its entire row with that segment’s color, and every 2-cell vertical segment is expanded to fill its entire column with that segment’s color. The output is otherwise just the background, with these full-row and full-column stripes overlaid where the corresponding 2-cell line pieces appeared in the input.
9356391f: The top row is a color key: read its entries from left to right up to the rightmost nonzero cell, including any 0s in between.
