Around the single nonzero cell below the solid gray row, draw concentric square rings centered on that cell, using those top-row values in order: the seed cell is the first color, the next value colors the 1-cell-thick ring at distance 1, the next value colors the ring at distance 2, etc.; if a key value is 0, that ring is left blank/background. Everything else stays the same, except that if the top-row cell directly above the seed’s column is nonzero, it is replaced by gray (5).
93b4f4b3:
The input is split into two equal vertical halves. In the left half, a solid background contains several black hole-shapes; in the right half, those same shapes appear as colored connected components on a black background.

Match each colored shape on the right to the black hole on the left with the exact same geometry, and fill that left-side hole with the shape’s color. The output is just the completed left half, with the entire right half discarded.
93b581b8: The input contains a single 2×2 nonzero block on a zero background, and that original block stays unchanged. From each corner of this 2×2 block, add a monochrome 2×2 square extending outward in the same diagonal direction: upper-left gets the original lower-right color, upper-right gets the original lower-left color, lower-left gets the original upper-right color, and lower-right gets the original upper-left color. If any added square would go off the grid, only the in-bounds cells are drawn.
93c31fbe: The nonzero color other than 1 forms several rectangular “corner-frame” boxes; keep these frame shapes unchanged. Remove all 1-cells that are outside such a box, and within each box take the existing 1 pattern and reflect it across the box’s longer central axis (horizontal reflection if the box is taller than wide, vertical reflection if it is wider than tall), adding the mirrored 1s while keeping the originals. Thus each framed region ends up containing a symmetric 1-pattern, with stray 1s elsewhere erased.
94133066:
Find the rectangular patch whose background is color 1 and contains the main pattern. Elsewhere in the black background are a few isolated single-cell colored markers; these have the same colors as some cells inside the rectangle and indicate the desired orientation.

Output just that rectangle, but rotated/reflected so that its four corner-marker colors match the arrangement implied by the isolated outside markers. In other words, choose the symmetry of the rectangular pattern that makes the colored cells inside line up with the same-colored reference dots outside, then crop away the rest of the grid.
941d9a10: The gray 5-cells form full horizontal and vertical separator lines that partition the black background into rectangular empty compartments. The output colors exactly three of these compartments: the top-left compartment becomes color 1, the unique central compartment becomes color 2, and the bottom-right compartment becomes color 3. All separator lines and all other cells remain unchanged.
94414823: Find the hollow square border made of color 5 and leave it unchanged. The two lone colored cells outside the square provide the two fill colors for the square’s empty 4×4 interior, which is split into four 2×2 quadrants and filled as a checkerboard: one color goes in the top-left and bottom-right quadrants, the other in the top-right and bottom-left. Which color gets the TL/BR diagonal is determined by the outside markers’ relative positions: the marker that is on the same side of the square as the top-left interior quadrant supplies that diagonal.
9473c6fb: The grid is mostly a single background color, and the output keeps that background everywhere. All non-background cells are taken in a single sorted sequence by position: if their row indices are all distinct, sort them top-to-bottom; otherwise sort them left-to-right. Then recolor the 1st, 4th, 7th, … marked cells to 2, the 2nd, 5th, 8th, … to 8, and the 3rd, 6th, 9th, … to 5, leaving their locations unchanged.
94be5b80: The input contains two kinds of objects: a small 3-cell-high row of vertical color bars, and one or more larger copies of the same shape in some of those colors. Read the bar colors from left to right; that ordered list tells which colors should appear in the final stack. The output redraws the common shape once for each listed color, stacked vertically in that order with no gaps between copies, and removes the original color-bar key.
94f9d214: The 8×4 input is made of two stacked 4×4 layers: an upper pattern in green (3) on black, and a lower pattern in blue (1) on black. The output is a single 4×4 grid that marks in red (2) exactly those cells that are black in both layers at the same position. All other cells are black, so the output highlights the overlap of the two layers’ empty spaces.
952a094c: The input contains a hollow rectangular frame of one color, with four colored cells placed at the rectangle’s four inner corners. In the output, those four inner-corner cells are erased, while the frame stays unchanged. Each removed color is moved to the cell diagonally outside the opposite corner of the rectangle, so the four colors appear outside the frame in a 180°-rotated arrangement.
9565186b: The output keeps only the color that occupies the most cells in the input, preserving those cells in their original positions. Every other cell is replaced with gray (color 5), so the result is a gray grid with just the most frequent color’s pattern left visible.
95755ff2: The grid contains a diamond/X-shaped boundary of 2s, and any nonzero, non-2 colors placed on the outer border are extended straight inward from that border toward the center. A color on the top edge grows downward in its column, one on the bottom edge grows upward, one on the left edge grows rightward, and one on the right edge grows leftward. Each extension fills only empty cells and stops when it hits an existing nonzero cell, so the 2-frame and any interior colored cells act as blockers and remain unchanged.
95990924: Copy the input grid and treat each gray connected object (here, each 2×2 block of color 5) as a square to be decorated. Add one cell diagonally outside each of its four corners: color 1 at the upper-left, color 2 at the upper-right, color 3 at the lower-left, and color 4 at the lower-right. Leave the gray blocks and all other cells unchanged, ignoring any added marker that would fall outside the grid.
95a58926: The input contains a noisy lattice made from gray (5) rows and columns, plus one other scattered color. The output reconstructs only that lattice: cells on the detected gray rows or gray columns stay gray, all other cells become black. At every crossing of a lattice row and lattice column, the gray cell is replaced by the single nonzero, non-gray color from the input, and all other occurrences of that color are removed.
963c33f8: The grid contains a small 3-row pattern of 9s and 1s near the top; this pattern is removed from its original location and reinserted lower in the grid, column by column. For each column of that top pattern, place its three cells vertically so they “hang” just above or just below the corresponding 5-shape in that same column range: if the column contains a 1, attach it above the first substantial 5-object encountered below; if it contains only 9s, attach it below, aligned to the next relevant 5-object. The 5-shapes themselves stay unchanged, except that any 5 directly underneath an inserted vertical strip may be cleared to 7 to make room.
963e52fc: The output keeps the same height but doubles the grid’s width. Any nonzero horizontal motif in the input is treated as a repeating pattern and continued seamlessly to the right until the row reaches the new doubled width, while all-zero rows simply remain zero across the wider canvas. In other words, the picture is stretched horizontally by periodic continuation, not by inserting blanks or mirroring.
963f59bc: The color-1 shape is the template and stays unchanged. Each other colored object is just a single “seed” cell; it grows into a full copy of the template in that seed’s color, with the seed occupying the corresponding cell of the new copy. If the seed lies to the right of the template, the copy is a left-right mirror of the template; if the seed lies below the template, the copy is an up-down mirror of the template.
96a8c0cd: Starting from the single red/orange seed cell (color 2) on the border, extend a continuous 2-colored path straight inward across the grid. Whenever the path’s next step would hit a green bar/object (color 3), it detours on the “lower/right” side of that obstacle; whenever it would hit a blue bar/object (color 1), it detours on the “upper/left” side, hugging the obstacle until it can continue straight again. The result is a zigzagging path of 2s that keeps going until it exits or reaches the far edge, while leaving the 1s and 3s in place except where the path may overwrite 1s it passes through.
9720b24f: The grid contains one or more non-background shapes that act as enclosing outlines or barriers. For each such colored shape, look at the cells strictly inside the area it encloses (the holes/interior bounded by that same color along rows and columns) and erase any nonzero cells there that are not the shape’s own color, turning them into background 0. Cells outside the enclosed interior, or cells of the enclosing color itself, are left unchanged.
97239e3d: The grid contains a regular array of blue 3×3 “rings” separated by black rows and columns. For each non-black, non-blue color, take all cells of that color as markers, find their bounding box, and expand it to the surrounding separator lines of the 4-cell lattice. Then draw that color as the rectangular frame on those separator lines, and recolor the center cell of every blue ring inside that framed region to the same color.
973e499e:
The input is treated as a blueprint for a self-referential enlargement: an \(n \times n\) input becomes an \(n^2 \times n^2\) output, divided into \(n \times n\) blocks of size \(n \times n\). For each input cell, place in its corresponding block a copy of the entire set of positions occupied by that same color in the original grid, recolored with that cell’s color; all other cells in the block stay 0.  

So each color defines its own binary pattern from the original grid, and every occurrence of that color expands into a block showing that pattern.
9772c176:
Each connected light-blue (8) blob is a truncated diamond: a wide central body with its four cardinal tips missing. The output keeps the 8-shape unchanged and adds yellow (4) cells outside it to complete those missing tips on the top, bottom, left, and right.

These added 4-cells form centered triangular/diamond caps that taper outward, so the combined 8+4 figure becomes a full diamond; if a cap would go beyond the grid, it is simply clipped.
97999447: Each isolated non-background cell acts as a seed for a horizontal stripe on its row. Starting at that cell and continuing to the right edge, the row is filled with an alternating pattern of the seed’s original color and gray (5), beginning with the original color at the seed position. All other cells remain unchanged.
97a05b5b: Find the large rectangular region made of color 2; this becomes the output canvas, cropped to its bounding box. Inside it, the 0-cells are “slots” whose shapes match the color-2 cells of the small multicolored pieces elsewhere in the input. For each small piece, rotate/reflect it as needed so its 2-pattern fits one slot pattern, then place it there: its 2-cells merge into the 2 background and its non-2 colors appear in the output.
97c75046: The grid contains a single misplaced 5, a background of 7s, and one connected 0-shaped region. Move the 5 so that it sits on the outer boundary of the 0-region at the “front tip” reached by starting from the side of the 0-shape nearest the original 5, going straight toward the shape until just before entering it, then sliding along the shape’s edge to the endpoint/corner of that exposed side. The original 5 cell is restored to 7.
981add89: The top row is a template for vertical stripes. For each column, extend the top-row color straight downward through the whole grid, but only in cells that are either the background color or already that same color; cells of other colors are left unchanged. Equivalently, each non-background mark in the first row paints a full-height vertical line of its color, carving through same-colored regions and background while not overwriting unrelated colored blocks.
9841fdad: The grid has two interior panels separated by vertical full-height divider lines. The left panel contains colored shapes on a plain background, while the right panel is initially blank/uniform; the task copies each non-background shape from the left panel into the right panel on the same rows, preserving its color and its horizontal role within the panel. In other words, a shape touching the left side of the left panel is redrawn against the left side of the right panel, one touching the right side is redrawn against the right side, and one spanning the full usable width is stretched across the full usable width of the right panel.
984d8a3e:
Each grid has three colors arranged as a left block, a middle block, and a right block. The left-edge color and the right-edge color stay as the two side colors, while the middle color acts like a movable band between them.

Row by row, some cells are transferred from the left block to the right side of the middle band so that the middle band’s right boundary is aligned across rows as far left as possible. Visually, this “slides” excess cells of the left color through the middle region and reappears them at the middle/right interface, while the far-right background region remains unchanged.
985ae207: The grid contains small two-color “seed” motifs, usually a 3×3 block whose outer color is the dominant color and whose center is a different color, plus separate solid monochrome bars/rectangles elsewhere. For each seed, find the solid object whose color matches the seed’s minority/center color, and extend the seed straight toward that object (left, right, up, or down), filling the gap with a rectangular bridge of the seed’s outer color. Along the bridge’s center row or column, repeat the seed’s center-color mark every third cell, preserving the original seed and target object.
98c475bf: The grid keeps only the side border color and any colors that appear as a matched pair of single cells on the same row near the left and right sides. Each such paired color acts as a label telling you to draw that color’s fixed template shape at that row, spanning across the interior. Any pre-existing interior shape is erased; the output is just the preserved border plus the template(s) for the paired marker color(s).
98cf29f8: The input contains two non-background colored objects: one is a solid rectangle that stays fixed, and the other is a solid block with a thin 1-cell-wide “stem” or connector reaching toward the first object. The transformation removes that thin connector and slides the block part straight along the connector’s direction until it sits directly adjacent to the fixed rectangle. The moved block keeps its original shape and color; only the connector disappears and the gap is closed.
992798f6: The input contains exactly two marked cells, one red (2) and one blue (1). In the output, these two cells stay unchanged and a green (3) path is added between them: it starts from the diagonally adjacent cell next to the upper marker, heads toward the lower marker, using diagonal steps as long as possible, then continues in a single straight horizontal or vertical segment. The path stops one cell before the lower marker, so the green line connects the two markers without overwriting either endpoint.
99306f82: Find the hollow rectangle outlined in color 1 and the sequence of single colored cells placed on the main diagonal from the top-left corner up to that frame. Fill the inside of the rectangle with concentric 1-cell-thick rectangular layers using those diagonal colors in order: the first diagonal color becomes the outermost inner ring, the next color the next ring inward, and so on. If the color list reaches the center, the last color fills the remaining central area.
995c5fa3:
The 4×14 input is split by two full-height black separator columns into three 4×4 panels. In each panel, the arrangement of black cells on the gray background encodes one of four symbols: blank panel → 2, centered 2×2 black block → 8, bottom-centered 2×2 black block → 4, and a vertically mirrored pair of side blocks (left-side pair or right-side pair) → 3.  

The output is a 3×3 grid with one solid-color row per panel, in left-to-right order: the left panel determines the top row, the middle panel the middle row, and the right panel the bottom row.
9968a131: Identify the dominant background color and look at the rows containing the other colors. Rows whose non-background cells appear in the same columns are left unchanged; any row whose two non-background colors are the same pair but shifted one step left is corrected by shifting that whole 3-cell segment one step right. Visually, a row like `[a, b, bg]` becomes `[bg, a, b]`, while all other cells stay the same.
996ec1f3: The input contains one full-width horizontal separator row and one full-height vertical separator column, both in the same solid color, splitting the grid into four quadrants. The output is a 3×3 summary: the center row and center column are filled with the separator color, and the four corners are the dominant/fill colors of the upper-left, upper-right, lower-left, and lower-right quadrants respectively. In other words, it compresses the four large regions plus their dividing cross into a miniature 3×3 map.
99b1bc43: The input contains two same-sized pattern panels separated by a solid divider line of color 4; in the examples this is a horizontal split into a top 4×4 blue/black panel and a bottom 4×4 red/black panel. Compare the two panels cell by cell and produce a single panel of the same size: color a cell green (3) if exactly one panel has a colored mark there, and leave it black (0) if both panels agree by being both empty or both filled. In other words, the output is the cellwise XOR of the two non-black masks.
99caaf76: The non-8 cells form separate composite objects, each containing some cells of color 1 and some cells of other colors. For each object, the 1-cells act like an anchor: the rest of the object is turned upside down (rotated 180° within its own bounding box), and the whole object is then slid in the direction from the 1-part toward the non-1 part until it reaches the corresponding edge of the grid. The output is the collection of these shifted objects on an otherwise all-8 background.
99fa7670: Each non-background colored cell becomes the corner of an L-shape. Its color is extended horizontally from its position to the right edge of the grid, and then continued downward from the far-right end along the last column. If several such L-shapes are present, the downward part only fills empty cells, so it stops when it reaches a cell already occupied by another color.
9a4bb226: The input contains several separate non-black connected patches, each forming a small rectangular motif on the black background. Count how many different colors each patch uses, select the patch with the greatest color variety, and output just that patch cropped to its bounding box. In these examples, this is the unique 3×3 block containing three colors while the others contain only two.
9aec4887: The input contains two parts: a hollow rectangular “template” whose four sides are different colors, and a separate shape made of color 8. The output is the template’s bounding box, with the 8-shape inserted into it: place the 8 pattern so its top-left aligns one cell inside the template’s top-left corner, and keep the colored border wherever the 8s would touch or cross it. In other words, the result is the colored frame/window with the detached 8-pattern overlaid into its interior.
9af7a82c: The input’s colored cells are reorganized into a vertical histogram by color frequency. Count how many times each color appears, sort the colors from most common to least common, and make an output grid whose height is the largest count and whose width is the number of distinct colors. Each column corresponds to one color and is filled from the top downward for exactly that many cells; any remaining cells below are black (0).
9b2a60aa: Find the one nontrivial multi-cell shape; it is the template to copy. The other colored cells are isolated 1-pixel markers arranged in a single row or a single column, and their order and gaps encode a sequence of copies: make one copy of the template for each marker, recolor it with that marker’s color, and place the copies in a straight line perpendicular to the marker line. The spacing between successive copied shapes matches the spacing between successive markers, while the original template and the marker pixels remain unchanged.
9b30e358: Identify the contiguous block of non-background rows at the bottom of the input; this is the motif to propagate. If that block has an odd number of rows, repeat it vertically unchanged to fill the whole grid; if it has an even number of rows, first rotate its row order by half a block (move the lower half above the upper half), then repeat that reordered block to fill the full height.
9b365c51: The grid has a set of full-height colored guide columns on the left and several separate light-blue (8) shapes on the right. Read the guide-column colors from left to right, then recolor the right-side 8-shapes in that same left-to-right order, preserving each shape’s exact footprint. Finally, erase the original guide columns by turning them to background 0.
9b4c17c4: The grid is split into two uniform background regions of colors 1 and 8, either side-by-side or top-and-bottom. In each row, the red cells (2) are horizontally packed within their own background region: inside color-8 area they slide to the left edge of that region, and inside color-1 area they slide to the right edge of that region. The number of red cells in each row and each region is preserved, but their original horizontal positions are discarded in favor of edge-alignment.
9b5080bb:
The grid contains one large connected shape in a non-background color, with holes/cut-ins made of two other colors. For each such large shape, infer its “regular” outer contour (the dominant left, right, top, and bottom edges of the shape), and whenever an interior row or column has a one-cell indentation from that contour, mark that notch by placing a 2-cell line in the opposite neighboring color.  

In effect, the output highlights inward dents of a large colored region: if the region is mostly bordered by color A and touches color B on the relevant side, the dent is filled/flagged with color B (and vice versa), while the rest of the grid stays unchanged.
9ba4a9aa: The grid contains several special 3×3 “ring” blocks: eight cells of one color surrounding a different center color. It also contains a sparse path/network of isolated colored cells that acts like a connector between these blocks. Starting from the unique checkerboard-like 3×3 marker, follow this connector path until it reaches one of the ring blocks; the output is that destination 3×3 ring block, copied exactly.
9bebae7a:
The grid contains a yellow shape (4) and a magenta marker shape (6). Remove the magenta shape, then add a reflected copy of the yellow shape directly adjacent to it: if the 6-shape is a vertical T, place a left/right mirror of the yellow on the side indicated by the T’s stem; if the 6-shape is a horizontal cross/plus, place an up/down mirror of the yellow on the side indicated by the longer arm.  

The original yellow shape stays in place, and the new copy is its mirror image touching the original across the corresponding outer edge.
9c1e755f: The gray/5 line acts as a scaffold for copying nearby patterns across the empty area it spans. If the 5s form a horizontal bar, then each colored cell directly above or below one end of that bar is stretched horizontally across the full bar width in its own row. If the 5s form a vertical bar, then any existing horizontal pattern on one side is repeated row-by-row along the full height of the bar, cycling through the available seed rows, while single colored cells on the other side are stretched sideways across that side’s width.
9c56f360: Treat each row independently. Any green cells (3) in a row slide as far left as possible through black cells, while blue cells (8) stay fixed and act as barriers: the green run ends up packed immediately to the right of the rightmost blue cell that lies to its left, or at the far left edge if there is no such blue cell. The number of green cells in the row is preserved, and their old positions become black.
9caba7c3: The red cells form small connected motifs that are almost a full 2×2 block: three red corners are present and one corner is missing. For each such motif, complete the missing corner with yellow (4), then expand it into the surrounding 3×3 “flower” by turning the other non-red cells of that 3×3 area orange (7). Original red cells stay red, and background cells outside these flower regions remain unchanged.
9caf5b84: The output keeps only the two colors that occur most often in the input grid, preserving them in their original positions. Every cell of any other color is changed to color 7. The grid shape and the placement of the two dominant colors stay unchanged.
9d9215db:
The input shows a partial pattern of nested square “frames” drawn on odd coordinates of the 19×19 grid, visible from just one corner or side. The output completes the full design by reflecting all given colored cells across the horizontal and vertical midlines, so the same motif appears in all four corners.

In addition, any color that appears as two same-row or same-column symmetric endpoints is extended into the corresponding dashed horizontal or vertical side of its square, filling every other cell between those endpoints. The result is a fully symmetric set of concentric, color-coded square borders.
9ddd00f0: The grid is partitioned into equal square blocks separated by 1-cell-wide black lines; the nonzero color is the same everywhere. The output completes the full block array: every block is filled with the foreground color except for one black cell, and the position of that black cell inside a block matches the block’s row and column index within the block grid. In other words, the input shows only some of these patterned blocks, and the output fills in all missing blocks to make the complete regular tiled pattern.
9def23fe: Find the solid rectangle of color 2 and treat every other nonzero, non-2 cell as a blocker. For each row of the rectangle, extend the 2s leftward and/or rightward all the way to the grid edge, but only on a side whose entire row segment outside the rectangle is empty; if any blocker exists on that side, that extension is omitted. Do the analogous thing for each column of the rectangle, extending upward and/or downward to the edge only when that whole outside column segment is clear. Blocker cells stay unchanged.
9dfd6313: The grid is square and has a fixed main diagonal of color 5 from top-left to bottom-right. All other colored cells, which appear only below/left of that diagonal, are reflected across the main diagonal to the symmetric positions above/right of it (swap row and column). The diagonal 5s stay unchanged, and the original lower-left colored cells are cleared to 0.
9edfc990: The grid contains scattered nonzero digits, with some cells already colored 1 and many empty 0-cells. Change every 0-cell that is edge-adjacent (up/down/left/right) to any existing 1-cell into a 1, while leaving all other cells unchanged. In effect, the color-1 region expands by one orthogonal step into neighboring empty cells.
9f236235: The input is a large grid partitioned by full-width/full-height separator lines of one uniform color, creating a regular array of rectangular cells. Each cell is either empty/background or filled uniformly with a single color; the task compresses this structure into a small grid with one output pixel per cell, using that cell’s interior color (or 0 if empty). Finally, the compressed grid is mirrored left-to-right.
9f27f097: The grid has a dominant background color, one rectangular block of black cells (0), and elsewhere a same-sized rectangular colored pattern made of the remaining non-background colors. Replace the black rectangle with a left-right mirror image of that colored pattern. Everything outside the black rectangle stays unchanged.
9f41bd9c:
The input contains a 5-wide gray motif above a solid magenta region: its top two rows are solid gray, and below that are alternating gray cells. In the output, this motif is recreated on the opposite side of the grid (left if it was on the right, right if it was on the left), with the top two solid rows kept fixed and the lower alternating rows continuing as a diagonal staircase, shifting one column per row toward the original side.

Where this diagonal progression reaches the top row of the magenta band, the magenta cells beyond that contact point are recolored to brown (to the right if the motif was moved left, or to the left if it was moved right). All other cells remain unchanged.
9f5f939b: The grid contains short horizontal 1-bars and short vertical 1-bars on an 8 background. A cell is changed from 8 to 4 exactly when it sits midway between two horizontal 1-bars in the same row and also midway between two vertical 1-bars in the same column, with a one-cell gap on each side (so the four surrounding 1-segments form a symmetric “plus” around that center). All other cells remain unchanged.
9f669b64: The grid contains three non-background objects arranged roughly in a line; the middle one is the “connector” between the other two. One of the two outer objects is selected as the movable small piece: if only one outer object touches the middle object, that touching one is chosen; if both touch, the smaller/thinner one is chosen. That chosen object is moved to the opposite side of the middle object, and the middle object is split into two halves that slide apart to make room for it, while the third object stays unchanged.
9f8de559: Inside the large block of 7s, a single 2 sits at one end of a short straight run of 6s; together they act like an arrow, with the 2 marking the tip and the 6s trailing behind. Extend from the 2 in the direction away from the 6s, continuing through any 7s until reaching the first non-7 cell, and recolor that one cell to 7. This creates a one-cell outward protrusion of the 7-region in the arrow’s pointing direction.
a04b2602: Find each red cell (2) that lies inside the bounding rectangle of a green connected component (3). Around every such red cell, color all eight neighboring cells blue (1), leaving the red cell itself unchanged. This can overwrite green or background cells, but red cells outside any green component’s rectangle are ignored.
a096bf4d: The grid is a lattice of identical framed tiles separated by black lines; only the 2×2 interior of each tile can vary. For each of the four interior positions independently, look across every row of tiles and every column of tiles: if the same non-background/non-default color appears at two tiles in that same slot, then all default-colored cells in that slot between those two matching endpoints are filled with that color. In other words, special interior colors are propagated straight through gaps between matching copies, while everything else stays unchanged.
a09f6c25: The grid contains several disconnected foreground shapes made of color 2, plus some isolated single 2-cells. The single-cell noise is removed, and each remaining multi-cell shape is recolored according to its symmetry: vertically symmetric shapes become color 3, horizontally symmetric shapes become color 1, and shapes with neither of those mirror symmetries become color 6. The background stays unchanged.
a1570a43: The grid contains two foreground colors: four fixed 3-cells marking the corners of a rectangle, and one 2-colored shape. The 3-cells stay where they are, while the entire 2-shape is translated without rotation so that its bounding box is centered inside the rectangle defined by the 3-corners, leaving a one-cell margin from that frame when possible. All cells where the 2-shape used to be are cleared to background.
a1aa0c1e: The input contains four full-width horizontal bars; the first three are colored “section labels” and the last one is always the separator color that reappears in the output. For each of the first three bars, look at all other cells of that same color elsewhere in the grid: they form a small 3-column motif made of alternating rows like `c0c` and `ccc`, and the output row records how many full `ccc` rows that motif has by writing that bar’s color 1, 2, or 3 times (padding the rest of the first three cells with 0; if there is no motif, all three are 0). The 4th column of every output row is the color of the bottom full-width bar, and the 5th column contains the lone `5` only on the row whose motif has the fewest full `ccc` rows.
a2d730bd: Each non-background color has one main multi-cell block and possibly some isolated single pixels of the same color. For every such singleton, extend the matching block toward it by drawing a 1-cell-thick “arm” from the nearest side of the block straight toward the singleton, and add short perpendicular caps at both ends so the result looks like a narrow H/T-shaped connector aligned with the block and the marker. The original isolated pixel itself is removed (turned back to background), while the new colored connector remains.
a2fd1cf0: The input contains one red cell (2) and one green cell (3) on a blank background. The output keeps those two cells unchanged and adds a sky-blue path (8) forming an L-shape: draw a horizontal segment along the red cell’s row until reaching the green cell’s column, then a vertical segment along that column until reaching the green cell. Only background cells on this orthogonal connector are filled.
a3325580: Find the non-background colored objects and compare their sizes by number of cells. Keep only the object(s) with the maximum size; for each such object, make a solid vertical bar of its color whose height equals that maximum size. Place these bars side by side in left-to-right order according to where the original objects appear in the input.
a3f84088: Find the gray (5) rectangular frame and keep it unchanged. Then fill its hollow interior with concentric one-cell-thick rectangular borders, moving inward one layer at a time, using the repeating color cycle **2, 5, 0, 5**. This creates nested rectangles inside the original frame until the center is reached; anything outside the frame stays as it was.
a406ac07: The bottom row serves as a horizontal color template, and the rightmost column labels each row by a color. For every row except the last, copy that row’s right-edge color into all columns where the same color appears in the bottom row; all other cells stay 0. Equivalently, each color’s run on the bottom row is repeated across every row whose rightmost cell has that color, while the bottom row and rightmost column remain unchanged.
a416b8f3: The output is made by placing a second identical copy of the entire input grid directly to its right. This keeps the height unchanged and doubles the width, with every row repeated exactly once side by side.
a416fc5b: The 11×11 grid is a 3×3 board of possible “plus” positions: rows 0–2, 4–6, 8–10 crossed with columns 0–2, 4–6, 8–10, separated by 6-colored divider lines. A single plus of color 2 marks one board cell; the output adds two more pluses in the two cells opposite it around the 8-position cycle of outer/middle locations, coloring one added plus 5 and the other 8. If the input already contains both colors 5 and 8 somewhere, then instead of modifying the board, the output is a solid square of 7s whose size equals the number of non-6, non-7 cells in the input.
a48eeaf7: The grid contains one solid rectangular colored block (the larger non-background object) and several isolated cells of another color. The block stays fixed, while each isolated cell is moved straight toward the block along the shortest row/column/diagonal direction until it lands on the nearest cell of the 1-cell-thick border surrounding the block. The original isolated-cell positions are erased, so the small cells end up packed onto the outer ring immediately around the main block.
a5313dff: Treat the red cells (color 2) as walls and find every non-red region that is completely enclosed, i.e. cannot reach the outer border of the grid. Recolor the black cells (0) in those trapped interior regions to blue (1). Leave the red structure unchanged, and leave any cells connected to the border unchanged.
a57f2f04: The grid contains one or more rectangular non-background blocks embedded in an 8-colored background. In each block, a small two-color motif appears in one corner; take the smallest rectangle containing that motif (including its 0-cells) as a tile, and repeat it periodically across the entire block. Everything outside those blocks stays unchanged.
a59b95c0: Count how many distinct colors appear in the input grid. Then make the output by tiling the entire input pattern that many times across and that many times down, so the result is an \(N \times N\) array of identical copies of the original grid, where \(N\) is the number of unique colors.
a5f85a15: The non-background cells form one or more continuous diagonals running down-right. In each such diagonal, keep the first cell unchanged and recolor every second cell after that (2nd, 4th, 6th, … along the diagonal) to yellow/4. All other cells stay the same.
a61ba2ce: The input contains four separate nonzero objects, each being a 3-cell L-shape occupying three cells of a 2×2 box. Build a 4×4 output by extracting those four 2×2 boxes and placing them in the four corners of the new grid according to which corner of the box is missing: missing bottom-right goes to the output’s top-left, missing bottom-left to top-right, missing top-right to bottom-left, and missing top-left to bottom-right. This preserves each shape’s color and orientation, and leaves the central 2×2 area as background (0).
a61f2674: The input contains several separate gray vertical bars on a black background. Keep only the tallest bar and the shortest bar: recolor the tallest gray bar to blue (1) and the shortest gray bar to red (2). Erase all other gray bars by turning them into background black.
a644e277: The input is a large mosaic made from three colors: a dominant background color, a rare “dot” color, and a separator color that forms grid-like dividing lines. Ignore the rare dot color by treating it as background, then find the rectangular region of separator-color cells that are true interior junction/line cells, i.e. separator cells surrounded on all four sides by background after that simplification. The output is the crop of the original image bounded by those interior separator cells, preserving the original colors inside that rectangle.
a64e4611: The grid contains a large orthogonal “empty corridor” of background cells (0) running through the picture, bounded by the non-background color. The output paints the interior of this corridor green (3): specifically, the inside of any sufficiently thick vertical or horizontal zero band is filled, while the outer boundary cells of the gap are left unchanged. If this main empty band connects to side/top/bottom zero arms that are also at least 3 cells thick, the fill extends through the interiors of those arms as well, producing a connected green cross/corridor shape inside the empty space.
a65b410d: The input contains a single horizontal bar of color 2 starting at the left edge; this bar stays unchanged. Above it, fill each row with a left-aligned bar of color 3 that gets 1 cell longer per row as you go upward. Below it, fill each row with a left-aligned bar of color 1 that gets 1 cell shorter per row as you go downward, stopping when the length would reach 0.
a680ac02: Identify the colored hollow square-frames in the input—objects whose bounding box contains a hole of background cells—and ignore the solid filled blocks. Extract those hollow squares unchanged and pack them together with no gaps in a single line. If the hollow squares are spread more by row than by column, stack them top-to-bottom in topmost-to-bottommost order; otherwise place them left-to-right in leftmost-to-rightmost order.
a68b268e: The central row and column of 1s split the 9×9 input into four 4×4 quadrants. The output is a single 4×4 composite made by aligning those quadrants and taking, at each position, the first non-black cell in this priority order: top-left quadrant first, then top-right, then bottom-left, then bottom-right. Equivalently, start with the top-left pattern and fill its 0-cells from the other three quadrants in that order.
a6953f00: The output is always a 2×2 block taken from the top of the input grid. If the grid’s width is odd, this block is the upper-left 2×2 corner; if the width is even, it is the upper-right 2×2 corner.
a699fb00: The grid is unchanged except for horizontal single-cell gaps between blue cells. In each row, whenever two blue cells (1) have exactly one background cell (0) between them, that middle cell is filled with red (2), so every `1-0-1` pattern becomes `1-2-1`. This also applies repeatedly in longer alternating runs like `1-0-1-0-1`.
a740d043: Find all cells that are not the background color (the dominant color, here 1), and crop the grid to the smallest rectangle that contains them all. In that cropped rectangle, keep the non-background colors unchanged, but turn any remaining background-colored cells into black (0). The result is the isolated pattern on a black background, with all surrounding empty border removed.
a78176bb: The input contains a colored NW–SE diagonal line and one or more gray triangular wedges attached to one side of it. Each gray wedge acts as a spacer: replace it by drawing a new full diagonal, parallel to the original and in the same color, on the far side of the wedge at a distance determined by the wedge’s size. Finally, erase all gray cells, leaving only the original diagonal plus the newly added parallel diagonal(s).
a79310a0: The input contains a single non-background shape made of color 8. In the output, that same shape is erased from its original location, copied one row lower, and recolored to 2. The shape’s geometry stays exactly the same; only its vertical position and color change.
a834deea:
The grid contains one or more nontrivial connected regions of 0s inside a background of 8s. For each such 0-region, look at its top-left corner and use that as the anchor of a fixed 3×3 label stencil:
top row = `1 7 6`, middle row = `4 0 5`, bottom row = `2 9 3`.

Overlay this 3×3 pattern starting one cell down and one cell right from that region’s top-left corner, but only write the labels into cells that are 0 in the input; any existing 8s in those positions stay unchanged. All other cells remain the same.
a85d4709: Each input row contains exactly one non-background cell (the 5), and its horizontal position encodes a color. Replace the entire row with a solid color based on that position: left column → 2, middle column → 4, right column → 3. The output keeps the same 3×3 shape, but every row becomes uniform.
a8610ef7: The input is a black-and-cyan grid where every cyan cell is recolored, while black cells stay unchanged. First imagine flipping the grid top-to-bottom: cyan cells that would still land on cyan after this vertical reflection are marked with color 2, and all other cyan cells become color 5. In other words, the output highlights the overlap between the cyan pattern and its upside-down version.
a87f7484: The input is a strip of non-overlapping 3×3 monochrome motifs, arranged either side-by-side or stacked vertically. Most of these 3×3 blocks share the same shape, while one block is the odd one out because its pattern is different and typically more filled. The output is simply that unique 3×3 outlier block copied exactly.
a8c38be5: The input contains nine separate 3×3 tiles on a black background, all mostly filled with color 5; eight tiles contain a small non-5 shape touching a particular side or corner, and one tile is entirely 5. The task extracts these nine tiles and packs them into a 3×3 block to make a 9×9 output filled with 5s. Each tile is placed according to where its colored shape sits inside the tile: top/left/right/bottom/corner tiles go to the corresponding outer position, and the all-5 tile goes in the center.
a8d7556c: The input is mostly a random-looking field of 5s and 0s, and the output keeps it unchanged except for certain 0-regions. Any place where the grid contains a solid all-0 square of size 2×2 is highlighted by recoloring that whole 2×2 block to color 2. Larger zero-rectangles are recolored only through their overlapping 2×2 zero sub-blocks, so exactly the cells that belong to at least one 2×2 all-zero block become 2.
a934301b: Treat each orthogonally connected non-background cluster of colored cells (the blue 1s together with any cyan 8s touching them) as one object. Count how many cyan/8 cells each object contains, and erase any object that has more than one 8 by turning all of its cells to 0. Objects with zero or one 8 are left unchanged.
a9f96cdd: Find the single red cell (2), treat it as the center of a diagonal 2×2 corner pattern, and then remove it. Paint its four diagonal neighbors with fixed colors by direction: upper-left = 3, upper-right = 6, lower-left = 8, lower-right = 7. Any of those diagonal positions that would fall outside the grid are simply omitted, and all other cells stay unchanged.
aa18de87: The input contains a single sparse non-background color forming left/right boundary points on some rows. For each row independently, whenever there are two or more such colored cells, every cell strictly between consecutive colored cells on that same row is filled with color 2. The original colored cells stay unchanged, and rows with fewer than two colored cells are left as they are.
aa300dc3: The grid contains an irregular region of black cells (0) inside a gray background (5). The output highlights one maximal diagonal chain of black cells by recoloring it to blue (8): either a down-right diagonal or a down-left diagonal. Among these two possible diagonal orientations, it chooses the longer continuous diagonal that fits entirely inside the black region, leaving everything else unchanged.
aa62e3f4: The grid is reduced to just the rarest non-background color, placed on a blank background of the original majority color. That rare color is copied onto every background cell that is orthogonally adjacent to the original non-background figure, i.e. it draws the 4-neighbor outer border/silhouette of the whole foreground shape. All original foreground pixels themselves are removed, leaving only this one-cell-thick exterior outline.
aab50785: Find all 2×2 blocks of color 8 that occur in pairs on the same two rows. For each such left/right pair, extract the rectangular strip lying strictly between the two 8-blocks, using those same two rows only. Stack these extracted 2-row strips vertically from top to bottom to form the output.
aabf363d: The grid contains a large multi-cell shape in one non-background color and a single isolated colored pixel in the bottom-left corner. Recolor the entire large shape with the bottom-left pixel’s color, then erase that bottom-left pixel by turning it into background. The shape’s position and geometry stay exactly the same.
aaecdb9a: The input contains several disconnected monochrome objects on a background of 7; only colors 5, 2, 8, 9, and 6 matter. The output is a small 5-column histogram-like summary: one column per color in the fixed order 5, 2, 8, 9, 6, and the height of each colored vertical bar equals the size of that color’s object (or 0 if absent). These bars are bottom-aligned in a background-7 canvas whose height is the largest object size among those five colors.
aaef0977: The input is an all-7 grid with exactly one special cell of a different color. The output ignores the 7s and fills the whole grid with concentric Manhattan-distance “rings” centered on that special cell: all cells at the same city-block distance from it get the same value. The center keeps its original non-7 color, and as distance increases the colors follow a fixed 9-color cycle \(3,4,0,5,2,8,9,6,1\), starting from the center’s color within that cycle.
aba27056: The input contains a single hollow, rectangular C-/cup-shaped outline in some color, open on one side. The output keeps that outline unchanged, fills the empty cells inside its bounding rectangle with yellow (4), and then lets that yellow “flow” out through the open side all the way to the grid edge in the opening’s direction. From the two corners bordering the opening, two diagonal yellow rays are also extended outward to the grid boundary.
abbfd121: The input is a large periodic wallpaper pattern that has been partially covered by one or more solid-colored rectangular “occlusions.” The output is the bounding-box size of the largest such occluding rectangle, but filled not with the occluder color: instead, it reconstructs the underlying repeating background pattern in that region. In other words, recover the clean tile pattern and crop out a patch whose height and width match the largest hidden rectangle.
ac0a08a4: Count the non-background cells (all cells that are not color 0) in the input; this count is the enlargement factor. The output is the entire input grid magnified by that factor, so each original cell becomes a solid square block of that size and keeps its color, including 0-cells staying as background. Thus the layout is preserved exactly, just blown up, with output dimensions equal to the input dimensions multiplied by the number of nonzero cells.
ac0c2ac3: Ignore the 7-background and look at the remaining colored seed cells. Treat the grid center as the center of a set of concentric square/rectangular rings: each non-7 seed color expands to fill all cells whose Chebyshev distance from the center is at most that seed’s distance, with nearer seeds overwriting farther ones. Equivalently, the output is a bullseye of nested square rings centered on the grid, colored from outside to inside according to the non-7 cells ordered by their distance from the center.
ac0c5833: The grid contains one orange/red shape (color 2) that serves as a template, and many yellow marker triples (color 4) where three cells occupy three corners of a 3×3-frame corner pattern: equivalently, they are three corners of a 2×2 square with one corner missing. For each such yellow L-shape, place a copy of the 2-shape at the missing corner, oriented by rotation/reflection so it has the same corner-direction as the marker arrangement and extends away from the three yellow cells. Keep all original 4s and 2s, and only add new 2s onto empty cells.
ac2e8ecf: Identify each connected colored object and split them into two shape classes: **hollow rectangular frames** (“O” shapes, whose cells are exactly the border of their bounding box) and **all other line/cross-like shapes**. Keep every object’s columns unchanged, but slide all frame objects **upward** as far as possible, packing them against the top without overlapping each other, and slide all non-frame objects **downward** as far as possible, packing them against the bottom without overlapping each other. Within each class, the original vertical order is preserved as they stack.
ac3e2b04: The grid contains red line(s) all running in one direction, and some of those lines pass through a 3×3 green “target” with a red center. For each such target, draw a blue line through its center in the perpendicular direction, spanning the whole grid. Wherever this new blue line crosses any red line, add the rest of a 3×3 blue target around that crossing (blue neighbors around the red center), while leaving existing red and green cells unchanged.
ac605cbb:
Each input contains isolated colored seed cells of colors 1, 2, 3, and/or 6. Each seed grows a fixed shape: **1** extends two cells to the right in gray (5) and then places another **1** one row up at the end; **2** extends three cells left in gray and then another **2** one more cell left; **3** extends two cells downward in gray and then another **3** one more cell down; **6** extends five cells upward in gray and then another **6** one more cell up.  

If gray extensions from different seeds land on the same cell, that overlap becomes yellow (4) instead of gray, and from every such yellow intersection a yellow diagonal is drawn down-left to the grid edge. The original seed cells remain in place.
ac6f9922:
The input is a large rectangular “board” partitioned into smaller rooms by thick lines of one separator color; the outer frame uses that same color. Inside each room, most cells are filled with a common background color, and some rooms contain a small patch of a third color.  

The output is a compressed map of the room layout: one output cell per room, arranged in the same row/column structure. Each output cell shows the non-background, non-separator color found in that room, or the separator/border color if the room is empty.
ad173014: The grid contains several separate color-2 rectangular frames embedded in a larger color-1 outline. Each framed box contains a small pattern made with exactly one extra color besides 0 and 2. The transformation keeps every frame and pattern shape unchanged, but cyclically shifts those interior colors from one box to the next box around the central structure (ordered by side/position), so each box’s motif is recolored with the color from its neighboring box.
ad38a9d0: The grid contains several disconnected clusters of color 6 on a color-7 background. Each 6-cluster is recolored uniformly according to its shape/type: identical cluster shapes get the same new color wherever they appear, while different shapes get different colors. In these examples, the mapping is by the cluster’s size and bounding-box form (for example, 5-cell blobs, 6-cell rectangles, 4-cell 2×2 blocks, 3-cell vertical lines, 3-cell L-shapes, and 2-cell dominoes each receive their own fixed color).
ad3b40cf: The 1-cells form a straight mirror line: it may be horizontal, vertical, or diagonal. Among the other non-background colors, take the color whose shape is smallest, and copy that shape by reflecting it across the 1-line onto the opposite side. Only reflected cells that land on background (8) are added; everything else stays unchanged.
ad7e01d0: Treat the input as both a tile and a placement map. Build a larger output grid of size \(N^2 \times N^2\) (for an \(N \times N\) input), divided into an \(N \times N\) array of \(N \times N\) blocks; whenever an input cell is gray/5, place a full copy of the entire original input into the corresponding block, and leave blocks for all other input cells black/0.
ae3edfdc:
The grid contains two single-cell “centers”: a blue 1 and a red 2. All orange 7s belong to the blue center, and all green 3s belong to the red center; each such cell starts somewhere on the same row or column as its center.

In the output, the 1 and 2 stay fixed, while each 7 or 3 is slid straight inward along its row/column until it becomes immediately adjacent to its matching center on that side. Thus the scattered aligned cells collapse into a compact cross/flower around the blue and red centers, and their original positions are cleared.
ae4f1146: The input contains several disconnected non-background clusters made of colors 1 and 8 on a 0 background. Select the connected cluster that contains the largest number of color-1 cells, then crop out its minimal bounding rectangle. In these examples, that chosen cluster is a 3×3 pattern, and that exact 3×3 block is returned as the output.
ae58858e: The input contains several disconnected red shapes (color 2) on a background. Find each orthogonally connected red component, and if it has more than 3 cells, recolor the entire component to magenta/pink (color 6). Smaller red components stay red, and everything else is unchanged.
aedd82e4: Copy the grid, then find every red cell (color 2) that is a singleton under side-adjacency: it has no red neighbor above, below, left, or right. Recolor each such isolated red cell to blue (color 1). All red cells that belong to a horizontal/vertical group, and all other cells, stay unchanged.
aee291af: The input contains several same-sized square patches whose outer border is entirely color 8, while their interiors contain only 8s and some 2s. Most of these bordered squares are identical, but one has a different arrangement of interior 2-cells. The output is that odd one out, extracted as its own grid with the 8 border and interior pattern preserved exactly.
af24b4cc: The input is partitioned by black separators into six small colored subgrids arranged as 2 rows by 3 columns. For each subgrid, take its dominant color (the color appearing most often within that block). The output is a compressed summary: a 4×5 grid with a black border, whose inner 2×3 cells contain those six dominant colors in the same spatial arrangement as the original blocks.
af726779: Find the single row containing the original 7s and treat its 7-columns as a 1D pattern. Then, every two rows below it, place a new row whose marked columns are the midpoints of adjacent marked columns from the previous such row; whenever two marks are separated by exactly one gap, that gap becomes the next mark. These derived rows alternate colors 6, 7, 6, 7, …, while all other cells stay as the background color 3.
af902bf9: Find every set of four non-background cells of the same color that sit at the corners of an axis-aligned rectangle. For each such rectangle, fill the area strictly inside it with color 2, leaving the four corner markers unchanged. If multiple such rectangles are present, apply this independently to each one.
afe3afe9: The grid contains many separated 3×3 “ring” tiles (same color around a 0 center), plus a full border line of color 1 on one outer edge that indicates the viewing direction. Remove that 1-border, collapse away the blank separator rows/columns, and shrink each 3×3 tile to a single cell of its color, producing a compact map of the tile layout. In the final compact map, within each row the nonzero cells are packed toward the side opposite the marked edge (equivalently: toward the marked edge after undoing the orientation normalization).
b0722778: The input is organized into three 2-column blocks per nonblank 2-row band: a left block, a middle block, and a right block, with all-zero rows separating the bands. For each 2×2 band, infer the relationship between the left and middle blocks: either the middle is the same shape as the left but with colors relabeled, or it is a rotated/reflected version of the left. Then apply that same color relabeling or geometric symmetry to the right 2×2 block, and output only the transformed right block as a 2-column grid. Blank separator rows become `[0,0]` rows in the output.
b0c4d837:
The input contains a gray U-shaped container (color 5) with blue fill (color 8) at the bottom and empty black space above it. Count how many interior rows of empty space lie between the top of the container and the top of the blue region.  

Output a 3×3 black grid, then place that many blue cells into it in reading order: left-to-right across the top row, then continuing on the next rows if needed.
b0f4d537:
The input is split by a full-height gray (5) divider into two parts: one side is a small colored template made from colors other than 0/4/5, and the other side is a black field with yellow (4) marks. The yellow side acts as a placement mask: its bounding box becomes the output, and the pattern of yellow cells in each row/column tells where to place columns/rows from the colored template.

In the template, one repeated “base” column/row pattern appears most often, while a few exceptional rows/columns contain the extra colors (such as 2 or 3). The output reproduces the template’s nonzero colors on the yellow-marked positions, matching the exceptional yellow rows/columns to the exceptional template rows/columns in order, and leaving all unmarked cells black.
b15fca0b: Find the two red cells (color 2) and treat the blue cells (color 1) as walls. Then draw the shortest 4-connected path through the non-blue cells that links the two red cells, coloring the path yellow (color 4). The two red endpoints and all blue wall cells stay unchanged; only the traversed empty cells are turned into yellow.
b190f7f5: The input contains two equal-sized square parts placed side-by-side or top-and-bottom: one part is a light-blue (8) stencil shape, and the other is a small color image using other colors. The output expands that small image by replacing each cell of it with a full copy of the 8-shaped stencil, recolored to that cell’s color. Thus, if the stencil is \(N \times N\), the result is an \(N^2 \times N^2\) tiled image, with zeros in all positions outside the stencil copies.
b1948b0a: The output keeps the grid exactly the same shape and pattern, but recolors one of the two colors. Every cell with color 6 is changed to color 2, while all other cells (such as color 7) stay in their original positions unchanged.
b1986d4b:
Ignore the scattered noise pixels and look only at the non-background colors that form complete square blocks. There are exactly three meaningful square sizes present: 4×4, 3×3, and 2×2, each size having its own color; count how many squares of each size appear.

The output is a 5-row horizontal summary made of one column-group per detected square, separated by a background-colored spacer column. Each group is a canonical “stacked footprint” of that square: a 2×2 square contributes its color in the top two rows and left two columns, a 3×3 square contributes its color in the top three rows and left three columns, and a 4×4 square contributes its color in the top four rows and left four columns; these groups are repeated according to the counts and arranged in a fixed order.
b1fc8b8e: The blue shape in the input is interpreted as four merged copies of the same tiny 2×2 motif. That motif is either a full 2×2 square of blue cells or a 2×2 L-shape with one missing corner, depending on whether the input contains 16 or 15 blue cells. The output separates these four identical motifs and places one in each corner of a 5×5 grid, leaving the middle row and middle column empty as a black cross.
b20f7c8b:
The grid contains a “legend” area: inside the large color-8 region are several small shapes, and each shape’s geometry defines a mapping from that geometry to the color it is drawn in. Elsewhere, the important objects are 5×5 blocks: some are solid single-color blocks, while others are framed/patterned blocks containing a small shape made of 1s inside a 2 background.

For each patterned 5×5 block, identify its inner shape and replace the whole block with a solid fill of the legend color for that shape. For each solid 5×5 block, replace it with the corresponding 1-on-2 pattern whose shape is the legend shape associated with that block’s color (allowing rotations/reflections of the legend shape to match). The legend itself and the rest of the grid stay unchanged.
b230c067: Find the separate sky-blue (8) shapes and compare them by shape only, ignoring where they are placed. Any shape that has at least one identical copy elsewhere is recolored blue (1), while the one shape whose normalized form is unique is recolored red (2). The shapes stay in the same positions and the black background remains unchanged.
b25e450b:
The grid contains one or more connected zero-colored blocks that touch an outer border. For each such zero block, the entire strip between that block and the opposite side of the grid is turned into 7s, and then the same zero rectangle is copied to the mirrored position on the opposite side of the grid (left ↔ right if the block touches a vertical border, top ↔ bottom if it touches a horizontal border).  

So each border-attached zero patch “jumps” across the grid to the opposite edge while the area it previously pointed across is filled with 7.
b27ca6d3: Copy the grid, then find every red connected component made of exactly two touching cells. Around each such 2-cell red object, draw a 1-cell-thick green rectangular outline just outside its bounding box, leaving the red cells themselves unchanged. All isolated red cells and everything else stay as they are.
b2862040: The grid contains several disconnected shapes made of color 1 on a color-9 background. Recolor to 8 every color-1 shape that is a closed loop enclosing at least one background cell—equivalently, shapes that surround a region of 9s cut off from the outer border. All other 1-shapes, and all 9-cells, remain unchanged.
b2bc3ffd: The grid has a uniform background and a solid row of 8s at the bottom that stays fixed. Every other colored connected shape above that row is copied upward by a number of rows equal to its own cell count, so the shapes “rise” toward the top while keeping their exact form and horizontal position. The original shapes are erased to background, and the 8-object is left unchanged.
b457fec5: The non-background, non-gray cells form a short color key; keep them unchanged. Each gray (5) staircase/diagonal wedge object is recolored by replacing its cells with diagonal contour bands whose colors follow that key in order, repeating cyclically as you move inward across the shape. If there are multiple gray objects, the same band-coloring rule is applied to each one independently.
b4a43f3b:
The input is split by the horizontal row of 5s. The 6 rows above it are compressed from 2×2 blocks into a 3×3 “tile” by keeping one cell from each 2-row/2-column block, so they define a small colored motif.

Below the separator, every cell colored 2 acts as a placement marker. For each such marker, a copy of the 3×3 motif is stamped into a large blank canvas, with the marker’s row and column determining the motif’s position on a 3-times-enlarged grid.
b527c5c6: Each non-black object is a solid green rectangle with one red cell embedded on one edge. For each such rectangle, extend that red cell straight outward from the rectangle, away from its interior, as a red line until the grid edge or until another colored region blocks it. Then thicken that extension into a band: fill the cells on both sides of the red line with green, with thickness equal to the rectangle’s depth behind the red cell (i.e. the rectangle’s size in the outward direction minus 1), while leaving the original rectangle intact.
b548a754: Find the non-background rectangular frame: one color forms a 1-cell-thick border and a second color fills its interior. A single cell of color 8 acts as a target outside the rectangle; stretch the rectangle only toward that target until the rectangle’s outer border reaches the 8’s row or column. The added edge keeps the original border color, the newly enclosed area is filled with the original interior color, and the 8 is overwritten.
b5bb5719: The top row contains a contiguous span of non-background cells (colors 2 and 5), and this row is used as the seed of a downward triangular pattern. Each lower row shrinks inward by one cell from both left and right, and inside that shrinking span each cell is determined from the two diagonal neighbors above: if they match, the color flips between 2 and 5; if they differ, the cell takes the upper-right color. Everything outside this descending wedge remains background 7, and the original top row stays unchanged.
b60334d2:
Each gray cell (color 5) is treated as the center of a fixed 3×3 motif. The original gray center is turned to black, its four orthogonal neighbors become blue (1), and its four diagonal neighbors become gray (5), producing the pattern

5 1 5
1 0 1
5 1 5

around every input gray cell. Cells outside these local neighborhoods stay unchanged, with any off-grid parts simply omitted.
b6afb2da: Each solid gray rectangle (color 5) is recolored in place according to position within the rectangle. The four corner cells become blue (1), the rest of the rectangle’s border becomes yellow (4), and all interior cells become red (2). All background cells remain unchanged.
b71a7747: The outer border is removed, and the remaining picture is reduced to just the cells whose color is neither the background color of the first row nor any other “separator/background” color. In effect, this extracts the embedded drawing made from the two non-background colors inside the larger patterned scaffold. The output is the tight cropped shape of that drawing, preserving those two colors and their arrangement.
b7249182: The input contains exactly two isolated colored cells, aligned either horizontally or vertically. The output connects them with equal straight “stems” of their own colors leading into a fixed central hollow frame: a 5×4 rectangle (or its rotated 4×5 version), oriented so its long side is perpendicular to the line between the cells. The half of this frame nearer one endpoint is colored with that endpoint’s color, the opposite half with the other color, while the interior remains black.
b7256dcd: Treat the grid as mostly background color 7, with one or more connected blobs of color 6. For each 6-blob, if it touches a non-7, non-6 colored cell, that blob is recolored to that neighboring color; otherwise it stays 6. All original non-7, non-6 “seed” cells disappear into the background (become 7), so the effect is that each colored marker transfers its color onto an adjacent 6-region.
b745798f: The non-background cells form small 2×2-corner patterns with one cell missing: each such shape indicates one of the four corner types (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, or bottom-right). In the output, each marker is “blown up” into a large L-shape of the same color anchored at the corresponding outer corner of the whole grid: a horizontal arm runs along the top or bottom edge toward the middle, and a vertical arm runs along the left or right edge toward the middle. The rest of the grid stays background-colored, so the result is a set of colored corner frames around the grid.
b74ca5d1:
The grid has a dominant background color, some special colors placed in the corners, and several interior monochrome shapes. Each interior shape is paired with the nearest corner-marker color, and the shape itself is recolored to that marker color in its original location.

In addition, a copy of that shape’s silhouette is stamped into the corresponding corner region, aligned to that corner (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, or bottom-right). If several shapes belong to the same corner, their corner stamps are combined there, while tiny attached “satellite” pieces are ignored when making the corner copy.
b775ac94: Each connected multicolor cluster contains one dominant “main” shape (the color with the most cells) plus one or more small differently colored cells touching it. Keep the main shape unchanged, and for each touching minority-colored cell, create a reflected copy of the main shape on the opposite side of that contact: reflect left/right if the cell touches from the side, and up/down if it touches from above or below. The new copy uses the touching cell’s color, so the small colored cells act as anchors that specify both the mirror direction and the color of the duplicated shape.
b782dc8a: Treat color 8 as walls, and the two other nonzero colors as seed colors placed inside the open black area. Recolor every black cell that is connected to those seeds through non-8 space into an alternating two-color checkerboard/parity pattern consistent with the seeds, while leaving the seed cells themselves unchanged. Any black region cut off by 8-walls stays black, and all 8-cells remain unchanged.
b7955b3c: Color 8 marks corrupted cells inside a picture made of solid-colored rectangular regions on a uniform background. The output reconstructs the clean rectangles: for each non-8 color, take the full bounding rectangle of its visible cells and fill that rectangle solidly with that color, with smaller/contained rectangles overriding larger enclosing ones. As a result, all 8s disappear and each region becomes a perfect filled rectangle of its proper color.
b7999b51: The input contains several non-background colors, each forming a shape with some vertical extent. For each color, measure the total row span it occupies from its topmost to bottommost appearance, then build a compact bar-chart grid whose columns represent these colors. The output has one column per nonzero color and height equal to the largest such span; columns are ordered from tallest span to shortest, and each column is filled from the top downward with that color for exactly its span, leaving the rest as 0.
b7cb93ac: Extract all non-background connected colored shapes from the large sparse grid and treat them as rigid puzzle pieces. Rotate pieces by 90° if needed, then pack them together without overlap to exactly tile a small solid rectangle; in these examples the rectangle is 3×4 (or 4×3 if that were the fitting orientation). The output is that compact fully filled rectangle, preserving each piece’s color and shape.
b7f8a4d8: The input is a regular grid of framed tiles whose interiors use several colors; one interior color is the dominant/default one, while the other interior colors are rarer “special” colors. In the output, each special color is extended to connect all tiles of that same color that lie in the same row or the same column, drawing straight horizontal/vertical bridges only through background (0) cells and leaving the tile frames and other nonzero cells unchanged. The most frequent interior color is not connected at all.
b7fb29bc: Find the single green cell inside the hollow green rectangular frame. Then fill every other cell inside that frame with concentric square layers centered on that cell: cells at Chebyshev distance 1 are yellow (4), distance 2 are red (2), distance 3 yellow again, and so on. The outer green frame and the central green cell stay unchanged, and everything outside the frame is untouched.
b8825c91: The input is an almost perfectly 4-way mirror-symmetric 16×16 pattern, except some cells have been replaced by color 4, which acts as a corruption mark. The transformation removes all 4s by copying the corresponding value from the cell related by the grid’s reflection symmetries, restoring symmetry across the main diagonal, anti-diagonal, horizontal axis, and vertical axis. The output is the fully repaired symmetric pattern.
b8cdaf2b: Keep the bottom two-row motif unchanged. Take the color of the central block in the bottom row, and extend it upward as two diagonals that start just above the left and right ends of the horizontal segment in the row above, moving up-left and up-right until they hit the side edges. The added cells form a symmetric V-shape made from that central bottom-row color.
b91ae062: The output is a magnified copy of the input grid. Count how many distinct nonzero colors appear in the input, and use that number as the zoom factor: every input cell, including 0/background cells, is replaced by a same-colored square block of that size. Thus the whole pattern is preserved but enlarged, with output dimensions multiplied by the number of nonzero colors.
b942fd60: Starting from the existing red cell(s) (color 2), extend a red line horizontally to the right through empty cells until it either reaches the grid edge or stops just before another colored cell. Whenever such a line stops because of a colored obstacle, treat that stopping point as a junction and grow new red lines in the two perpendicular directions, again continuing through empty cells until edge or obstacle. Repeat this recursively, never overwriting the nonzero colored cells, so the output is the original picture plus a network of red orthogonal paths that turn at obstacles.
b94a9452: Find the single non-background rectangular pattern embedded in the black field and crop the grid down to just that rectangle. Then swap the two colors used inside that rectangle: the dominant/background color of the rectangle becomes the rarer accent color, and the accent color becomes the dominant one. The shape and layout stay the same; only the surrounding black area is removed and the two colors are exchanged.
b9630600:
The input contains several separate green rectangular outlines (“rooms”). The output connects these rooms with straight horizontal or vertical green corridors between pairs of rooms that face each other and overlap in the perpendicular direction, choosing enough nearest links so that all rooms become one connected network.

Each corridor is drawn through the interiors aligned one cell in from the room walls, so wide corridors appear as hollow rectangular tubes while narrow ones are solid lines. Where a corridor meets a room, the wall is opened at that side so the passage connects into the room.
b9b7f026: The input contains several monochrome color regions, one per non-background color. Exactly one of these regions is not a solid filled rectangle: within its own bounding box, some cells are missing/empty, creating holes or gaps. The output is a 1×1 grid whose value is the color of that irregular, non-solid rectangular region.
ba1aa698: The input is a wide grid split into several equal vertical panels by full-height separator lines, with the same border and background structure in each panel. One panel contains a small non-background motif; that same motif is repeated in other panels at different vertical positions. The output collapses the multi-panel layout into a single panel of the same height, keeping the border/background, and places one copy of the motif at each row position where it appeared in any of the original panels.
ba26e723: Copy the 3-row pattern and recolor some yellow cells (4) to magenta (6) in a regular vertical rhythm. Specifically, in every third column starting from the leftmost one (columns 0, 3, 6, 9, …), change any yellow cell in that column to magenta, while leaving black cells and all other positions unchanged.
ba97ae07: The grid contains two non-background colored bars/stripes that cross each other. One color is the more prevalent non-background color; in the output, every cell inside that color’s full rectangular span is forced to that same color. Visually, this makes the most extensive stripe become solid and uninterrupted through the overlap, overwriting the crossing stripe only in the intersection region.
ba9d41b8: Each nonzero color forms a solid rectangular block on a zero background. In the output, every such block keeps its outer border unchanged, but its interior is turned into a checkerboard with the background: interior cells whose row+column parity matches the rectangle’s top-left corner are erased to 0, and the alternating interior cells keep the block’s original color. Cells outside these rectangles stay unchanged.
bae5c565: The input has a multicolored top row and, lower down, a vertical line of 8s in a field of 5s.
