The output erases the original top row and recreates it as a downward-growing wedge centered on that 8-column: on each lower row, more of the top-row pattern appears, shifted inward so the left part moves diagonally right toward the 8s and the right part moves diagonally left toward it. The 8 column stays in place as the center spine, so by the bottom the full top-row sequence has been “dropped” and aligned around that spine.
baf41dbf:
The green 3-cells form a hollow rectangular pattern, possibly with internal full rows/columns. The magenta 6-cells act as directional markers: if a 6 lies left/right/above/below the rectangle, that side of the rectangle is stretched outward until it ends one cell before the farthest such marker in that direction.

After resizing, the same rectangular line pattern is redrawn at the new size: the outer border remains hollow, and any original full horizontal or vertical green lines are extended across the enlarged rectangle. The 6-cells stay fixed in place.
bb43febb: Find each solid gray (color 5) rectangular block in the input. Recolor the interior of each block to red (color 2) while leaving a one-cell-thick gray border unchanged, so each solid rectangle becomes a gray frame with a red center. The background stays the same.
bb52a14b: Find the unique 3×3 template that contains some yellow 4-cells. Ignore its yellow cells and treat the remaining 1/8 arrangement as the defining pattern. Then scan the whole grid for every other occurrence of that same 1-and-8 layout, and complete each one by adding yellow 4s in exactly the same relative positions as in the template, leaving everything else unchanged.
bbb1b8b6: The 9-column input is split into a left 4×4 pattern, a separator column of 5s, and a right 4×4 pattern. The output is a 4×4 composite made by keeping every nonzero cell from the left pattern (the 1-shape) and, only in the positions where the left pattern has 0s, showing the corresponding cells from the right pattern. In effect, the left half acts as a stencil/mask and the right half fills its holes.
bbc9ae5d: The input is a single row containing one contiguous left-aligned block of a nonzero color, followed by zeros. The output keeps the same width but has height equal to half the input width; its first row is the original row, and each following row extends the colored block one cell farther to the right. This creates a downward staircase/right-triangle growth of the original color.
bc1d5164: The input has a blank horizontal middle row and a blank vertical middle column, which split it into four corner subgrids. Take those four corner blocks and overlay them onto one another, aligning each corner block to the corresponding corner of a smaller central square. The output is this superposition: a cell is colored if that position is colored in any of the four corner blocks, preserving the single non-background color.
bc4146bd: The input grid is stretched horizontally to five times its original width by repeating the same picture side by side. The repeated blocks alternate between the original grid and its left-right mirror image, in the order: original, mirrored, original, mirrored, original. The height stays the same.
bc93ec48: The grid contains small non-background objects that touch one or more of the four outer corners. Each such corner-touching object is copied to the next corner clockwise: bottom-left goes to top-left, top-left goes to top-right, top-right goes to bottom-right, and bottom-right goes to bottom-left. The object keeps its exact shape and orientation, aligned so the same corner of its bounding box sits on the new grid corner, while everything else in the grid stays unchanged.
bcb3040b: Find the two red cells (color 2) and connect them with the straight segment they define: horizontal, vertical, or 45° diagonal. Recolor every cell on that segment based on its original value: black/background cells (0) become red (2), blue cells (1) become green (3), and the red endpoints stay red. Everything else in the grid is unchanged.
bd14c3bf: Use the red shape in the upper-left as a template, but compare shapes after removing any repeated identical rows or columns, so only the essential pattern matters. Any blue object elsewhere whose simplified form matches that template, in any rotation or mirror orientation, is recolored red. All nonmatching objects stay unchanged.
bd283c4a: The input is treated as just a multiset of colors: count how many cells of each color there are, then ignore their original positions. Rebuild the same-sized grid by packing the colors into solid vertical strips from left to right, ordered from most frequent color to least frequent. Each strip’s area matches that color’s count, filled column by column, with any partially filled final column occupied from top to bottom.
bd4472b8: The input has a top row containing an ordered color sequence, followed by a full horizontal separator row of 5s. Keep these two rows unchanged, then fill every row below with a solid color row using the top-row colors in left-to-right order, repeating the sequence cyclically until the grid is full. Each generated row is uniform across the entire width.
bd5af378:
Each input is a two-color rectangle where one color forms an L-shaped border along two adjacent outer edges meeting at a corner. The output redraws those two border lines using the opposite color arrangement: the row/column just inside the L takes the border color, the outer row/column at the corner side takes the interior color, and the corner cell itself becomes 8.  

Then a diagonal of the interior color is drawn starting one step inward from that corner and continuing into the grid, while all remaining cells not on these lines are filled with 8. So the result is an 8-filled field containing a swapped L-frame and a same-direction diagonal running away from the original corner.
bda2d7a6: The grid is made of nested, concentric rectangular color regions (outer border, inner frame(s), and center). The transformation keeps the same geometry but cyclically shifts the colors one layer inward: each layer takes the color of the layer just outside it, while the innermost region’s color wraps around to become the new outermost border. In effect, only the layer colors rotate; the shapes and positions of the rectangles do not change.
bdad9b1f: The input contains short straight line segments: one horizontal and one vertical, each in its own color. Complete each segment into a full grid-spanning line in the same row or column, extending it all the way to both edges. Where the completed horizontal and vertical lines cross, replace that cell with color 4.
be03b35f: Take the 2×2 block in the lower-left part of the 5×5 input, just below the all-zero middle row and to the left of the 2-colored block. Rotate that 2×2 pattern 90° clockwise, and use the rotated result as the output. All other parts of the input are irrelevant.
be94b721: Find the largest single-color connected shape in the input (ignoring the black background and any other smaller shapes). Output just that shape, cropped to its tight bounding rectangle, preserving its original color and any 0-cells inside the shape’s bounding box.
beb8660c: Each non-black row is a single horizontal colored bar, and the bottom row is a full-width bar of color 8. The task gathers all the non-8 bars, right-justifies them, and restacks them directly above the 8-row in order of increasing length from top to bottom (equivalently, longest at the bottom, shortest at the top). All remaining rows above are filled with black, and the colors and lengths of the bars are preserved.
bf32578f: The input shows one side/half of a symmetric outline in a single nonzero color. Reflect that pattern horizontally to complete the full closed outline, then fill the enclosed area with the same color. The output keeps only this filled interior region, not the outline itself.
bf699163: The input contains several 3×3 “ring” tiles: a solid-colored border around a gray center. Color 7 forms one connected marker shape, and exactly one of those 3×3 rings lies completely inside the marker’s bounding rectangle while the others lie outside or overlap it. The output is that selected 3×3 ring copied out by itself, preserving its border color and gray center.
bf89d739: Find the two red cells (color 2) that are farthest apart while lying on the same row or the same column, and use them as the endpoints of a main straight “spine.” Draw that spine in green (color 3), keeping the red endpoints unchanged. Then connect every other red cell to this spine with a single perpendicular straight green segment (vertical if the spine is horizontal, horizontal if the spine is vertical), again leaving the red cells themselves as 2.
c074846d: Each input has a single gray cell (5) acting as a pivot, with a straight contiguous line of red cells (2) extending from it in one cardinal direction. In the output, that red line is turned 90° clockwise around the gray pivot, keeping the same length, while the original red cells are recolored to green (3). The gray cell and all other background cells stay unchanged.
c0f76784: Find each connected gray (5) shape, which in these examples is a hollow square/ring or a single isolated gray cell. Keep the gray outlines unchanged, but fill the enclosed hole according to its size: a 1-cell hole becomes color 6, a 2×2 hole becomes color 7, and a 3×3 hole becomes color 8. Thus 3×3, 4×4, and 5×5 gray square frames get their interiors filled with 6, 7, and 8 respectively.
c1990cce: The input is a single row with one red cell in the middle; it is expanded into an \(N \times N\) square, where \(N\) is the row length. Red cells form a symmetric V (or open triangle) starting from the top-center cell and extending diagonally down-left and down-right to the side edges. Inside and below this red V, blue cells are added as evenly spaced parallel down-right diagonals, beginning near the left arm of the V and repeating every other such diagonal, while leaving the red border unchanged.
c1d99e64: The grid contains one nonzero pattern color plus black background. Any entire row or entire column that is completely black (all 0s) is highlighted by recoloring that full line to red (2). Everything else stays unchanged, including black cells that are not part of a full-width or full-height black line.
c3202e5a: The input is partitioned into equal rectangular cells by full-width and full-height separator lines of one color. Remove those separator rows and columns, inspect each cell, and find the unique cell whose nonzero contents use only a single color (all other cells contain multiple nonzero colors). The output is that cell copied exactly, preserving its size and the positions of its colored pixels and zeros.
c35c1b4c: The transformation restores left-right symmetry for the main shape color. First identify the color that is most common in the grid (the large central object), then for every cell of that color, paint its horizontally mirrored counterpart across the vertical midline with the same color. All other cells stay unchanged unless they lie at one of these mirrored positions, in which case they are overwritten by the main color.
c3e719e8: Take the 3×3 input and identify its most frequent color. Build a 9×9 output as a 3×3 array of 3×3 blocks, and in each block whose position corresponds to a cell containing that dominant color, place a full copy of the original 3×3 input. Leave all other blocks as 0, so the large pattern reproduces the spatial layout of the most common color.
c3fa4749:
The grid contains large, mostly solid rectangular blocks of a single color, but some cells inside those blocks are noisy “holes.” For each such block, look at the holes inside its cleaned-up rectangle: if a hole-region touches the rectangle border at exactly one point, treat that border cell’s color as the hole’s true color and flood that color through the whole enclosed hole (also absorbing any adjacent cells of the block color that are trapped against that hole).  

So the transformation repairs enclosed defects inside large monochrome rectangles by extending the color seen at the hole’s single border connection across the entire interior cavity. If no such qualifying enclosed hole exists, the grid stays unchanged.
c444b776: The color-4 full rows/columns act as divider lines that split the grid into equal rectangular compartments. Exactly one compartment contains a nonzero colored pattern; copy that same pattern, preserving all colors and relative positions, into every other compartment. The divider lines remain unchanged.
c48954c1: The input 3×3 pattern is expanded into a 9×9 grid made of a 3×3 arrangement of transformed copies of the original tile. The center tile is the original input, the tiles directly above and below it are vertically flipped copies, the tiles directly left and right are horizontally flipped copies, and the four corner tiles are flipped both ways (equivalently, rotated 180°). The result is a larger pattern symmetric around the central tile.
c4d1a9ae: The grid contains one dominant background color and three rarer colors that each occupy a vertical band. In the output, every background-colored cell is recolored according to which band/column-range it lies in: cells under the leftmost rare-color band become the middle rare color, cells under the middle band become the rightmost rare color, and cells under the rightmost band become the leftmost rare color. All originally non-background cells stay unchanged, so the three sparse color patterns are effectively “projected” across their horizontal spans onto the background and cyclically relabeled.
c59eb873: The task uniformly enlarges the entire input picture by a factor of 2 in both height and width. Every original cell is replaced by a 2×2 block of the same color, including background cells, so the output is just a doubled-resolution version of the input pattern.
c6141b15:
The grid contains exactly two non-background colors: one color appears as a single connected “template” shape, while the other appears as several separate marker objects. In the output, these roles are exchanged: the marker-object centers are connected pairwise with straight lines using the template’s color, and copies of the template shape are placed at the two extreme endpoints of the original template using the marker color.  

So, one color supplies the shape to be stamped, and the other color supplies the anchor points to be linked; the two colors swap these structural roles in the result.
c61be7dc: The grid contains a plus-shaped arrangement of full 0-colored separator lines and a cluster of 5s centered on their intersection. The output collapses the 5-region into a single straight bar through its center: if the separator structure is vertical (full 0 columns), the 5s become one vertical line; if it is horizontal (full 0 rows), the 5s become one horizontal line. The two neighboring separator lines perpendicular to that bar are redrawn as full 0 lines on either side of it, while the rest of the grid stays 7.
c62e2108: The input contains one or more hollow 4×4 square frames in some color, plus blue `1` guide segments. Each blue segment marks a direction in which a matching frame should be repeated in 4-cell steps: if the guide is horizontal, copies are stacked vertically toward it; if the guide is vertical, copies are repeated horizontally toward it, continuing until the grid boundary/guide is reached. The repeated copies keep the same hollow-square pattern and may merge into longer bands, and all blue guide cells disappear in the output.
c64f1187:
The input has a legend at the top: each colored marker is paired with a small 2×2 blue shape, defining which shape belongs to that color. In the lower gray region, arranged as a lattice of 2×2 gray cells separated by black rows/columns, some cells contain a colored marker.  

The output is the gray lattice cropped out and cleaned: every marked 2×2 cell is replaced by the corresponding legend shape, drawn in that marker’s color, while unmarked gray cells become empty black and the separator rows/columns stay black.
c658a4bd: Ignore the black background and treat each remaining color as one square-like object, measured by the size of its bounding square. Rebuild these colors into a single concentric square: the largest color becomes the outer border, the next largest becomes the next inner border, and so on, with the smallest color filling the center. The output is therefore a nested stack of colored square layers ordered from largest to smallest.
c6e1b8da: Each nonzero color forms a mostly solid rectangle, and some rectangles have a thin 1-cell-wide same-colored “tail” sticking out from one side. That tail is a movement instruction: move the rectangle in the tail’s direction by exactly the tail’s length, then discard the tail. Rectangles without such a protruding bar stay where they are, and if moved rectangles overlap others in the final picture, the moved shape simply overwrites those cells.
c7d4e6ad: The grid contains a gray shape (color 5) spread across several rows, and each such row has a colored reference cell at the far left. Recolor every gray cell in a row to match that row’s leftmost colored cell, so the gray shape becomes striped by row color. All non-gray cells stay unchanged.
c803e39c: The input is split into four panels by gray separator columns: a shape in color 1, a shape in color 2, and two solid-color reference panels whose colors become the two output colors. Take the color-1 shape as a small stamp, and for every filled cell of the color-2 shape, place a copy of that stamp in the corresponding position of a larger grid. The large canvas is initially filled with the rightmost panel’s color, and the stamped copies are drawn in the other reference color, so the result is a block pattern whose size is the product of the two shape sizes.
c87289bb: The blue 8-cells at the top act like vertical streams that must be continued down to the bottom of the grid. If a stream’s column is clear, it simply extends straight downward; if it hits a horizontal red 2-block, it detours around that block to the nearest open side (or both sides if equally near), adding a short horizontal/top-corner segment and then continuing vertically downward from the side column. The red 2-cells remain unchanged.
c8b7cc0f: The blue cells form a rectangular outline that defines a region of interest. Count the non-black, non-blue cells of the other color that lie inside this blue rectangle, then make a 3×3 black grid and fill its cells with that color in reading order (left-to-right, top-to-bottom) for exactly that many positions. The blue frame itself is discarded.
c8cbb738: Treat each non-background color as one sparse shape, and take its minimal bounding rectangle. Create a new grid whose height and width are the largest such height and width among all colors, filled with the original background color. Then recenter every colored shape so that the center of its own bounding box matches the center of the new grid, and overlay them all together.
c8f0f002: The output is the same grid as the input, except every cell of color 7 is recolored to 5. All other colors stay in their original positions unchanged, so the grid’s size and pattern are preserved.
c909285e: The input is mostly a large repetitive pattern, but one color forms a single solid square frame around a smaller embedded patch. The task is to find that uniquely framed square region and crop it out. The output is exactly that square subgrid, including its uniform border and the interior pattern unchanged.
c920a713:
The input contains one connected non-background object for each color, and these colored shapes touch each other in a chain via shared border cells. The task extracts the colors in that contact order, from the unique “end” color that touches only one other shape through to the opposite end.

It then builds a new square image of concentric 1-cell-thick square rings, using those colors in that same order from outside to inside. Thus the outermost ring is the first end color, the next ring is the color it touches, and so on, with the last color becoming the center cell.
c92b942c: The output is a 3×3 tiling of the entire input grid, so the canvas becomes three times taller and three times wider. Every original nonzero cell is copied into all 9 corresponding tile positions and keeps its original color; in each such row, all other cells across the full output row are filled with color 1. Additionally, for each copied nonzero cell, the diagonally adjacent cells at up-left and down-right are colored 3 when they exist, while all remaining cells stay 0.
c9680e90: The row of 9s is a fixed horizontal mirror axis. In the lower half, each red 2 is attached to a contiguous line of magenta 6s; keep only the far endpoint of that 2→6 segment and turn that endpoint into a red 2. Then place a gray 5 at the position vertically reflected across the 9-row, and erase all other non-9 markings to background 7.
c97c0139: Each red object is a straight line segment, and it stays unchanged. Around each such line, add light-blue cells on both perpendicular sides to make a symmetric diamond/pyramid shape: for a horizontal red line, blue rows are added above and below; for a vertical red line, blue columns are added left and right. The added blue bands get shorter by one cell at each end as they move farther from the red line, so the shape tapers to points.
c9e6f938: The output keeps the input grid unchanged on the left and adds a left-right mirror image of it immediately on the right. Thus the height stays the same, the width doubles, and each row becomes the original row followed by that row reversed.
c9f8e694: Each row contains one “source” color (a non-black, non-gray color) and some gray cells (5). The transformation repaints every gray cell in a row with that row’s source color, so the gray blocks become horizontal extensions of the row’s colored marker. Black cells stay black, and the grid’s size and block layout are unchanged.
ca8de6ea: The 5×5 input is a sparse pattern with colored cells only at the four outer corners, the four inner diagonal positions, and the center. The output compresses these nine colored cells into a 3×3 grid: outer corners stay as corners, the center stays in the center, and the four inner diagonal cells move to the four edge-middle positions according to their direction (top-left inner → top middle, top-right inner → right middle, bottom-left inner → left middle, bottom-right inner → bottom middle). All 0/background cells are discarded.
caa06a1f: The grid contains a periodic pattern made from the non-background colors, but part of it is missing and replaced by a single filler color that occupies one or two bordering strips (right side, bottom, or both). The task is to recover the underlying repeating motif and extend/tile it so it fills the entire grid, replacing all filler-color cells. The completed pattern is aligned consistently with the visible motif, so the whole output becomes a full seamless checker/repeating pattern of the original non-filler colors.
cad67732: The input contains a nonzero motif that repeats along a diagonal, either from top-left to bottom-right or, if the pattern starts at the top-right, along the opposite diagonal. The output is a blank grid with both height and width doubled, where that same motif is continued in the same diagonal direction and with the same spacing until it spans the larger canvas. All other cells remain 0.
cb227835: The input contains exactly two blue cells (8), which are kept in place. Add green cells (3) to connect them by drawing the outline of a thin diagonal/tilted rectangle: two parallel 45° staircase edges whose endpoints are the two blue cells. When the row and column gaps are unequal, the longer direction gets straight horizontal or vertical stretches at the ends, so the two blue cells remain opposite corners of the same slanted rectangular band.
cbded52d: The black 0-lines split the picture into a 3×3 array of 2×2 blocks, with 1 as the default fill color. Any non-1 colored cell is treated by its position inside a block (same corner/slot across blocks): if that same colored slot appears in two blocks aligned in the same row or the same column of the 3×3 layout, the missing middle block on that line is filled with that same color in the same slot. Everything else stays unchanged.
cc9053aa: The grid contains a single connected “maze” or frame of 8s, plus one or two 9s placed just outside it as terminals. The transformation recolors to 9 the border path of 8s that connects the terminal-adjacent entry points, choosing the unique outer route that does **not** run next to any 7s; if both possible routes touch 7s, it instead marks the corresponding outer perimeter segments determined by the terminal alignment. All other cells, especially 0s and 7s, stay unchanged.
ccd554ac: The entire input grid is used as a tile and repeated to make a larger mosaic. If the input has \(N\) rows (and in these examples is \(N \times N\)), the pattern is copied \(N\) times across and \(N\) times down, producing an \(N^2 \times N^2\) output made of identical copies of the original grid. Colors and arrangement stay unchanged; only whole-pattern repetition occurs.
cce03e0d: The input pattern is used as a tile to build a larger grid of size \(N^2 \times N^2\) (here, \(3\times3 \rightarrow 9\times9\)), viewed as an \(N\times N\) array of \(N\times N\) blocks. Every cell in the input that is color 2 marks one block position in the large grid; in each such block, place a full copy of the entire original input. All other blocks remain filled with 0.
cd3c21df: Find all orthogonally connected non-background objects in the grid and compare them by their exact shape and color pattern, ignoring position. Most objects have an identical duplicate elsewhere; one object appears only once. Output that unique object alone, cropped to its bounding box.
cdecee7f: Take all non-black cells from the sparse 10×10 input, ordering them by scanning columns from left to right and, within each column, from top to bottom. Write their colors into a 3×3 output grid in that same sequence, but with a snake layout: top row left-to-right, middle row right-to-left, bottom row left-to-right. If fewer than 9 colored cells exist, leave the remaining output cells black.
ce039d91: Copy the input grid unchanged, then look for gray cells (color 5) that are part of a left-right mirror match across the grid’s vertical center line. Any gray cell whose horizontally reflected counterpart in the same row is also gray is recolored to blue (color 1), and its mirrored partner is too. All other cells remain unchanged.
ce22a75a: Each gray seed cell is expanded into a solid 3×3 blue square centered on that cell. Equivalently, the cell and all eight neighboring positions become blue, clipped by the grid edges. If multiple such 3×3 squares overlap, they merge into one blue region; all other cells stay unchanged/background.
ce4f8723: The input contains two same-sized pattern blocks separated by a solid divider line of color 4: the upper block uses color 1 on black, and the lower block uses color 2 on black. The output overlays these two blocks cell-by-cell: any position that is colored in either block becomes color 3, while a position stays black only if both corresponding cells are black. Thus the result is the union of the two shapes, recolored uniformly to 3.
ce602527: The input contains several non-background colored shapes, one of which touches the outer border while the others are interior. Compare the border-touching shape with the interior shapes: one interior shape is the same pattern as the border shape, just enlarged by a factor of 2 and recolored. The output is the other interior shape, cropped to its bounding box, preserving both its foreground color and any background-colored holes inside it.
ce8d95cc: The input is a sparse “woven grid” made of full-height colored vertical stripes and full-width colored horizontal stripes, separated by repeated identical background rows and columns. The transformation compresses the picture by collapsing each run of consecutive identical rows to one row, and each run of consecutive identical columns to one column. This produces a minimal version of the same stripe-intersection pattern, preserving the left-to-right/top-to-bottom order and the original color seen at every crossing.
ce9e57f2: The input contains several separate vertical bars of red cells (color 2) on a black background. In the output, each bar is split horizontally: its lower half is recolored to light blue (color 8), while the upper half stays red. If a bar has odd height, only the bottom ⌊height/2⌋ cells change, so the red part is one cell taller than the blue part.
cf133acc:
Each row that contains a broken horizontal bar of some color—two same-colored runs separated by a single 0—gets completed by filling that gap. The gap’s column becomes a vertical “spine”: in that column, the cells are colored from top to bottom in stacked segments, where each segment takes the color of the nearest such broken bar at or above that row, and below the lowest bar it takes the color of the first existing nonzero cell already present beneath it.  

Visually, this turns the broken bars into solid bars and creates vertical connectors through their gap columns, producing aligned T-shaped/joined structures while leaving unrelated cells unchanged.
cf5fd0ad: The input 3×3 pattern is expanded into a 12×12 image made of four 6×6 quadrants. Each quadrant is a 2×2 tiling of one orientation of the input: top-left is the 180° rotation, top-right is the 90° clockwise rotation, bottom-left is the 270° rotation, and bottom-right is the original pattern. In other words, the output shows all four rotations of the input, each enlarged by repetition rather than scaling.
cf98881b:
The 14-column input is three 4×4 pictures placed side by side, separated by two vertical divider columns of color 2. The left picture uses color 4 on black, the middle uses color 9 on black, and the right uses color 1 on black.

The output is a single 4×4 image formed by overlaying these three pictures at matching positions: keep the left block’s non-black cells first, otherwise use the middle block’s non-black cells, otherwise use the right block’s non-black cells, and leave black only where all three are black. So the effective priority is **4-layer over 9-layer over 1-layer over 0**.
cfb2ce5a: The 4×4 block in the upper-left interior is the template, and it uses exactly two colors. The output fills the whole inner 8×8 area with four versions of this template: original in the top-left, mirrored left-right in the top-right, mirrored top-bottom in the bottom-left, and rotated 180° in the bottom-right. In each of the three new quadrants, the template’s two colors are replaced by the two clue colors already present in that quadrant’s corresponding border/corner region, while the outer border of zeros stays unchanged.
d017b73f: The input contains several separate nonzero connected shapes. These shapes are kept in their left-to-right order and packed together into a single horizontal chain: each later shape is translated leftward until it sits immediately to the right of the previous one, while also being shifted up or down so the two shapes touch by a side and form a zig-zag/snake-like connection. Finally, the result is cropped horizontally to the tight bounding width, while preserving each shape’s color and orientation.
d037b0a7: Each non-background colored cell grows straight downward in its own column. Copy its color into all cells below it until the bottom edge of the grid, leaving the original cells unchanged. In effect, every colored square becomes a vertical bar extending from its starting position to the bottom.
d06dbe63: The input contains a single special cell of color 8 on an otherwise empty grid. Keep that 8 in place, and add color-5 cells to form two opposite staircase arms: one grows from the 8 toward the upper-right, the other toward the lower-left. Each arm repeats an L-shaped step pattern of 2 cells vertically (up or down) followed by 3 cells horizontally (right or left), continuing until the grid edge truncates it.
d07ae81c: The grid has two large background colors and a few isolated “wrong-color” single cells embedded in them. From each such dot, draw an X reaching the grid edges by extending all four diagonal rays. The color used on each ray segment depends on which background region that cell belongs to: cells in one background are recolored with the dot-color associated with that background, and cells in the other background use the other dot color, so the diagonal lines swap color when they cross between the two background regions.
d0f5fe59: Identify the number of separate non-background blobs in the input, treating diagonally touching cells as connected. Then produce a square output whose side length equals that blob count, filled with background color except for a top-left to bottom-right diagonal drawn in the blobs’ color.
d10ecb37:
The output is simply the 2×2 block from the upper-left corner of the input grid.  
Copy those four cells exactly, preserving their colors and arrangement, and ignore the rest of the input.
d13f3404: The output is a canvas twice the input’s height and width, with the original input appearing in the top-left corner. Every non-background cell in the input is then extended along its down-right diagonal, repeating the same color one step southeast at a time until the enlarged grid’s edge is reached. Background cells are ignored, so only the colored cells create these diagonal trails.
d19f7514: Split the input horizontally into two equal halves and compare them cell-by-cell. The output has the size of one half, and marks a cell with color 4 wherever either half contains a colored cell at that position; a cell stays 0 only if both corresponding cells are 0. In other words, it overlays the two half-patterns and shows their union in a single new color.
d22278a0: Each nonzero input cell is a colored “seed” that grows into a square-ring/diamond-like wave pattern centered on that seed, using every other concentric Manhattan-distance layer of cells. A cell is colored by a seed only if it lies on one of that seed’s odd-distance rings and is strictly closer to that seed than to any other seed; ties and even-distance layers stay black. The result is a partition of the grid into nearest-seed regions filled with alternating colored and empty diagonal bands.
d23f8c26: The output keeps only the cells in the grid’s central vertical column. Every cell outside that middle column is turned into the background color (0), while the middle-column values remain unchanged in place.
d255d7a7: The grid is mostly filled with 7s, plus several isolated 0-colored shapes that are really 3-cell-thick “stubs” attached to one side of a larger rectangular span; some cells inside those shapes are marked with 9. Each such 0/9 shape is detached from its long span and slid across its own bounding box to the opposite side, so a left-attached shape becomes right-attached, a bottom-attached shape becomes top-attached, etc., while keeping its orientation and any embedded 9s in the same relative places. Everything else is reset to 7, except any 9s not belonging to a moved shape, which stay where they were.
d282b262: Identify each connected non-background object and keep its shape, colors, and row positions unchanged. Then slide every object horizontally to the right as far as it can go, processing objects from the originally rightmost to the leftmost so they preserve their left-to-right order. Each object stops when it would hit the right border or overlap an object that was originally farther right.
d2abd087: Identify each separate gray (5) shape as a connected component. Recolor every component of exactly 6 cells to red (2), and recolor every other gray component to blue (1). The shapes’ positions and geometry stay unchanged, and the black background (0) remains untouched.
d2acf2cb: Yellow 4s act as endpoint markers when they appear at both ends of a row or at both ends of a column. In every such marked line, all cells strictly between the two 4s are color-toggled: black 0 ↔ light-blue 8 and magenta 6 ↔ orange 7. The 4s themselves and all cells not lying between such endpoint pairs stay unchanged.
d304284e: Find the single 7-colored shape and use it as a stamp. Repeat this shape horizontally to the right with exactly one blank column between copies, cycling the copy colors as 7, 7, 6, 7, 7, 6, … until the grid edge. For every copy that is recolored to 6, also repeat that same 6-shape downward in the same column, again separated by one blank row, continuing to the bottom of the grid.
d364b489: Each blue cell (color 1) acts as the center of a fixed cross-shaped motif. Keep the blue cell in place, and add color 2 directly above it, color 8 directly below it, color 7 directly to its left, and color 6 directly to its right. If any of those neighboring positions would fall outside the grid, they are simply omitted.
d37a1ef5: Find the hollow rectangle made of color 2 and the set of color-5 cells inside it. Compute the smallest axis-aligned rectangle that contains all the 5s, then fill the interior of the outer 2-frame with color 2 everywhere except inside that 5-bounding rectangle. The 5s and any 0s inside that inner rectangle are left unchanged, and everything outside the outer frame stays the same.
d406998b: The grid has one non-background cell in each column, all colored 5. Recolor every other such column’s 5 to 3, starting from the rightmost column and then skipping one column each time moving left. Equivalently, the 5s in columns whose parity matches the right edge become 3, while all other cells stay unchanged.
d43fd935: Find the unique solid rectangle of color 3, which acts as the anchor. For each other non-background colored cell that lies in one of the anchor’s rows or one of its columns, extend that cell straight toward the 3-block in its own color, filling the empty cells along the row or column until the line reaches the edge of the 3-block. Cells not aligned horizontally or vertically with the 3-block are left unchanged, and the 3-block itself is not overwritten.
d4469b4b: The 5×5 input contains black plus exactly one non-black color, and the arrangement of those colored cells is irrelevant. The output is always a 3×3 black grid with a gray (5) shape chosen only by that color: color 1 gives a plus sign, color 2 gives a T shape (top row plus center column), and color 3 gives a reversed L shape (right column plus bottom row).
d47aa2ff: The input contains two 10×10 pictures side by side, separated by a vertical column of 5s. The output is a 10×10 cell-by-cell comparison of those two pictures: any nonzero symbol that appears in the same position in both halves is kept in its original color, a symbol present only in the right half is marked with color 1, and a symbol present only in the left half is marked with color 2. Empty positions remain 0, and the separator column is discarded.
d492a647: Find the single “seed” cell whose color is neither black (0) nor gray (5). Then copy that color onto every black cell that lies on the same checkerboard offset as the seed—i.e. all cells whose row parity and column parity match the seed’s, so the colored cells repeat every other row and every other column. Gray cells and all nonmatching black cells stay unchanged.
d4a91cb9: The input contains exactly two non-background cells: a red `2` and a blue `8`. The output connects them with a yellow `4` L-shaped path whose corner is at the intersection of the red cell’s row and the blue cell’s column. In other words, draw a horizontal segment from the red cell across to the blue’s column, then a vertical segment from there to the blue cell, while leaving the original `2` and `8` unchanged.
d4b1c2b1: The input grid is enlarged uniformly, keeping exactly the same color pattern and layout. The enlargement factor is the number of distinct colors present in the input: each original cell becomes an \(N \times N\) solid block of the same color, where \(N\) is the count of unique colors. Thus a monochrome input stays the same size, while inputs with 2, 3, 4, etc. colors are magnified by that factor in both dimensions.
d4c90558: Find each non-background, non-gray colored shape (each colored rectangular frame/block) and count how many gray cells (color 5) lie inside its bounding rectangle. Then convert these counts into a small horizontal bar chart: one output row per colored shape, filled from the left with that shape’s own color repeated as many times as its gray-cell count, with the rest padded by black. The rows are ordered from the smallest count to the largest, and the output width equals the largest count.
d4f3cd78: Find the gray 5 rectangular frame that is missing one border cell, creating a single opening. Recolor the whole inside of that frame to 8, also color the missing/opening cell as 8, and then continue a straight 8-colored line outward through that opening until it reaches the edge of the grid. All remaining gray border cells and background stay unchanged.
d511f180: The output is the same grid as the input, with a global color swap between gray and light blue. Every cell of color 5 becomes 8, and every cell of color 8 becomes 5; all other colors and positions stay unchanged.
d56f2372: The input contains several separate colored shapes on a black background. Choose the one whose bounding-box image is perfectly symmetric under a left-right mirror, then crop out exactly that shape’s bounding rectangle. The output is this cropped patch, preserving the shape’s color and any black holes/background cells inside the rectangle.
d5c634a2:
The input contains several red T-shapes made of four cells: a 3-cell horizontal bar with one cell attached at its center, either above the bar or below it. Count how many are of each type: stem-above (“upward” T) versus stem-below (“downward” T).

The output is always a 3×6 summary grid. In the left 3×3 half, place one green cell for each upward T; in the right 3×3 half, place one blue cell for each downward T, filling the four corner slots in order: top-left, bottom-left, top-right, bottom-right.
d5d6de2d: Find each connected red shape and erase all red cells. If a red shape is a hollow rectangular frame, replace it by coloring its enclosed interior area green; if it has no interior (like a solid block), it simply disappears.
d631b094: The input contains black background cells and exactly one non-black color scattered arbitrarily. Ignore the positions of those colored cells, count how many there are, and output a single horizontal row of that same color with exactly that many cells. In other words, the 2D pattern is compressed into a 1×N bar encoding only the color and its frequency.
d6542281: The grid contains some multi-color connected shapes and some smaller “anchor” objects. Each large shape has one distinctive color that also appears in one or more anchor objects elsewhere; for every such anchor, copy the entire large shape to that location, aligning the copied shape so that the cell of the shared color in the shape lands on the matching-colored cell in the anchor. If a small object’s rare color does not match any large shape, that small object is erased; otherwise the original shapes and anchors remain, with the new copies added.
d687bc17: The grid has a colored rectangular frame: each side is a solid color, with black corners. Any interior cell whose color matches one of the four side colors is moved straight toward that matching side, keeping its row for left/right moves or its column for top/bottom moves, until it sits in the first interior cell adjacent to the frame. All other interior colored cells disappear, and the frame itself stays unchanged.
d6ad076f: The input contains two non-background rectangular blocks separated by a strip of black cells, either side-by-side or one above the other. The output keeps both original blocks and fills the empty gap between them with a new rectangle of color 8. This added rectangle spans the full gap in the separation direction, but in the perpendicular direction it only covers the shared overlap of the two blocks, trimmed by one cell at each end (so it sits inset from the overlap’s boundaries).
d6e50e54: Find the single solid rectangular block of 1s and recolor it to 2s. Then take every 9 and “project” it straight toward that rectangle onto the nearest side: if its row overlaps the rectangle, place the 9 on the left/right edge line of the rectangle; otherwise place it on the top/bottom edge line using its column, and if it is farther away than the rectangle’s thickness, put it just outside that side instead of on the rectangle. All original 9s disappear from their old locations, and the 7 background stays unchanged.
d749d46f:
The input has a background color and one foreground color; in each column, the foreground occupies some number of cells from the top downward. Consecutive nonempty columns are treated as blocks, and each block is converted into a “corner” shape whose top arm length is the smaller of the block’s width and height and whose bottom arm length is the larger one.  

The output is always 10 rows tall: on a background canvas, these block-shapes are laid out left-to-right (with a one-column gap between blocks), drawn twice with the same foreground color — once growing downward from the top edge and once growing upward from the bottom edge. This creates matching top and bottom silhouettes separated by empty background space.
d753a70b: The grid contains several disconnected colored objects on a 7-background. Objects of colors 2 and 5 are always diamond/Manhattan-circle outlines; the task redraws each such object as the same diamond shape but centered at the unique lattice point from which its occupied cells form a complete equal-Manhattan-distance ring, effectively shifting the diamond by one cell when it is off-center. All other colored objects (such as 8 and 9) are copied unchanged.
d89b689b: Find the unique 2×2 square block in the grid and treat its four cells as target positions. The other colored single cells are removed from their original locations and their colors are copied into the square’s four cells according to relative position: the nearest/appropriate cell from the upper-left goes to the square’s upper-left cell, upper-right to upper-right, lower-left to lower-left, and lower-right to lower-right. Everything else becomes background.
d8c310e9: The grid contains a horizontally repeating motif that is shown only on the left side, with the rest of the row area blank. Complete the picture by continuing that same motif to the right across the full width of the grid, preserving the same horizontal period and allowing the final repetition to be cut off by the right edge. Empty rows remain unchanged.
d90796e8: The grid contains scattered red (2) and green (3) cells among unchanged background cells. Whenever a green cell is orthogonally adjacent to a red cell, that touching pair is transformed: the green cell becomes light blue (8) and the red cell disappears into background (0). Any red or green cell not touching the opposite color, as well as all other colors such as gray (5), stays unchanged.
d931c21c: Find each connected blue shape that forms a closed loop around at least one empty cell. For every such enclosed shape, keep the blue outline, fill its enclosed interior empty cells with green, and add a one-cell-thick red border in the empty cells immediately outside the shape. Blue shapes that do not enclose any interior region are left unchanged.
d93c6891: Treat color 4 as the base fill, and look at each connected block of color 7. If a 7-block touches some color-5 cells from one side, those 5s define a strip attached to the block. In the output, that attached strip is “absorbed” into the 7-block’s bounding rectangle: the touched side of the rectangle is recolored to 5 with the same thickness as the attached 5-strip, the rest of the rectangle stays 7, and all original 5 cells elsewhere are removed back to color 4.
d94c3b52:
The grid is partitioned into 3×3 motif slots separated by black lines. One slot contains a highlighted pattern in color 8; treat its shape as a template, and recolor every other slot with the same 3×3 pattern to color 8 as well.

Then, among these matching template slots, any non-template slot that lies strictly between two matched slots in the same row or in the same column is recolored to 7 (keeping its original shape, just changing its 1-cells to 7). All other cells remain unchanged.
d968ffd4: The input contains exactly two non-background colored blocks, separated either left-to-right or top-to-bottom. The output extends these two colors straight toward each other across the empty background gap, splitting the gap into two contiguous halves: one half is filled with the first block’s color and the other half with the second block’s color. In horizontal layouts this creates full-height vertical bands between the blocks; in vertical layouts it creates full-width horizontal bands between them.
d9f24cd1: The input contains some red seeds on the bottom row and some gray obstacle cells elsewhere. In the output, each bottom-row red seed grows straight upward as a red vertical path to the top. If such a path would hit a gray cell, the gray cell stays unchanged and the path shifts one column to the right at that height, then continues upward from the new column; this can repeat for multiple obstacles.
d9fac9be: The input uses exactly two non-background colors. One of them appears as a single cell that is completely surrounded in all 8 neighboring positions by the other color, forming a 3×3 “ring-around-center” pattern somewhere in the grid. The output is a 1×1 grid containing the color of that surrounded center cell.
da2b0fe3: Find the single full-width row or full-height column of background cells that lies inside the overall extent of the non-background pattern, acting as the gap between two parts of the figure. Recolor that entire empty separating line to green (3), extending it all the way across the grid in that row or column, while leaving the original colored shapes unchanged.
da6e95e5: The grid contains many small colored motifs on a uniform background; four copies of one color form matching L-shaped corner markers that frame a rectangular region. The task is to find the color whose four corner-marker motifs define such a frame, then extract the subgrid strictly inside that frame. The output is that interior patch, preserving its colors and any background cells inside it.
dae9d2b5: The 3×6 input is treated as two 3×3 pictures placed side by side. These two pictures are overlaid cell-by-cell into a single 3×3 output: if either half has a colored cell at a position, the output gets a 6 there; only positions that are black in both halves stay 0. The original colors (3 and 4) are ignored except as “occupied” cells, and all occupied cells become color 6.
db118e2a:
The input is a framed picture whose outer border has color 7 at the corners and another color along the edges. Remove that border, find the smallest box containing all non-7 cells inside, and take the centered 3×3 motif around it.  

Build a 5×5 tile from this motif by putting it in the middle, surrounding it with the edge-border color on the four sides, and setting the four tile corners to 7. Then place two identical copies of this 5×5 tile onto a 15×15 background of 7s: one near the upper-left and one near the lower-right.
db3e9e38: The input contains a single vertical orange line (color 7) starting at the top. Keep that line as the center spine, and extend it symmetrically left and right to form a triangular/pyramid shape whose width increases upward and whose side columns get one cell shorter each step away from the center. Color the added columns alternately 8, 7, 8, 7, … moving outward from the central 7-column, clipping the pattern at the grid edges.
db615bd4: The input contains a large hollow rectangular frame made of the most prominent non-background color, plus a few smaller monochrome rectangles elsewhere in the grid. The transformation erases those small rectangles from their original locations and packs them into the interior of the large frame, preserving each small rectangle’s size and color. If the frame is wider than tall, the pieces are arranged side-by-side in a centered horizontal row ordered by their original left-to-right positions; if the frame is taller than wide, they are stacked top-to-bottom in a centered vertical column ordered by their original top-to-bottom positions.
db7260a4: The grid contains one blue marker cell (1) above a red structure (2). Remove that marker, then look at the red connected component directly below its column: if that component is a closed “container” whose top edge spans across the marker’s column, fill its interior under that top edge with blue (1), between the two red vertical boundaries that bracket the marker’s column. If no such closed container exists, fill the entire bottom row with blue instead.
db93a21d:
The input contains several solid color-9 rectangles on a 0 background. In the output, each rectangle is given a one-cell-thick color-3 border whose thickness depends on the rectangle size: a 2×2 block gets one border ring, a 4×4 block gets two nested border rings, a 6×6 block gets three, etc.  

Additionally, from every original 9-cell, a vertical color-1 line is extended straight downward through the background until it hits the bottom of the grid or another non-background structure.
dbc1a6ce: The input contains isolated dark-blue cells (1) on a black background. In the output, any row that has two or more 1s gets a horizontal light-blue line (8) filling the gap between the leftmost and rightmost 1, and any column that has two or more 1s gets a vertical light-blue line filling the gap between the topmost and bottommost 1. The original 1s stay unchanged, and only the empty cells between aligned 1s are turned into 8s.
dc1df850: Each red cell (color 2) gets a one-cell halo: all 8 neighboring positions around it are turned blue (color 1), forming a 3×3 block centered on the red cell. This only fills empty/background cells, so existing nonzero cells stay unchanged, and halos are clipped naturally at the grid edges.
dc2aa30b: The grid is split by the full 0-valued separator rows and columns into nine 3×3 tiles. For each tile, count how many cells are color 2, then sort the tiles from fewest 2s to most 2s. Rebuild the 3×3 arrangement with that sorted order placed right-to-left across each row (smallest in the top-right, largest in the bottom-left), keeping each tile unchanged internally and preserving the 0 separators.
dc2e9a9d: Each green object is a hollow rectangular frame with one missing side-cell pattern that makes it behave like a frame with a single “tab” sticking out on one side (top, bottom, left, or right). For each such object, add a reflected copy of the frame-only shape on the opposite side of the original, leaving a one-cell gap; the tab itself is not copied. Use color 1 for copies reflected horizontally (objects whose tab is on the left or right) and color 8 for copies reflected vertically (tab on the top or bottom), while keeping the original green shapes unchanged.
dc433765: The grid contains exactly two non-background cells: a green/teal `3` and a yellow `4`. Keep the `4` fixed, and move the `3` by exactly one step toward the `4`—one row, one column, or one diagonal step depending on their relative positions. All other cells remain unchanged.
dc46ea44:
The grid is split by a horizontal bar of 4s across the middle. Everything below that bar is cleared, and the lower-half pattern is copied into the upper half after shifting it upward.

The 6-colored cells keep their relative shape but are moved straight up so their topmost cell sits at the top row. The other non-background object (the only color besides 4, 6, and 7) is moved upward by the same amount and also shifted horizontally so that its position relative to the leftmost column of the main 6-column/diagonal structure is preserved.
dce56571: The input contains a background color and one foreground color. Count how many foreground cells there are, erase the original shape, and replace it with a single horizontal bar of that same color whose length equals that count. This bar is centered horizontally and placed on the middle row of the grid.
dd2401ed: The input contains a full-height vertical gray bar (5). Move this bar rightward to a new full-height column whose horizontal offset from the old bar equals the farthest distance of any blue cell (1) to the left of the bar; equivalently, the new bar is placed just to the right of the mirror position of the leftmost blue-side extent. The old gray column is erased, and if every such left-side blue offset is matched by a red cell (2) at the same offset on the right side before the new bar, then those in-between red cells are recolored to blue (1); otherwise all non-bar cells stay unchanged.
ddf7fa4f: The top row contains a few single colored “key” cells, while the rest of the grid contains several separate gray (5) blocks. Each gray connected block is recolored uniformly with the color of the top-row key whose column falls inside that block’s horizontal span (equivalently, the block shares a column with that key). Everything else, including the top-row key cells and the background, stays unchanged.
de1cd16c: The input consists of several large uniform color blocks, each containing some scattered “intruder” cells of one other common marker color. Determine which block contains the most such intruder cells, then output a 1×1 grid colored with that block’s main/background color. If every block has a different main color, the intruder color is the one repeated across blocks; if all intruders are different, they are the rare colors inside the blocks.
de493100: The grid contains a rectangular block of 7s marking a missing patch inside an otherwise symmetric design. Recover that patch by taking the corresponding cells from the intact pattern under the appropriate reflection of the whole image: if needed, use the left-right mirror, top-bottom mirror, or diagonal transpose so the surrounding symmetry is respected. The output is just the recovered contents of the 7-rectangle, cropped to that rectangle’s size.
ded97339: Treat the non-background cells (the 8s) as anchor points to be connected. In every row that contains multiple 8s, fill the entire horizontal span from the leftmost to the rightmost 8 with 8s; likewise, in every column that contains multiple 8s, fill the full vertical span from the topmost to the bottommost 8. Any 8 that has no partner in its row or column simply remains as an isolated point.
df8cc377: Find the hollow rectangular frames (single-color outlines) and ignore all other scattered nonzero cells except as a source of fill colors. Erase everything except the frames, then fill each frame’s interior with a checkerboard pattern of one scattered color, starting at the interior’s top-left cell. The fill colors are assigned by matching rarer scattered colors to smaller frames and more frequent scattered colors to larger frames.
df978a02:
Treat the largest non-background colored shape as the “main” object. It is extended by adding a 3-cell cap on the side of the object that faces away from the overall center of all colored cells, aligned with that object’s outermost tip.

For every other colored object, remove the single cell at its tip that points toward the overall center. In effect, the biggest shape grows outward by one 3-pixel bar, while each smaller shape loses its inward-facing endpoint.
df9fd884: The grid contains two separate yellow (4) corner-like shapes and one small non-background colored object. That colored object is removed from its original place and copied into the empty rectangular corner region of the *other* yellow shape, preserving its own shape and orientation. In other words, the non-4 object “jumps” from one yellow frame/corner to the matching missing-corner area of the opposite yellow frame, and its old cells become background (7).
e048c9ed: The grid contains a single marker cell colored 5 in the top row; its column is used as an output column. For each horizontal nonzero bar below it, place one new cell in that same row at the marker’s column, encoding the bar’s length as a digit: 1 for length 2, 4 for length 3, 9 for length 4, and 6 for length 5. If a bar is nested inside longer bars spanning the same horizontal interval, its encoded value is increased by 1 for each such containing bar, but never beyond the maximum allowed by its own length.
e0fb7511: The grid is mostly color 1 with scattered black cells (0). Find each orthogonally connected group of black cells: if a group has more than one cell, recolor the whole group to 8; if a black cell is isolated as a single-cell group, leave it 0. All non-black cells stay unchanged.
e133d23d: The 7-column input is split into three parts: a left 3×3 pattern, a middle separator column of 4s, and a right 3×3 pattern. The output is a 3×3 overlay of the left and right parts: a cell becomes red (2) if either corresponding cell in the two side blocks is non-black, and stays black (0) only when both side cells are black. In effect, it removes the divider and takes the union of the two side patterns, recolored into red/black.
e179c5f4: The input is a blank rectangle with one blue cell at a corner; the output keeps the same size but turns the background to color 8. A single color-1 cell is then placed in every row so that, from the starting corner, it moves one column sideways per row while progressing upward, reflecting off the left/right borders to make a zigzag “bouncing diagonal” path. Thus the result is an all-8 grid with a 1-valued path snaking between the side walls.
e1baa8a4: The input is a tiling of solid-colored rectangles arranged in a regular row-by-column block layout, where each block may have different size but is uniform in color. The output compresses this mosaic by replacing each rectangular block with a single cell of the same color. Thus, the output is the small “macro-grid” of block colors, preserving the original top-to-bottom and left-to-right arrangement.
e1d2900e: The grid contains fixed 2×2 blocks of color 2 and scattered single cells of color 1. Each 1 that lies on the same row as one of a block’s two rows, or on the same column as one of its two columns, is pulled straight toward the nearest such 2×2 block and relocated to the empty cell immediately touching that block on that side. If a 1 is not aligned with any 2×2 block, or if its nearest target is ambiguous, it stays where it is; the 2-blocks themselves do not move.
e2092e0c: The top-left 3×3 block acts as a template and appears exactly twice in the grid: once at the corner and once somewhere else. At the second occurrence, the output draws a hollow 5×5 square border of color 5 centered on that copied 3×3 block, so the copied block becomes the interior of the frame. Only the border cells are changed to 5; everything else stays the same.
e21a174a: The input contains several separate non-background colored shapes stacked from top to bottom. The output keeps every shape exactly the same in color, form, and horizontal placement, but reverses their vertical order: the bottom shape is moved to the top, the next one above it becomes second, and so on. The shapes are re-stacked tightly in this reversed sequence on the same-sized blank background.
e21d9049: The input contains a small colored cross: one horizontal run and one vertical run intersecting at a single cell. The transformation extends these two seed lines to the full width and full height of the grid, repeating their color order cyclically so the entire row and the entire column become periodic continuations of the original pattern. All other cells remain background.
e26a3af2: The input is a noisy picture made of full-length color stripes, either vertical bands or horizontal bands. For each line in the stripe direction (columns if the bands are vertical, rows if they are horizontal), take its most frequent color and replace the whole line with that color. This removes all scattered off-color noise and restores clean uniform stripes across the grid.
e345f17b: The 4×8 input is treated as two side-by-side 4×4 panels. Compare the two panels cell by cell, and produce a single 4×4 output marking only the positions where both panels have a black cell (0): those shared black positions become yellow (4). Every other position in the output is black (0).
e3497940: The input is split by the vertical column of 5s, which serves as a divider and is removed. Take the right half, mirror it left-right, and overlay its non-background colored cells onto the left half. The output is this 4-column combined image: a compact superposition of the left-side pattern and the reflected right-side pattern.
e39e9282: The grid contains 3×3 colored blocks (notably color 6 and color 5) plus isolated 9s on the background. Keep only the 3×3 blocks of color 6; erase every 3×3 block of color 5 and also erase any 9 that was touching a 6-block. For each erased adjacent 9, place a new 9 inside the neighboring 3×3 block: if the block is color 6, move the 9 to the cell on that block’s middle row or middle column on the side facing the original 9; if the block is color 5, replace the whole block by background except for a 9 placed in the edge/corner cell of the former block nearest that touching side.
e3f79277: The input contains a small non-background shape tucked into one corner; its color and corner determine the output. The output is a larger grid, filled with the original background color, where that corner is replaced by a much larger right-triangular “fractal” version of the seed: a Sierpiński-triangle-like pattern anchored to the same corner and oriented inward from it. The non-background color is preserved, and the triangle’s orientation flips appropriately depending on whether the seed was in the left/right and top/bottom edge.
e3fe1151: The 5×5 grid is split by the central all-7 row and column into four 2×2 corner quadrants. In each quadrant, exactly one cell is a 7; that 7 is replaced by whichever non-7 color is underrepresented in the whole grid, so that each non-7 color ends up appearing about equally often across the four quadrants. All other cells stay unchanged.
e4075551: Find the unique red cell (2) and the four other colored single cells around it. The highest, lowest, leftmost, and rightmost of those four define the top, bottom, left, and right sides of an axis-aligned rectangle; each side is drawn across the grid in that defining cell’s color. Then draw a gray (5) vertical line from the red cell to the top/bottom sides and a gray horizontal line from the red cell to the left/right sides, forming a plus sign inside the rectangle, while keeping the red center unchanged.
e40b9e2f: Find the central non-background shape and treat it as the symmetry center. Any extra colored cells or protrusions that appear on only one side/corner of this shape are copied to the three corresponding positions obtained by reflecting across the shape’s horizontal and vertical center lines (equivalently, making 4-way symmetry around the center). The original pattern stays in place; the output just completes the missing symmetric counterparts.
e41c6fd3: Treat each nonzero color as one separate object, and use the color-8 object as the vertical reference. Move every object straight up or down so that its top row aligns with the top row of the 8-colored object, while keeping each object’s horizontal position and shape unchanged. The output is otherwise blank, so all objects end up arranged in one horizontal band at the 8-object’s height.
e45ef808: The grid contains a descending boundary between a top region of 1s and a bottom region of 6s. The output highlights two special columns of 1-cells above that boundary: paint the entire column aligned with the **rightmost 6 in the topmost row containing any 6** as color 4, and paint the entire column aligned with the **rightmost 1 in the bottommost row containing any 1** as color 9. Only the 1-cells in those columns are recolored; all 6s and 0s stay unchanged.
e4888269: The grid contains a special 2-column-wide vertical strip of adjacent non-background cells that acts as a lookup table: each row in that strip gives a color substitution rule “left color → right color.” All other non-background cells elsewhere in the grid are recolored by following this rule chain as far as possible (so a color may change through several linked substitutions to its final descendant). The lookup-table strip itself and the background stay unchanged.
e48d4e1a: The input contains a full-row/full-column cross in one color, plus some gray marker cells (color 5) stacked at the top of the rightmost column. Count those gray cells, erase them, and use that count as the shift amount: move the cross’s intersection diagonally down-left by that many cells. Equivalently, redraw the vertical line that many columns to the left and the horizontal line that many rows lower, keeping the cross’s original color.
e4941b18: The grid contains a solid rectangle of 5s, plus a single 2 and a single 8 elsewhere above it. In the output, the 2 is moved onto the 8’s original cell, and both original marker cells are otherwise cleared back to background 7. The 8 is then placed just outside the bottom edge of the 5-rectangle: one column to the right of the rectangle if the 2 originally lay left of the 8, or one column to the left if the 2 originally lay right of the 8.
e5062a87: Identify the shape formed by the rarest color in the grid (the red/orange 2s) and treat it as a template, preserving its exact orientation. Then scan the whole grid for every translated copy of that same shape made entirely of black cells (0), and recolor those matching black cells to 2. All other cells remain unchanged.
e509e548:
Find each connected green shape and recolor it according to its overall form, leaving its geometry and position unchanged.  
Shapes that exactly trace the border of their bounding rectangle (a hollow rectangular frame) become magenta/6; among the remaining shapes, vertically oriented ones (taller than wide) become blue/1, and horizontally oriented ones (wider than tall) become red/2.  
Background cells stay 0.
e50d258f: The input contains several disjoint non-black blocks separated by background 0s. Among these blocks, choose the connected block that contains the largest number of red cells (color 2). Output that block exactly as it appears in the input, cropped to its tight bounding rectangle.
e57337a4: The 15×15 input is a uniform non-black color with some black (0) cells scattered in it. Split the input into a 3×3 array of 5×5 blocks, then compress it to a 3×3 output where each output cell represents one block: it is black if that 5×5 block contains any black cell, otherwise it stays the background color. Thus the output is a coarse map of which large regions contain black pixels.
e5790162: Starting from the single green cell (3), draw a continuous green orthogonal path that initially goes to the right through empty cells. Whenever this path reaches a nonzero marker cell, it stops just before that marker, keeps the marker unchanged, and turns 90°: at a 6 it turns clockwise, and at an 8 it turns counterclockwise. Continue extending the green path through zeros until the next marker or, after the last turn, until the path runs out to the grid edge.
e5c44e8f: Starting from the single green cell, extend a green path in a clockwise square spiral: go up 2, right 2, down 4, left 4, up 6, right 6, and so on, increasing the run length by 2 after every two turns. Paint each visited in-bounds empty cell green while leaving all existing red cells unchanged. Stop the entire spiral as soon as the next step would land on a red cell; if no red cell is hit, it continues outward until it can no longer add any in-bounds cells.
e619ca6e: Each non-background connected shape is kept in place and treated as the apex of a downward “mountain.” The same shape is then copied repeatedly in two diagonal directions: one copy chain goes down-left and the other goes down-right, with each step moving down by the shape’s height and sideways by the shape’s width. This continues until no part of a new copy fits in the grid; copies that extend past the border are clipped.
e633a9e5: The task enlarges a 3×3 grid into a 5×5 grid by duplicating the outer rows and outer columns while leaving the middle row and middle column single. As a result, each corner cell becomes a 2×2 block, the top and bottom middle cells become vertical 2×1 strips, the left and right middle cells become horizontal 1×2 strips, and the center cell stays 1×1. The output preserves the same 3×3 arrangement, just with this non-uniform expansion away from the center.
e6721834: The input is split into two equal halves by a full-width/full-height separator: one half is a sparse “target” half containing only isolated colored marker cells on a uniform background, while the other half contains larger connected template shapes built around those same marker colors. The output is the target half alone, keeping its background and marker cells, but each isolated marker is expanded by copying the matching template from the other half and aligning the template so its marker-colored cells land on the corresponding target markers. In effect, the puzzle transfers the surrounding shape/enclosure from the template half onto the matching dots in the sparse half.
e681b708: The nonzero/non-blue cells on the long blue line structure act as labeled corners/endpoints of rectangular “rooms” whose walls are made of 1s. Any isolated 1 that is not part of those wall lines is recolored according to the room it lies in: look at the non-1 marker colors on that room’s surrounding wall/corners, and change the interior 1 to the majority marker color for that region. The wall lines and the existing colored markers stay unchanged.
e69241bd: Treat 5s as walls and all other nonzero colors as seed cells. Each seed color spreads orthogonally through every connected 0-cell region reachable from that seed, recoloring those black cells with the seed’s color while leaving the seed itself and all 5s unchanged. Any 0s not connected to a colored seed remain 0.
e6de6e8f:
The 2×12 input is read as a 1D sequence of movement symbols, where each nonblank column (or short run of adjacent nonblank columns) encodes a step direction for a falling path: one symbol means “shift left,” the other means “shift right,” and isolated blank separators mean “just continue downward.”  
The output is always an 8×7 grid with a fixed starting marker `3` at the top center; beneath it, `2`s draw the trajectory of a falling 2-cell-wide piece following those encoded left/right steps. As the piece descends, horizontal moves are shown by adjacent pairs of `2`s, and after the encoded turns are exhausted, the path continues straight downward as a single vertical column.
e729b7be: The grid contains a full vertical divider of 4s and a special row marked by 8s at the far left and right; their intersection defines a center point. Copy every non-4/non-8 cell on the right side of the divider by taking the color from the cell that is opposite through this center point (a 180° rotation of the left-side pattern). In effect, the colored figure on the left is reproduced on the right as a half-turn around the crossing of the 4-column and the 8-row, while the 4s, 8s, and background stay unchanged.
e73095fd: The grid contains gray (5) line drawings that sometimes form complete rectangular frames, possibly with extra gray lines attached outside. Copy the picture unchanged, but whenever a gray connected component contains a fully closed axis-aligned rectangle, fill that rectangle’s interior background cells with yellow (4). Open shapes and outside background remain unchanged.
e734a0e8: The zero rows and zero columns partition the picture into rectangular tiles. One tile contains a colored motif made of some color other than 0 or 7, while certain other tiles are mostly 7s but include one or more stray 0 cells inside them. Copy the motif tile exactly into every such marked tile, leaving the separator lines and all unmarked tiles unchanged.
e74e1818: Each nonzero color is treated as its own separate shape. For each color, look at the vertical span from its topmost to bottommost row and flip that shape upside down within that span, i.e. reverse the order of the rows containing that color while keeping the columns and overall location unchanged. Background cells stay the same.
e760a62e: The blue 8s form a fixed lattice that divides the picture into rectangular cells/blocks. Any block containing a red 2 or green 3 seed is treated as a colored block, and for each color separately, if two or more such blocks lie in the same block-row or block-column, all blocks between the outermost ones are filled in to connect them. Blocks reached only by red become 2, only by green become 3, and blocks where the red and green connections overlap become 6; the blue grid lines stay unchanged.
e7639916: Find all light-blue cells (color 8) and determine the smallest axis-aligned rectangle that contains them all. Draw that rectangle’s border as a 1-cell-thick dark-blue frame (color 1), while leaving the original 8 cells unchanged so they appear embedded in the frame. All other cells stay the same.
e76a88a6: Find the one multicolored non-background object: this is the source pattern. Every other nonzero object is a solid color-5 rectangle of the same size, and each such rectangle is replaced by an exact copy of the source pattern, aligned by its top-left corner. The original pattern and the black background remain unchanged.
e78887d1:
The input contains one or more 3-row “bands” of the same set of colored 3×3 symbols, separated by blank rows. Read the symbols left-to-right by their color positions, then make a new single 3-row band in which each position keeps its original color but takes on the shape/pixel pattern of the next symbol to the right, with wraparound from the last back to the first.

If a copied shape is incomplete in one band, use another band where that same shape appears completely to reconstruct it. The output is this single reconstructed, cyclically shape-shifted band.
e7a25a18: Find the rectangle outlined in color 2 and crop the grid to that framed region. Inside the frame, the nonzero/non-border colors form a tiny 2×2 template (possibly as single cells or small blocks); expand that template so the framed interior is split into four equal quadrants, each quadrant filled uniformly with the color from the corresponding template position (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right). Keep the color-2 border unchanged.
e7b06bea: Keep the left-side vertical bar of color 5 unchanged; its height tells you the run length. The solid colored columns on the far right are collapsed into a single column placed immediately to the left of that right-hand block. Fill this new column from top to bottom by cycling through the original right-side colors in left-to-right order, repeating each color for as many rows as the height of the 5-bar; all other non-5 cells become 0.
e7dd8335: Find each connected shape made of color 1, treating diagonally touching 1s as part of the same shape. For each shape, determine its top and bottom rows, and recolor to 2 every cell of that shape that lies strictly below the vertical midpoint of the shape’s height.
