The upper half of each 1-shape stays unchanged, and the background remains 0.
e84fef15:
The 29×29 input is a 5×5 array of 5×5 tiles, with full rows and columns of color 3 acting as separators. Almost all tiles are identical; exactly one tile is a corrupted version of the common tile.

The output is the common 5×5 tile pattern itself, but any cell where the odd tile differs from that common pattern is replaced by color 1. In other words, it shows the standard tile and marks the corruption locations.
e8593010: The grid has a gray background (5) with scattered black cells (0). Group the black cells into connected components using 8-way adjacency (touching by edge or corner), then recolor each whole component by its size: single-cell components become 3, two-cell components become 2, and three-cell components become 1. All gray cells stay unchanged.
e872b94a: Identify the separate gray (color 5) blobs in the input, treating diagonally touching cells as connected and ignoring the black background. Count how many such gray objects there are, then output a 1-column black grid whose height equals that count plus one. The output contains only black (0) cells.
e88171ec: Find the largest axis-aligned rectangle made entirely of black cells (color 0). Recolor only its interior—everything except the outermost one-cell border—to light blue (color 8), leaving that black frame intact. All other cells remain unchanged.
e8dc4411: The grid contains two foreground colors: a larger black shape and a single colored “marker” cell touching it diagonally. Use that touching diagonal direction as a translation vector, and repeatedly place shifted copies of the black shape in that same direction, coloring the copies with the marker’s color while leaving the original black shape unchanged. Continue propagating the copies until they run off the grid.
e9614598: The input contains two non-background cells of color 1, aligned either horizontally or vertically. The output keeps those cells unchanged and adds a color-3 plus sign centered at the midpoint between them. The plus is the 5-cell cross made of the center cell and its four orthogonal neighbors.
e98196ab: The input is split into two equal 5-row pictures by a solid divider line of color 5 across the middle. Remove that divider, then superimpose the lower 5-row picture onto the upper 5-row picture, aligning corresponding rows and columns. The output is this single 5×11 merged image containing all non-background cells from both halves.
e99362f0:
The yellow 4s form a central cross that splits the input into four equal 5×4 quadrants. Each quadrant contains one foreground color only: 7 in the top-left, 9 in the top-right, 2 in the bottom-left, and 8 in the bottom-right.

The output is a single 5×4 grid made by superimposing those four quadrants cell-by-cell at matching positions, ignoring the divider. If multiple quadrants occupy the same position, the color priority is 8 highest, then 7, then 9, then 2; if all four cells are 0, the output cell is 0.
e9ac8c9e: Find each connected rectangle of gray cells (color 5). Look at the four single colored cells placed one step diagonally outside its four corners, then replace the gray rectangle by splitting it into four equal quadrants and filling each quadrant with the color from the corresponding corner marker (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right). The output is otherwise blank/background, so the original markers and all non-filled cells disappear.
e9afcf9a: The input is a 2-row grid where each row is a solid color. The output keeps the same size and the same two colors, but turns the two solid bands into an alternating checkerboard-like pattern across columns: the top row starts with its original color and alternates with the bottom row’s color, while the bottom row does the opposite. In other words, every other column is swapped between the two rows.
e9b4f6fc: Find the largest non-black connected object and crop the grid to its bounding box; this cropped region is the output shape. Elsewhere in the input, there are isolated 1-cell objects that occur in horizontal adjacent pairs, and each such pair defines a color substitution from the right cell’s color to the left cell’s color. Apply those substitutions inside the large cropped object, leaving any colors not mentioned (typically the surrounding frame/background color of the large object) unchanged.
e9bb6954: Each solid monochrome 3×3 square in the input acts as a source. For every such square, extend its color across the whole grid along the square’s middle row and middle column, creating a full horizontal and vertical line through its center. If lines from different-colored squares cross, that crossing cell is cleared to black; everything else stays as drawn or originally present.
e9c9d9a1: The green 3-cells form a rectangular lattice that partitions the board into rectangular regions. Keep all green grid lines unchanged, then color the four corner regions with fixed colors: top-left = 2, top-right = 4, bottom-left = 1, bottom-right = 8. Every region fully enclosed away from the outer border (i.e. not touching the grid’s outside edge) is filled with 7, while the remaining edge regions stay 0.
e9fc42f2: Treat each non-background connected cluster as a separate “tile” cut out by its bounding box; these tiles contain mostly 1s on a 2 background plus one special marker color such as 3, 4, or 8. The special non-{1,2} colors act like matching connectors: tiles that share the same marker are parts of one chain. Reconstruct that chain by placing the tiles edge-to-edge so that matching marker cells coincide at the touching borders, then crop to the overall bounding box of the assembled figure.
ea32f347: The input contains several separate gray connected components on a black background. Keep each component’s shape and position unchanged, but recolor them according to their size: the largest component becomes blue (1), the smallest becomes red (2), and all remaining gray components become yellow (4). Background cells stay black.
ea786f4a: The input is an odd-sized square filled with one color except for a single different-colored cell at the exact center. In the output, that center cell’s color is extended along both diagonals, reaching all four corners and forming an X shape. All other cells remain unchanged.
ea959feb: The input is a globally repeating wallpaper-like color pattern, but some cells have been overwritten so the repetition is broken, usually by patches of color 1. The task is to infer the underlying periodic pattern from the intact parts and restore the whole grid so every row and column position fits that repeating tile again. In other words, remove the corrupted/masked cells and replace them with the colors required by the repeating background pattern.
ea9794b1: The 10×10 input is split into four 5×5 quadrants, each using a characteristic color: top-left has 4s, top-right has 3s, bottom-left has 9s, and bottom-right has 8s. These four quadrants are then overlaid cell-by-cell to make a single 5×5 output. When multiple quadrants contribute a nonzero color at the same position, the visible color is chosen by priority: 3 overrides 9, 9 overrides 8, 8 overrides 4, and 0 is only used if all four cells are 0.
eb281b96: The input is treated as a vertical motif and extended downward by reflecting its row order: after the original rows, append the same rows in reverse order but without repeating the last row. This creates one up-and-down vertical palindrome. Then continue the pattern once more, again avoiding a duplicated boundary row, so the final output is two such mirrored stretches joined together into one continuous vertically repeating wave/diamond pattern.
eb5a1d5d: The input is a set of concentric, solid-colored rectangular layers: a background color, then one or more nested inner rectangles, possibly with repeated colors at different depths. The output compresses this nesting into the smallest possible square diagram, where each original layer becomes a 1-cell-thick concentric ring in the same outer-to-inner order. Thus, if there are \(n\) layers, the result is a \((2n-1)\times(2n-1)\) square with the innermost layer reduced to the center cell.
ec883f72:
The input contains two non-background colors: a larger enclosing shape/frame and a smaller object of another color. Keep the whole picture unchanged, then use the smaller object’s color to draw outward 45° diagonals from the enclosing shape’s corners: top-left shoots up-left, top-right up-right, bottom-left down-left, and bottom-right down-right.  
Only the corners that are not already on the grid boundary produce a diagonal, and each diagonal continues until it reaches the edge of the grid.
ecaa0ec1: Find the single non-black 3×3 block made of colors 1 and 8, and rotate that block by a multiple of 90°. The rotation is chosen by comparing two directions: the corner of the block that already has a nearby diagonal 4, and the quadrant indicated by the other scattered 4s; rotate so those directions line up. Then erase all original 4s and keep only one 4, placed at the corresponding outer corner of the rotated block.
ecb67b6d: Treat the majority color (7) as background and look at each connected region of the other color (5), using diagonal connectivity. Any 5-region that contains a straight diagonal run of at least three of its own cells—either slanting `\` or `/`—is recolored entirely from 5 to 8. All other cells, especially the 7s and any 5-regions without such a 3-cell diagonal, stay unchanged.
ecdecbb3: The grid contains one or more full-length blue divider lines (all 8s, either vertical or horizontal) and some isolated red cells (2s). For each red cell, draw a straight red segment perpendicular to the divider line(s) until it reaches the nearest blue line in that direction; if the red cell lies between two parallel blue lines, connect it to both sides. At each contact point, the touched blue cell becomes red and its 8 neighboring cells are colored blue, creating a 3×3 blue halo around the red endpoint.
ed36ccf7: The task rotates the entire input picture 90° counterclockwise. Every cell keeps its color, but its position shifts as if the whole 3×3 grid were turned one quarter-turn to the left.
ed74f2f2: The input contains two separate 3×3 gray patterns: one on the left and one on the right. The output is just the right-hand 3×3 pattern, keeping its shape exactly, but recoloring all gray cells according to which symbol appears on the left: if the left pattern has gray in both top corners it becomes color 1, if it has gray only in the top-left corner it becomes color 2, and if it has no gray in the top-left corner it becomes color 3. Black cells stay black.
ed98d772: The output is a 2×2 tiling of the entire input pattern, so a 3×3 input becomes a 6×6 grid. The four quadrants contain the input in its four orientations: original at top-left, 90° counterclockwise at top-right, 180° at bottom-left, and 90° clockwise (270° counterclockwise) at bottom-right.
edcc2ff0:
The grid has a small 7-row header above a larger picture. The three single colored cells in the header (at rows 1, 3, and 5) act as selected colors: in the lower picture, every other non-background color is erased and replaced by the picture’s background, while objects of the selected colors are kept.

Then each header marker is expanded horizontally to show how many separate connected objects of that same color remain in the lower picture: row 1 shows that many copies of the first header color, row 3 does the same for the second, and row 5 for the third. If a selected color does not appear below, its header row becomes blank.
ef135b50: The grid contains several solid red/orange (2) rectangles. Whenever two such rectangles lie on the same row with a horizontal gap between them, that gap is bridged by filling the intervening background cells with dark red/brown (9), effectively connecting the rectangles across their overlapping rows. All original 2-cells stay unchanged; only the empty cells between horizontally aligned red blocks are recolored.
ef26cbf6: The yellow 4-cells form full divider lines that split the grid into rectangular compartments. In each compartment row/column pairing, one compartment contains a single non-blue colored “sample” cell, while the opposite compartment contains a shape made of blue 1-cells. The transformation recolors every blue cell in that shape to match the color of the sample cell in the corresponding compartment across the divider, leaving the dividers, background, and sample cells unchanged.
f0100645: The grid has a dominant background color, plus a solid color on the left edge and another solid color on the right edge. Any interior connected blobs of the left-edge color are shifted horizontally left as far as possible, and any interior blobs of the right-edge color are shifted horizontally right as far as possible, without overlapping each other or crossing the border. Their original interior positions are cleared back to the background color, so the result looks like those colored pieces have “slid” toward their matching side.
f0afb749: The output is a 2× enlargement of the input grid: each nonzero input cell becomes a solid 2×2 block of its original color in the corresponding doubled position, while the background stays black. In addition, each colored cell generates a blue diagonal “trail” continuing down-right from its position: for every empty input cell lying on the same down-right diagonal below-right of a colored cell, place a 2×2 block containing blue only on its main diagonal (`[[1,0],[0,1]]`). Thus the enlarged colored squares stay fixed, and blue diagonal markers appear only in the empty cells along their southeast diagonals.
f0df5ff0: Each blue cell (color 1) expands to its full 3×3 neighborhood: the center plus all 8 surrounding cells. Any black cell (0) in those neighborhoods is turned blue, while cells that already contain some other nonzero color are left unchanged. All expansions are based only on the original blue cells, so newly added blue cells do not expand further.
f0f8a26d: The input contains several disconnected monochrome objects on a uniform background. Any object that is already a perfect horizontal or vertical line is turned into a centered line of the same length in the opposite orientation: horizontal segments become vertical segments, and vertical segments become horizontal segments. All other non-background objects are left unchanged, and the transformed/unchanged pieces are redrawn on a blank background of the original color.
f15e1fac: The non-background color 8 forms a seed pattern on one border, and color 2 marks “trigger” positions on the perpendicular border. Copy the 8-pattern step by step across the grid in the perpendicular direction, repeating the current row/column pattern until a line aligned with a 2 is reached; at each such trigger, shift the whole 8-pattern by one cell away from the 2-side and continue. Thus the output is a propagated, staircase-like repetition of the original 8 pattern, while the 2 cells remain fixed.
f18ec8cc:
The input is divided into contiguous vertical strips, where each strip is identified by its dominant color (allowing a few off-color noise cells inside each strip). The output rearranges these whole strips horizontally while preserving every strip’s internal pixel pattern.

If the strips’ dominant colors increase from left to right, the strip order is completely reversed. Otherwise, the strips are cyclically shifted left by one position, so the leftmost strip moves to the far right.
f1bcbc2c: The 7s form wall-like line structures that create one-cell-wide “corridors” of background cells. The task highlights one such corridor by filling it with 8s: if a 9 is present, it marks the corridor to use, and the fill follows the connected corridor cells adjacent to the 7-structure; if the 9 lies inside that corridor it is included in the fill, otherwise it stays unchanged. In the case with no 9, the same corridor-filling is done for the unique relevant corridor.
f1cefba8: The input contains a large solid rectangle of one color with a smaller inner rectangle of a second color, plus a few single-cell “bumps” of the inner color that protrude one cell into the outer band. Each such bump marks either a row or a column: extend that row/column across the whole grid. Outside the large rectangle the extension is drawn in the inner color, while the part passing through the large rectangle is recolored to the outer rectangle’s color, so the bump itself disappears and becomes part of a full cross-line.
f21745ec: The grid contains several single-color rectangular frames; exactly one of them already has a non-empty pattern inside its border. Use that rectangle’s interior as a template, ignoring its color but keeping the arrangement of filled vs. empty cells. Keep only rectangles whose interior dimensions match the template rectangle: the template rectangle stays as is, and every other same-sized frame gets the same interior pattern copied into it using that frame’s own color; all differently sized colored objects are erased.
f25fbde4: Crop away all-uniform border rows and columns so that only the rectangular region containing the figure remains. Then magnify that cropped rectangle by a factor of 2 in both height and width: every cell, including background 0s inside the crop, becomes a 2×2 block of the same color.
f25ffba3: The grid contains a colored pattern in only one half and empty background in the opposite half. Reflect the non-background pattern across the horizontal midline, copying it into the empty half while leaving the original half unchanged. In the shown examples, this means the bottom half is flipped vertically and placed into the top half, creating top-bottom symmetry.
f2829549: The input is split into two equal 4×3 panels by the solid divider line of 1s. Compare the left and right panels cell-by-cell, treating any nonzero cell in either panel as “occupied”: the output is a 4×3 grid where positions occupied on at least one side become 0, and positions that are 0 in both panels become 3. In effect, it overlays the two side patterns and marks only the cells empty in both with green.
f28a3cbb:
The grid has background color 6 and two other colors. For each non-background color, one of its occurrences is a solid 3×3 anchor block; all other cells of that color are treated as stray pieces that get “pulled” straight toward that 3×3 block from the outside, stopping when they touch the growing shape.  

So the output replaces the scattered pixels by compact enlargements of the two main color clusters: the top-left 3×3 block grows outward to absorb its same-colored outliers, and the lower-right 3×3 block does the same, while all unrelated scattered positions revert to background 6.
f341894c: The grid contains isolated adjacent `1`/`6` pairs, plus some `7`s that act as directional markers. For each horizontal or vertical `1`–`6` pair, look along that same row or column to the nearest `7`; if the pair is ordered so that the `1` is farther from the `7` than the `6`, swap the two colors, otherwise leave it unchanged. All other cells stay the same.
f35d900a:
The input has four single colored cells at the corners of a rectangle, using exactly two non-background colors. Each cell is expanded into a 3×3 motif centered on it: the center keeps its original color, while the eight surrounding cells are filled with the other nonzero color, so each point becomes a small opposite-colored frame.

Then add gray (5) dotted connectors along the rectangle’s sides: on the two rows through the centers and the two columns through the centers, place 5s in every other cell of the empty gap between the 3×3 motifs, starting just outside each motif and stopping before the next one.
f3b10344: Find each separate rectangular object and group objects by color. For every pair of same-colored objects that are aligned side-by-side or one-above-the-other, add a light-blue (8) rectangular “bridge” through the empty gap between them. The bridge is drawn only through the shared overlap band of the two objects, inset by one cell from each side of that overlap, so the original objects stay unchanged and only the connecting corridor is added.
f3cdc58f: The input’s scattered nonzero cells are reduced to just their color frequencies. In the output, those counts are redrawn as four bottom-aligned vertical bars in the leftmost four columns: color 1 in column 0, color 2 in column 1, color 3 in column 2, and color 4 in column 3. Each bar’s height equals how many times that color appeared in the input, and all other cells are 0.
f3e14006: The input contains one prominent nonzero row and one prominent nonzero column crossing each other. The output discards their original positions and rebuilds a compact rectangular pattern: rows alternate between a solid row of the row-line’s majority color and a patterned row whose cells are determined by the row-line’s minority colors, while the sequence of these patterned rows follows the non-majority values encountered along the column. The patterned block is placed near the center/right of the grid, with all other cells set to 0.
f3e62deb: The input contains a single nonzero object, typically a 3×3 hollow square, on a blank background. Its color determines a direction, and the whole shape is translated unchanged all the way to the corresponding grid edge: color 6 moves to the top, color 8 to the right, color 4 to the bottom, and any other color to the left. The shape keeps its orientation and its coordinate on the perpendicular axis; only its position shifts.
f45f5ca7: The input has isolated colored cells only in the leftmost column. In the output, each such cell is moved horizontally to a fixed destination column determined by its color, while staying in the same row: color 8 goes to column 1, color 2 to column 2, color 4 to column 3, and color 3 to column 4. The original leftmost column is cleared to black, and everything else remains black.
f5aa3634: The input contains several disconnected nonzero shapes on a black background, and exactly one of those shapes appears twice as an identical pattern. The task is to find that repeated shape and output a tight crop of one copy of it. The crop preserves the full rectangular bounding box of the shape, including any 0-cells inside that rectangle.
f5b8619d: Find every column of the input that contains at least one non-background colored cell. In those columns, turn all remaining background cells into color 8 while leaving the original colored cells unchanged; columns with no colored cell stay all background. Then tile this transformed grid in a 2×2 arrangement, doubling both width and height.
f5c89df1: The input contains one green cell (3), some blue cells (8) forming a shape around it, and one or more red cells (2). Treat the green cell as the reference point for the blue shape: record where every blue cell lies relative to the green cell, then reproduce that same blue pattern at each red cell so that each red cell takes the green cell’s role. The output is a blank background with only these copied blue shapes; the original blue shape and all red/green markers disappear.
f76d97a5: Each input uses exactly two colors: gray (5) and one other color. The gray cells define the shape to keep; in the output, those gray positions are recolored to the other input color, while every cell that originally had that other color is turned black (0). The grid size and the pattern of gray-cell positions stay unchanged.
f823c43c: The input is a noisy version of a 2‑color periodic wallpaper pattern, where color 6 is just corruption. Recover the underlying repeating tile by finding the smallest row/column period that makes all non‑6 cells consistent, then repaint the whole grid with that clean repetition. In effect, all 6s are removed and any other mismatches are corrected so the entire image becomes a perfectly regular 2‑color repeating pattern.
f83cb3f6: The grid contains a fixed barrier/axis made of color 8, either as a vertical segment or a horizontal segment. All other colored cells are erased from their original positions and “collapsed” toward this 8-axis: for each row of a vertical 8-line, place one cell immediately left of the 8 if that row had any colored cell on the left, and/or one immediately right if it had any on the right; analogously, for each column of a horizontal 8-line, place one cell immediately above/below the 8 if that column had any colored cell above/below. The 8 cells stay unchanged, and multiple cells on the same side collapse to a single adjacent cell.
f8a8fe49: The grid contains a red frame or pair of opposing red brackets (color 2) with a gray pattern (color 5) in the enclosed middle region. Keep the red structure fixed, erase the gray pattern from inside it, and copy that pattern to the outside on both opposite sides of the frame: for side-by-side vertical brackets, mirror the left half outward to the left and the right half outward to the right; for stacked horizontal brackets, mirror the upper half upward and the lower half downward. In other words, the red border acts like a pair of mirrors that flips the interior gray design outward.
f8b3ba0a: Remove the all-uniform separator rows and columns, then look only at the colors that remain. Ignore the background color 0 and also ignore the nonzero color that appears most often (the dominant filler color in the repeated blocks). Sort the remaining colors by decreasing frequency and output them as a single vertical column, top to bottom from most frequent to least frequent.
f8be4b64: Find each cell whose four orthogonal neighbors are green (3): this is the center of a green “plus”, and its own color is the color to propagate. From each such center, extend that color horizontally and vertically across the grid, filling empty cells until the path hits a green cell that is not one of that plus’s immediate arms (or the grid edge); the green cells themselves stay unchanged. If a horizontal and a vertical propagation overlap, the vertical color wins because vertical lines are drawn last.
f8c80d96: The input contains one colored shape and a second, translated copy of the same shape, revealing a fixed row/column offset between copies. The output continues that same translation repeatedly in both directions, stamping more copies of the shape at equal spacing so the pattern extends across the grid (clipped by the borders). Finally, every original colored cell is changed to gray 5, while the newly propagated copies are drawn in the original nonzero color.
f8cc533f: The grid contains several separate motifs, each drawn in a unique non-background color. For each color independently, its occupied cells are completed so that the shape becomes mirror-symmetric across both the horizontal and vertical midlines of its own bounding box. Equivalently, any cell is filled if any of its four positions related by left-right and/or top-bottom reflection is already present, so each motif is “completed” into a fully symmetric version of itself.
f8f52ecc: The grid contains scattered markers of several colors on a background of 1, plus color 8 cells that act as obstacles. For each color other than 1 and 8, connect its occurrences in sorted order by drawing straight segments between consecutive same-colored cells: horizontal, vertical, or diagonal when aligned. If a diagonal connection would pass through any 8-cell, use the other right-angle route between the two points instead, so the connection bends around the 8 barrier.
f8ff0b80:
The input contains several separate non-background colored shapes. Measure the size of each shape by its number of cells, then discard the shapes themselves and keep only their colors.  
The output is a 1-column grid listing those colors from top to bottom in order of largest shape to smallest shape.
f9012d9b: The input is a regularly repeating nonzero pattern with one rectangular patch replaced by black cells (0). Infer the grid’s horizontal and vertical repeat periods from the visible part, then reconstruct the hidden patch by continuing that periodic pattern into the black rectangle. The output is exactly that missing rectangular piece, cropped to the size of the 0-region.
f9a67cb5: The grid contains one seed cell colored 2 on the outer border, and a set of broken parallel 8-lines acting like barriers or dividers. Starting from the seed, color 2 extends straight inward perpendicular to the border until it reaches the first 8-line, then it “fans out” along the gap interval on that line (between the nearest 8s on each side, or to the edge if unbounded). From each newly reached endpoint, the same process repeats in the same inward direction, so the output is the network of 2s obtained by propagating through the successive gaps between the 8-structures.
f9d67f8b: The grid is meant to be symmetric across both the horizontal and vertical midlines, but some cells have been replaced by 9s, creating holes in that symmetry. The transformation removes every 9 by filling it with the matching color from the corresponding mirrored position in the completed symmetric pattern, using horizontal/vertical/180° counterparts as needed. The result is the same image with all 9s replaced and the full bilateral symmetry restored.
fafd9572: The grid contains a small “palette” made of isolated nonzero colored cells, plus several separate objects drawn entirely in color 1. Read the palette cells in top-to-bottom order, and read the color-1 objects in top-to-bottom/left-to-right order; then recolor each whole 1-object with the corresponding palette color. Everything else, including the palette itself, stays unchanged.
fafffa47: Split the 6×3 input into its top and bottom 3×3 halves and conceptually overlay them. The output is a 3×3 grid that marks, in color 2, exactly those positions that are black/background (0) in both halves; every other position is left black. In effect, it highlights the cells that remain empty when the two half-patterns are combined.
fb791726:
The output is a canvas twice as tall and twice as wide as the input. The entire input pattern is copied unchanged into the upper-left quadrant and again into the lower-right quadrant, leaving the other two quadrants empty.

Then, for every input row that is completely black but lies between two non-black rows, the corresponding row in each copy is replaced by a full-width horizontal green line (color 3), spanning the whole output width.
fbf15a0b: The color-5 cells act as corner markers telling you which half of the picture to discard. If the 5s lie in the same row, the grid is cut vertically into left/right halves; if they lie in the same column, it is cut horizontally into top/bottom halves. Keep the half opposite the marked corner, and then change any remaining 5s to background color 8.
fc10701f:
The grid contains a 9-object and a 7-object, aligned either in the same rows or in the same columns. The 9-object is moved onto the 7-object’s color role: all 9 cells become 7, and the original 7 cells are erased to background 6.

Then the pattern of 0-cells is projected into the strip between the two objects: if the 9 and 7 share rows, keep the 9’s columns and place 2s in those columns at every 0-column lying strictly between the two objects; if they share columns, keep the 9’s rows and place 2s in those rows at every 0-row lying strictly between them. In short, the 0-pattern is transferred across the gap onto the 9-object’s row/column span, using color 2.
fc4aaf52: The input contains one connected non-background figure made of exactly two colors. Split this figure into its upper half and lower half across its horizontal midpoint, then slide the upper half to the right so it sits immediately to the right of the lower half, while keeping the lower half in place. Finally, swap the two figure colors in the result.
fc754716: The output keeps the same size as the input but replaces the contents with a hollow rectangular frame around the grid’s outer edge. The frame is colored with the input’s non-background color (the lone colored cell), while every interior cell is set to 0.
fcb5c309: Find the large hollow rectangular frame: it is the only non-background object that is a perfect box and is at least 3 cells tall and wide. Crop out that framed rectangle, then recolor the frame itself to the other nonzero color in the grid (the color of the scattered dots), while leaving the interior pattern of dots and blanks unchanged. The output is this cropped rectangle with its border color swapped to the dot color.
fcc82909: Each non-black object is a 2×2 colored block. For each such block, count how many distinct colors it contains, then attach a green (color 3) rectangle directly underneath it, aligned to the same two columns. The added green rectangle has width 2 and height equal to that distinct-color count, while the original colored blocks remain unchanged.
fd02da9e: The input is an 8×8 field of 7s with possible special colored markers in the four corners. Each corner marker is removed from the corner and reproduced as a fixed small shape placed inward from that same corner: top-left and top-right corners become 2×2 blocks near the top, while bottom-left and bottom-right corners become 3-cell bent/diagonal shapes near the bottom-middle. The copied shape keeps the corner marker’s color, and if multiple corners are marked, all corresponding inward shapes are drawn on the otherwise 7-filled grid.
fd096ab6: The complete shape in color 4 is the template. Every other non-background colored pattern is an incomplete copy of that same shape, and the output completes each one by adding cells of its own color so it exactly matches the color-4 shape in the same orientation and placement-relative arrangement. The template shape itself is left unchanged, and the background stays the same.
fd4b2b02: The input contains a single solid rectangular bar of one color (either 3 or 6). Keep that bar, then grow a symmetric pattern outward from each of its four diagonal directions by repeatedly placing copies of the same-sized bar one step diagonally away, alternating both color and orientation each time: color 3 bars are horizontal, color 6 bars are vertical. Continue this corner-to-corner zigzag replication until the next bar would go out of bounds, producing an overall X-like pattern of alternating horizontal green and vertical magenta bars.
fe45cba4: The grid has one background color and exactly two other colors. One non-background color appears as a single connected blob; the other appears as two disconnected pieces, one of which overlaps the blob’s columns. The transformation deletes all cells of the two-piece color and replaces them with a solid rectangle of that color, using the same height as the single blob and the same total area as the original two-piece color, positioned on the opposite side of the single blob so the two colored rectangles line up horizontally/vertically as in the examples.
fe9372f3: Find the red 5-cell plus and keep it unchanged. Add blue (1) cells at the four diagonal neighbors of its center, then extend those four diagonals all the way to the grid edges with blue, forming an X. Also extend the plus’s horizontal and vertical arms outward to the borders, coloring those rays with a repeating pattern of 8, 8, 4 as distance from the plus increases; all other cells stay black.
fea12743:
The grid consists of six 4×4 symbol panels arranged as 3 rows × 2 columns, separated by blank lines/columns. One panel is the “odd one out” because its number of colored cells differs from the majority; that entire panel is recolored to green (3).

Then the panel(s) that match this odd panel’s shape by vertical reflection are highlighted in blue (8): if its mirror appears as the other panel in the same row, both mirrored copies are colored blue; otherwise, the copy in the same left/right column position of the corresponding matching row is colored blue, and in some cases one additional mirrored counterpart is also colored blue. All other panels remain unchanged.
feca6190:
The input is a single 5-cell row. Count its nonzero cells; if there are \(k\), make a \(5k \times 5k\) black square output.

Then copy the entire 5-cell input row repeatedly along an up-right diagonal: place it on the bottom row starting at the left edge, and on each row above shift that same 5-cell pattern one column to the right. As a result, each nonzero input cell becomes a diagonal line of its color running from lower-left to upper-right, while zeros remain background.
ff2825db: The top row is a fixed header and is left unchanged. In the 9×10 area below it, ignore the background (0) and the original border color; among the remaining colored cells, find the color that appears most often. The output replaces the entire lower area with zeros except for two things drawn in that dominant color: the full outer border of the lower area, and the rectangular outline of the dominant color’s own bounding box from the input.
ff28f65a: The input contains several separate 2×2 blocks of color 2 on a black background. Count how many such 2×2 blocks there are, then produce a fixed 3×3 output with that many 1s placed in a preset X-shaped order: top-left, top-right, center, bottom-left, bottom-right. All other output cells remain 0.
ff72ca3e: Each yellow cell (4) is treated as the center of a filled red square (2). For each such center, find the nearest gray cell (5) using chessboard/Chebyshev distance, and grow the square uniformly in all directions until it stops one cell short of that gray cell. The yellow center and all gray cells remain unchanged; only the surrounding cells inside that maximal square are painted red.
ff805c23: The input contains one color whose full pattern is perfectly symmetric under all four reflections/diagonal mirrors; part of that pattern has been overwritten by color 1. The task is to identify that fully 4-way-symmetric color pattern, choose the symmetric color with the smallest total area if several qualify, and reconstruct the hidden cells covered by the 1s. The output is the minimal bounding box of the 1-region, filled with the recovered underlying pattern using only that chosen color and background 0.
